The paper details the comparative analysis of point distribution methods, presents experimental results demonstrating the superiority of the Fibonacci spiral, and validates the improved metric on test 3D models, showing increased accuracy and stability.

title: Energy-Efficient Machine Learning Models for Real-Time Anomaly Detection in IoT Sensor Networks
authors: K Sathish
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Smart Electronics and Communication (ICOSEC)
abstract: ML models used to detect anomalies in IoT sensor networks in real-time require more energy-efficient methods as such networks have seen full deployment and reduced power availability. In this study, a combination of methods of more sophisticated pre-processing combined with a Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) model and optimized using Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) was also proposed. An evaluation was done based on the popular and commonly known Numenta Anomaly Benchmark (NAB) dataset. Findings show that likelihood of accuracy, F1-score, recall and precision of the GRU+PSO model was 92%, 0.93, 0.92 and 0.94 respectively compared to the conventional models such as Random Forest (84%), K-Means (83%) that tend to perform poorly. The suggested framework was also highly energy-efficient with no more than 30% of the reduced energy consumption and 35 percent less data redundancy when compared to conventional approaches. This means that the optimized deep learning method is scalable and feasible to use to increase the level of IoT security and reliability and reduce resource utilization. Finally, the study provides strong and interpretable large-scale real-time anomaly detection in IoT sensor networks.

title: Explainable Artificial Intelligence in Robotics: A Review of Applications and Methods
authors: Gulsum Alicioglu, Bo Sun
publish: 2025 International Conference on Machine Learning and Applications (ICMLA)
abstract: Since many robots are trained by black-box artificial intelligence (AI) models, their decision-making process is uninterpretable by end-users. Therefore, explainable AI (XAI) methods are crucial in making these systems transparent, trustworthy, and understandable for acceptance. This paper reviews the recent XAI methods and applications in the field of robotics to highlight trends, limitations, and challenges. We review the selected papers across four main robotic tasks: control, navigation, manipulation, and interaction. We discuss XAI applications in terms of explanation granularity, task-decision complexity, and deployment settings. We reveal that most of the robotics research relies on virtual environments with navigation and manipulation tasks. Our findings indicate that the current XAI applications for the reasoning of robotics systems are still emerging and need user-centric multimodal explanations to enhance and repair trust, especially in failure cases.

title: A Machine Learning-Based Traffic Prediction Framework for Smart Cities
authors: Chetan Ghadi, Akanksha Hake, Sara Khare, Harshil Kanakia, Renuka Pawar
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
abstract: The rapid expansion of urban transportation systems has intensified the need for intelligent traffic prediction models that can operate reliably under dynamic real-world conditions. Traditional rules-based approaches often fail to account for fluctuating factors, such as weather variations, public events, infrastructure constraints, and transit disruptions, resulting in limited forecast accuracy and delayed decision-making. This research presents a machine learning–based traffic prediction framework that leverages historical traffic weather data along with real-time environmental inputs obtained from the OpenWeather API. A Random Forest regression model is trained to estimate vehicle volume and is improved through contextual modifiers, including road construction, transit outages, and event-driven surges. The system further evaluates congestion severity, estimates effective road capacity, and generates risk assessments to support proactive decision-making. Additionally, it provides actionable recommendations for traffic authorities and police departments through automated alerts and deployment strategies. The proposed framework is modular, scalable, and designed for real-time operational use, contributing towards safer, smarter, and more sustainable traffic management in modern urban environments.

title: A New Federated Learning Approach for Imbalanced Medical Image Datasets
authors: Aniruddha Tiwari, Zhen Ni, Xiangnan Zhong, Ahmed Imteaj
publish: 2025 International Conference on Machine Learning and Applications (ICMLA)
abstract: Medical image classification plays a vital role in disease diagnosis, but often suffers from class imbalance and privacy concerns, particularly in rare disease categories. Such an imbalance can bias machine learning models toward majority classes, reducing the diagnostic accuracy for minority conditions across geographically diverse populations. To address these challenges, we propose integrating the synthetic minority oversampling technique (SMOTE) into the federated learning (FL) framework for 2D medical image analysis, enabling privacy-preserving and balanced learning. The SMOTE technique enhances neural networks’ generalization by generating informative synthetic samples, outperforming raw duplication, and reducing overfitting. In our approach, by operating on features obtained from deep models like VGG19, we avoid pixel-level interpolation artifacts and retain critical semantic information. The SMOTE technique is applied to the extracted features at the local client level, where it generates new samples for the minority class by interpolating between existing samples to achieve the local data balance. The proposed approach allows each client to balance its local data without compromising privacy, promoting fair learning across all classes in disease classification tasks. We evaluate our approach on two publicly available chest X-ray datasets for binary and multi-class classifications. Our method achieves 83% accuracy and 97% recall in binary classification and 50% accuracy in multiclass settings, highlighting its potential to contribute to diagnostic fairness and robustness in healthcare applications.

title: Physics-Informed Smart Maintenance for Offshore Wind Power: Prospects and Research Directions*
authors: Xinxing Wang, Jiahao Chen, Qianwei Zhang, Jiewu Leng
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Neural Networks, Information and Communication Engineering (NNICE)
abstract: The transition of offshore wind to deep-water environments requires advanced O&M strategies to manage coupled loads. Harsh marine conditions and data scarcity limit current reliability management. While TinyML supports monitoring, traditional models still face latency and interpretability challenges. This paper reviews Physics-Informed Smart Maintenance and proposes four future research prospects: edge-native intelligence for real-time simulation, physics-consistent data fusion, generative intelligence to address data scarcity, and cognitive decision-making enabled by Large Language Models (LLMs). These prospects support a resilient, autonomous ecosystem for next-generation wind turbines.

title: A Generalizable Energy Management System for Quantified Thermal Virtual Energy Storage Control
authors: Peidong Li, Yue Cao
abstract: The thermal mass of a building offers a significant resource for virtual energy storage (VES). However, its grid integration is hindered by the lack of generalizable modeling and grid-compatible control metrics. This paper proposes a comprehensive energy management framework to address these limitations. The framework introduces a generalizable, adaptive gray-box model for building thermal mass using equivalent thermal parameters (ETP) which employs an unscented Kalman filter (UKF) for real-time parameter identification. Based on this model, a novel electrical state-of-charge (SOC) formulation is developed to directly translate the thermodynamic state of a building into an electrical energy metric. Ultimately, this framework enables buildings to be modeled, quantified, and co-optimized as dispatchable assets within any standard energy management system (EMS), thus turning the global building stock into a valuable grid resource.

title: Interpretable Transfer Learning System for Multi-Class Lung Cancer Identification Using Enhanced CT Imaging
authors: Savitha N J, Uday Raj, Sathvik T Soged, Sandeep Jadhav
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
abstract: Lung cancer continues to be the leading cause of cancer mortality worldwide, making early identification crucial for improving patient survival. We introduce a transparent deep learning system for categorizing lung cancer into multiple classes using CT scan imagery. Our approach employs transfer learning with ResNet-50 as its foundation, strengthened through extensive data enhancement and Grad-CAM visualization for improved model transparency. The system utilizes the publicly accessible IQ-OTHNCCD dataset containing three distinct categories: benign conditions, malignant tumors, and healthy lung scans. Our methodology achieves 92.8% validation accuracy, with precision at 91.5%, recall of 90.9%, and F1-score reaching <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 1. 2 \%}$</tex>, demonstrating superior results compared to standard models. The inclusion of explainable AI methods offers clinical transparency, positioning this approach as suitable for computerassisted diagnosis in pulmonary radiology.

title: Active Authentication via Korean Keystrokes Under Varying LLM Assistance and Cognitive Contexts
authors: Dong Hyun Roh, Rajesh Kumar
publish: 2025 International Conference on Machine Learning and Applications (ICMLA)
abstract: Keystroke dynamics is a promising modality for active user authentication, but its effectiveness under varying LLM-assisted typing and cognitive conditions remains understudied. Using data from 50 users and cognitive labels from Bloom’s Taxonomy, we evaluate keystroke-based authentication in Korean across three realistic typing scenarios: bona fide composition, LLM content paraphrasing, and transcription. Our pipeline incorporates continuity-aware segmentation, feature extraction, and classification via SVM, MLP, and XGB. Results show that the system maintains reliable performance across varying LLM usages and cognitive contexts, with Equal Error Rates ranging from 5.1% to 10.4%. These findings demonstrate the feasibility of behavioral authentication under modern writing conditions and offer insights into designing more context-resilient models.

title: A Dual-Purpose Interpretable Ensemble Framework of Fraud Stratification and Credit Risk
authors: Sujitha S, Suresh Kumar G, Elambarathi E, R Mohan Ram Kumar, Nithish Raj K
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
abstract: Modern financial institutions are under greater pressure to not only identify a fraudulent action but also to generate transparent credit evaluation, and one in which the regulation is not compromised. Modern pipelines generally approach these as discrete tasks which results in disjointed attribute mapping and black box behaviours not meeting the regulatory requirement of traceability. In this paper, the authors propose a single framework (called Dual-Objective Interpretable Ensemble) that simultaneously fraud stratifies and scores credit using a single interpretable framework. DOIE combines a diversity-optimal ensemble backbone with a hierarchical say layer, resorting to monotonicity based constrained learners, trajectory union between local and global attribution, as well as sample boundary singleness characterization. The outputs of the system are synchronized: a categorical risk of fraud-risk and a continuous credit rating and a report to the regulator consolidating rule-level evidence. The methodology has the benefit of providing every judgment with an equivalent and evidenced analytical rationale. Experimental analysis of multimodal financial data shows that DOIE is able to reach an F1-score of 0.962 with an association rate of 0.011 on fraud detection, as well as an AUC of 0.947 on credit default prediction. Besides, the framework retains over 0.91 accountability scores in all elements. These findings confirm that DOIE is a powerful framework that can reconcile high-performance predictive analytics with the demands of financial compliance that are highly interpretable.

title: Impact of AI Integration on Downsizing Intentions in Entrepreneurial Enterprises
authors: Smita Deshpande, Vimal Bhatt
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
abstract: The current article seeks to explore the relationship between: 1) entrepreneurial digital literacy / learning (EDL), 2) usage of AI (UAI), 3) entrepreneurial mindset (EM), 4) entrepreneurial strategic intent (ESI), 5) competitive psychology (CP) and 6) entrepreneurial attitude (EA). The impact of these constructs on downsizing intention (DSI) is studied. DSI is a concept that is frequently used when there are large layoffs of employees in an organisation. While analysing the literature, it was found that there exists a research gap in exploring the impact of DSI in AI-driven enterprises. This relationship established among the six independent variables and the dependent variable is well-knitted with the help of two intents of the theory of planned behaviour (TPB). To conduct this empirical study, a questionnaire was sent to those entrepreneurs who have adopted AI techniques in their enterprises. The data collected was cleaned and a regression analysis was run in Excel. The results revealed that there is significant variance in EA as explained by the five independent variables (EDL, UAI, EM, ESI and CP). Further, the second regression analysis revealed that there is significant variance in DSI too as explained by two variables (EA and SN). The study can be used to frame a favourable yet knowledge-provoking syllabus for the new generation entrepreneurs, and help the AIenabled entrepreneurs to brainstorm remedial ideas to reduce the downsizing intentions in their respective enterprises. This study is an original work to add value to the new generation and existing entrepreneurs who have adopted AI technology in either way.

title: SmartEduNotes: A Retrieval-Augmented Platform for Personalized Note-Taking, Revision, and Career Guidance
authors: Vernika Singh, Tarun Kumar Pradhan, Shreya SS, Kashish Upadhayay, Shah Suraj Kumar
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
abstract: Effective learning in modern education requires tools that not only store information but actively help learners retain knowledge and plan future careers. We present SmartEduNotes, a web-based, retrieval-augmented platform that enhances personal note-taking through dense semantic search, provenance-aware question answering, automated flashcard generation, and concept-graph-driven career guidance. The system integrates Sentence-BERT embeddings, FAISS vector search, and LLM-based generation to provide accurate, context-grounded answers from personal notes, while automatically converting content into cloze-style flashcards scheduled with our novel Adaptive SM-2 algorithm for optimized spaced repetition. Central concepts extracted via a concept graph inform personalized skill recommendations based on real-world job postings. Pilot evaluation on 150 lecture note documents demonstrates up to 87 % QA accuracy, 85% 7-day flashcard recall (compared to 62 % manually), and effective identification of career-relevant skills. The architecture supports modular expansion, multi-modal input (audio, text), on-device privacy, and adaptive learning strategies. This work contributes a reproducible framework for personalized learning, knowledge retention, and career-aligned skill development.

title: DefenSys: An Integrated Platform for Malware Detection and Containerized Attack Simulation using Deep Learning
authors: Elvis Vincent, Prabu Jayant, Mohana, Abhilash Chakkan
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Smart Electronics and Communication (ICOSEC)
abstract: The increased growth of cyber threats, with attacks happening every 11 seconds and global losses exceeding $6 trillion annually, creates a lot of challenges for enterprise security. Standard cybersecurity solutions remain costly and very complex for small enterprises, creating substantial security gaps. This paper presents DefenSys, a comprehensive cyber defense platform integrating intelligent malware detection with dynamic network attack simulation to help increase cybersecurity across organizations of all scales. DefenSys employs a containerized Docker architecture for attack simulation environments, enabling secure execution of real-world cyberattacks including DDoS attacks using Apache Benchmarking tools. The malware detection service uses deep learning techniques using Residual Neural Networks and Convolutional Neural Networks for binary classification. Malware files undergo binary-to-grayscale image conversion, achieving 98.7% binary classification accuracy and 76% multi-class classification accuracy across 25 malware categories including worms, trojans, ransomware, and botnets, with macro-averaged F1-score of 0.76 and weighted F1-score of 0.71. The system architecture incorporates Flask-based web services with Apache HTTP Server for maintaining reduced deployment overhead. Experimental testing demonstrates DefenSys's performance in real life threat simulation and detection with reduced computational resource utilization while also maintaining high accuracy and faster response capabilities through the dual stage classification approach. DefenSys addresses the cybersecurity gap by providing business level protection through a cost effective and intuitive platform that reduces technical barriers for small organizations with little to no cybersecurity expertise.

title: PEPS: Quantum-Inspired Reinforcement Learning for Coherent Reasoning Traces in LLMs
authors: Venkat Margapuri, Garik Kazanjian, Naren Kosaraju
publish: 2025 International Conference on Machine Learning and Applications (ICMLA)
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) often struggle with maintaining coherent multi-step reasoning traces, particularly in tasks that require a structured logical flow. This work introduces a quantum-inspired approach to address the challenge by incorporating a fidelity-based reward derived from Projected Entangled Pair States (PEPS) into Proximal Policy Optimization. Unlike prior approaches that use direct supervision or contrastive objectives, the proposed method guides learning through structural consistency, offering a novel approach to enforce global coherence in generated reasoning traces. The proposed framework is evaluated using multiple coherence-determining metrics on diverse datasets such as GSM8K, StrategyQA, and EntailmentBank spanning arithmetic, intuitive, and entailment-based reasoning. Results show that the proposed quantum-inspired approach offers significant improvements over supervised, contrastive, and pretrained baseline approaches, highlighting the effectiveness of quantum-inspired fidelity as a foundation to improve reasoning trace coherence in LLMs.

title: Graph Neural Networks for Predicting Urban Traffic Congestion in Smart Cities
authors: B.Mahalakshmi, P N Shiammala, Gowtham M, R.Bagavathi Lakshmi, A.Angel Cerli, S. Sudha
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Smart Electronics and Communication (ICOSEC)
abstract: Urban traffic congestion is a serious issue for all smart cities in terms of sustainability and efficiency. Existing traffic prediction models do not properly account for the spatial and temporal dependencies in roads within the urban road network. This paper proposes a new methodology for modeling urban traffic congestion using a graph neural network (GNN) to design the road network as a defined dynamic graph, with the addition of multi-view traffic data (i.e. speed, volume, and occupancy), temporal sequences, and external contextual factors such as weather conditions and public events. Enhanced GNN architecture is utilized with attention and hierarchical graph pooling to learn local and universal traffic patterns. The model is validated and evaluated on a dataset collected over six months for a medium smart city, alongside three baseline models (LSTM, Xgboost and Static GCN). The proposed GNN model outperformed the three baseline models RMSE is 3.42 km/h MAE is 2.78, and -2 R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> score 0.915 with a F1 score of 0.89 in congested hours classification. The results of the proposed methodology confirm the suitability of spatial-temporal graph learning approaches for traffic forecasting methods. To conclude, this methodology demonstrates a strong scalable approach to computing predictive traffic models as part of critical smart city infrastructure.

title: Spatio-Temporal Registration Method for Sea Surface Infrared and X-Band Radar Image
authors: Qun Yang, Haipeng Yu, Xiaoliang Chu, Dabo Guo, Guang Yuan
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Neural Networks, Information and Communication Engineering (NNICE)
abstract: Radar systems and infrared cameras are commonly employed to observe nearshore ocean surface phenomena. Owing to their distinct viewing perspectives and spatial resolutions, the images acquired by these two sensors are typically analyzed independently. However, joint observations can provide more comprehensive information about sea-surface dynamics. In this study, radar–infrared image registration is achieved through combined spatial and temporal alignment. Radar data are transformed into images from the infrared camera perspective and associated with infrared imagery to accomplish spatial alignment, while temporal alignment is performed using timestamps recorded in the data files. The effectiveness of the proposed approach is validated using field data collected in a nearshore environment.

title: Research for Garbage Classification and Detection Based on HWM-YOLO
authors: Xu Gao, Chen Dong, Hanyang He, Xiaozhuo Bi, Huiyanhua Dai
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Neural Networks, Information and Communication Engineering (NNICE)
abstract: For four common types of garbage, namely metal, cardboard, glass, and plastic, existing garbage classification and detection models had suffered from garbage missed detections in complex scenes, insufficient bounding box regression accuracy, and poor adaptability. To address these challenges, this paper proposed an HWM-YOLO garbage classification and detection model. Built upon YOLOv11, the proposed model replaced the original PANet-style neck with a dynamically cross-scale fused Hyper Neck module, which effectively reduced garbage missed detections of small-scale and occluded garbage objects in complex environments. Moreover, a WIoUv3 loss function incorporating a dynamic non-monotonic focusing mechanism was introduced to handle stacked and irregularly shaped garbage, which suppressed gradient anomalies caused by low-quality samples during bounding box regression and significantly improved regression accuracy in complex scenes. In addition, a Mixed Aggregation Network (MANet) that integrates three convolutional variants was employed to enhance the representation capability for garbage objects with diverse shapes, thereby improving the model’s adaptability to complex scenarios. Experimental results on a public dataset demonstrated that the proposed model had achieved a precision of 90.8%, a recall of 87.3%, an mAP50 of 91.7%, and an mAP50–95 of 76.6%, providing a robust solution for garbage classification and detection in complex scenes.

title: Generation of Programmatic Rules for Document Forgery Detection Using Large Language Models
authors: Valentin Schmidberger, Manuel Eberhardinger, Setareh Maghsudi, Johannes Maucher
publish: 2025 International Conference on Machine Learning and Applications (ICMLA)
abstract: Document forgery poses a growing threat to legal, economic, and governmental processes, requiring increasingly sophisticated verification mechanisms. One approach involves the use of plausibility checks, rule-based procedures that assess the correctness and internal consistency of data, to detect anomalies or signs of manipulation. Although these verification procedures are essential for ensuring data integrity, existing plausibility checks are manually implemented by software engineers, which is time-consuming. Recent advances in code generation with large language models (LLMs) offer new potential for automating and scaling the generation of these checks. However, adapting LLMs to the specific requirements of an unknown domain remains a significant challenge. This work investigates the extent to which LLMs, adapted on domain-specific code and data through different fine-tuning strategies, can generate rule-based plausibility checks for forgery detection on constrained hardware resources. We fine-tune open-source LLMs, Llama 3.1 8B and OpenCoder 8B, on structured datasets derived from real-world application scenarios and evaluate the generated plausibility checks on previously unseen forgery patterns. The results demonstrate that the models are capable of generating executable and effective verification procedures. This also highlights the potential of LLMs as scalable tools to support human decision-making in security-sensitive contexts where comprehensibility is required.

title: Fine-Grained Monocular Depth Estimation Based on Multi-Scale Recursive Laplacian Fusion Network
authors: Yanhui Xi, Zhen Ding, Wenxin Zhu, Lanlan Liu
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Neural Networks, Information and Communication Engineering (NNICE)
abstract: Monocular depth estimation is crucial for 3D scene perception, with applications in autonomous driving and robot navigation. Traditional methods often struggle with object boundary delineation and scene layout complexity. This paper introduces a novel Multi-Scale Laplacian-ConvGRU Fusion Network (MSLCF-Net) to address these challenges. By integrating a ResNeXt101 encoder with a Multi-Scale Interpolation and Cross-Layer Aggregation (MICRA) module, and a decoder incorporating a Laplacian-based Convolutional Gated Recurrent Unit (LAP-CGRU), MSLCF-Net enhances feature representation and long-range dependency capture. Experiments on KITTI datasets demonstrate superior performance, with a 5.2% improvement in Abs Rel and a 2.3% reduction in RMSE compared to state-of-the-art models. Our approach offers a significant advancement in monocular depth estimation, providing finer structural details and accurate depth boundaries.

title: A Compact Frequency-Reconfigurable Monopole Antenna Using PIN-Diode-Controlled Parasitic Stubs for IoT Applications
authors: Anvesh Pandey, Arun Kumar Saurabh
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
abstract: This paper presents a frequency-reconfigurable compact monopole antenna designed for Internet of Things (IoT) and next-generation communication systems. The antenna is exceptionally low-profile <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(0.01 \lambda \mathrm{g})$</tex>, with a compact footprint of 16 <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\times 20 \text{mm}^{2}\left(0.21 \lambda_{\mathrm{g}} \times 0.26 \lambda_{\mathrm{g}}\right)$</tex>, where <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\lambda_{\mathrm{g}}$</tex> corresponds to the guided wavelength at 3.28 GHz. The antenna integrates a PIN diode that connects a semi-circular monopole radiator to precisely tuned arc-shaped parasitic stubs, enabling multiple resonant modes and wide multi-band operation through an integrated biasing network. Full-wave simulations validate a reflection coefficient <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\left\vert\mathbf{S}_{\mathbf{1 1}}\right\vert)$</tex> below −10 dB across multiple frequency bands: 3.18-3.37 GHz, 5.65-5.90 GHz, 6.387-7.78 GHz, and 8.88-11.48 GHz. The antenna maintains stable omnidirectional radiation characteristics and achieves a total radiation efficiency of up to 87.4% across its operating frequency range. The compact form factor, frequency reconfigurability, and stable radiation performance of the proposed antenna enables its use in IoT, 5G NR, and emerging wireless applications.

title: Research on an Improved Global Correlation-Track Association Algorithm
authors: Xiangfeng Liu, Shijie Sun, Runsheng Wang, Xiaojun Li, Feng Luo, Jian Wang
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Neural Networks, Information and Communication Engineering (NNICE)
abstract: Space measurement system requires real-time tracking, identification, and processing of multiple space targets, and the primary challenge to address is the multi-target association problem. Compared with other association algorithms, the global correlation-track association algorithm offers advantages such as flexible application, strong adaptability, and high association accuracy. However, due to the inherent characteristics of the algorithm, its computational complexity increases geometrically as the number of tracked targets grows. This paper designs a global correlation-track association algorithm based on "association clusters", which can solve the track association problem of dense multi-targets in real-time space measurement system under complex conditions.

title: Intelligent Hybrid Deep Learning System for Automated Retinal Disease Diagnosis
authors: D. Chandra Prakash, Yerram Sneha, Nayani Sateesh, Bathula Balnarsaiah, Ashok Kumar Vootla, Kokkula Shiva Prasad
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
abstract: Diabetic Retinopathy (DR) is a main cause of acquired blindness and a leading cause of preventable blindness in the world. Individual detection of DR on the embodiment of a retina is critical to timely intervention and save sight. This proposed project will utilize a Hybrid Deep Learning Model (resnet50 plus vision transformer (ViT)) with the aim of discriminating Diabetic Retinopathy (DR) and Non-Diabetic (Normal) cases. The ResNet50 element is efficient in obtaining local retinal specific characteristics including microaneurysms and hemorrhages, whereas the ViT element assesses overall structural details of the retina which allows full image insights. The combination of these two feature extractors promotes the discriminatory capability and strength of the model. The proposed model performed at 99.49% overall classification accuracy using the given dataset of more than 500 well-crafted images per class with the best precision, recall, and F1-score. The system is convenient to the clinical screening and tele-ophthalmology uses because of a Flask-based web interface that permits real-time image upload and instant disease detection.

title: Dynamic Sign Language Identification Using Mediapipe Landmarks and Random Forest Classifier
authors: Shrivathsa TV, Amritha UR, Panchami Pai, Vishrutha, Ananya Amin
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
abstract: Communication is an important tool for effective information exchange over people and it ensures messages are clear and as intended, which fosters relationships. Communication between hearing and non-hearing individuals remains limited due to the lack of efficient a utomated sign language recognition systems. The paper outlines a machine learning framework for real-time gesture and sign language detection based on custom dataset. The dataset used includes all 26 letters of the American Sign Language alphabet, each with around 300 samples for training, testing, and validation of the classifiers. <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$O$</tex> penCV and MediaPipe were used for annotation and accurate landmark feature extraction from the frames captured through the camera interface. These features are then used by a Random Forest Classifier to detect the gestures correctly. The combined approach of random forest along with the MediaPipe model provides a promising precision in the identification o fg estures and, in combination with OpenCV provides robustness in dynamic identification o ver a set of variations in gestures and conditions.

title: Proposal of Modular Multi-Active Bridge Converter Analysis as an Interconnected System
authors: Silvana Gamboa, Leonardo Ortega
abstract: This document presents a proposal for analyzing the Modular Multiactive Bridge Systems under the interconnected system theory. This study verified the feasibility of the proposal, as the matrices describing the relationships between some of the variables in these systems met the requirements to be considered Laplacian matrices. It also verified the possibility of establishing consensus algorithms in MMAB systems, taking advantage of their interconnected nature. Finally, the proposed approaches were validated through simulation on a 5-port system for a typical MMAB operating scenario and their results demonstrated the validity of the proposal.

title: Digital Twin-Based Vehicle to Grid Coordination for Voltage Regulation and Peak Shaving in Unbalanced Distribution Networks
authors: Mohammed A. Mousa
abstract: The rapid growth of electric vehicles (EVs) presents both challenges and opportunities for distribution networks. This paper proposes a digital twin (DT)-based vehicle to grid (V2G) coordination framework for simultaneous voltage regulation and peak demand management in unbalanced three phase distribution networks. The framework integrates a real time digital twin model of the IEEE 13-bus test feeder, synchronized at 15 minute intervals, with a multi-objective optimization algorithm implemented in MATLAB/Simulink. Four case studies are conducted over a 24 hour simulation horizon: baseline uncoordinated charging, V2G for voltage regulation, V2G for peak shaving, and combined DT-V2G optimization. Results demonstrate that the proposed approach reduces voltage violations by 90.6%, decreases peak demand by 13.7% (470 kW), improves the network load factor by 16.1%, and reduces power losses by 31.0%. The MATLAB/Simulink implementation provides a validated simulation platform for evaluating coordinated V2G optimization strategies in distribution networks with high EV penetration.

title: End-to-End Behavioral Cloning with Throttle and Speed Fusion: A Multi-Modal Approach Using Udacity Jungle and Lake Tracks
authors: Barshan Mondal, Kartikey Singh, Krishnan V Namboothiri, K. Namitha
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
abstract: This paper presents an autonomous steering vision based behavioral cloning architecture, which is developed based on data gathered on the Udacity Jungle as well as Lake simulator track. It is an extension of a traditional image-only regression architecture that it uses throttle and speed as auxiliary inputs to an already trained ResNet-18 backbone, thus facilitating the context-based inference of steering. To reduce the severe zero-steering effect, we push the zero-steering effect, which is apparent in 61.8% of the samples, to recalculate selective datacuration strategy 5% of straight-driving matches are simply kept, other straight steering matches are artificially extended by using singular flipping. The result of this procedure is a balanced set of training that contains 5,831 recordings. After 25 training cycles using the Adam optimizer (learning rate= 1e-4), the model achieves a validation mean-squared error equal to 0.0579 and has attained steady convergence. These held out frame real time inferences give a steering estimate that is accurate on held out frames as indicated through an example left turn of a deviation of -6.08 degrees. The findings are relevant to stress that modular sensory-related cues and data-knowledgeable preprocessing play a vital role in increasing the resilience of behavioral cloning frameworks.

title: Implementation of Smart Doorbell for Hearing Impaired Using FPGA, ModelSim and MATLAB
authors: Chejarla Venkata Surya Sai Anish, Rajesh Kannan Megalingam
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
abstract: Hearing-impaired individuals often encounter significant difficulties responding to conventional audio doorbell notifications, which may result in missed visitors and a higher security risk. Current solutions either are not reliable or do not support multi-modal feedback for varying environmental conditions. To counter these challenges, we propose a smart doorbell system particularly for the hearing-impaired individuals. The system incorporates a push-button interface, a knock sensor, and a microphone sensor to identify visitor presence by physical contact or voice. A camera module offers real-time video surveillance of the entrance zone, and an LDR controls LED brightness in relation to ambient lighting to maintain visibility. A vibration motor offers haptic feedback, and visual signals are presented by adaptive LEDs, which are controlled by LDR. An FPGA acts as the core control unit, allowing for effective realtime processing. For added reliability, FIR filtering is used to remove environmental noise from the knock and microphone sensors so that they trigger exactly, mitigating fake alerts. The system provides double feedback-visual and tactile, assuring the users are immediately alerted to a visitor's presence. Future development will concentrate on integrating image recognition techniques with mobile app development to alert the user about the visitors using the live feed from the camera module.

title: Wireless Sensing with Movable Intelligent Surface
authors: Ziyuan Zheng, Qingqing Wu, Yanze Zhu, Wen Chen, Ying Gao, Honghao Wang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Signal Processing
abstract: Future wireless networks are envisioned to deliver not only gigabit communications but also ubiquitous sensing. Reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RISs) have emerged to reshape radio propagation, recently showing considerable promise for wireless sensing. Still, their per-element electronic tuning incurs prohibitive hardware cost and power consumption. Motivated by the concept of fluid antenna systems (FAS), this paper introduces a low-cost movable intelligent surface (MIS) for wireless sensing, which replaces element-wise electronic phase tuning with panel-wise mechanical reconfiguration. The MIS stacks two pre-phased metasurface (MS) layers: a large fixed layer and a smaller movable layer, whose differential position shifts synthesize distinct composite phase patterns, enabling multiple beam patterns for multi-target detection. We characterize a MIS-enabled multi-hop echo signal model with multi-target interference and then formulate a worst-case sensing signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR) maximization problem that jointly designs MIS phase shifts and schedules MS2's position. A Riemannian Augmented Lagrangian Method (RALM)-based algorithm is developed to solve the formulated mixed-integer non-convex problem. We further extend the MIS sensing scheme to a peak-to-side-lobe-ratio (PSLR)-oriented design with the same algorithm framework. We also derive a heuristic MIS beam-steering design with a closed-form phase distribution and position scheduling. Simulations validate MIS's beam pattern reconfiguration capability, show that the RALM-based scheme significantly outperforms the closed-form scheme in improving sensing SINR, and uncover a gain-diversity trade-off in beam patterns that informs the optimal choice of MIS configuration.

title: Multimodal Feedback for Handheld Tool Guidance: Combining Wrist-Based Haptics with Augmented Reality
authors: Yue Yang, Christoph Leuze, Brian Hargreaves, Bruce Daniel, Fred Baik
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: We investigate how vibrotactile wrist feedback can enhance spatial guidance for handheld tool movement in optical see-through augmented reality (AR). While AR overlays are widely used to support surgical tasks, visual occlusion, lighting conditions, and interface ambiguity can compromise precision and confidence. To address these challenges, we designed a multimodal system combining AR visuals with a custom wrist-worn haptic device delivering directional and state-based cues. A formative study with experienced surgeons and residents identified key tool maneuvers and preferences for reference mappings, guiding our cue design. In a cue identification experiment (N = 21), participants accurately recognized five vibration patterns under visual load, with higher recognition for full-actuator states than spatial direction cues. In a guidance task (N = 27), participants using both AR and haptics achieved significantly higher spatial precision (5.8 mm) and usability (SUS = 88.1) than those using either modality alone, albeit with modest increases in task time. Participants reported that haptic cues provided reassuring confirmation and reduced cognitive effort during alignment. Our results highlight the promise of integrating wrist-based haptics into AR systems for high-precision, visually complex tasks such as surgical guidance. We discuss design implications for multimodal interfaces supporting confident, efficient tool manipulation.

title: Calibration of the Spaceborne GNSS-R Specular Delay Tracking on Fengyun-3 Missions and Its Impact on Ocean Wind Retrieval
authors: Feixiong Huang, Junming Xia, Wenqiang Lu, Cong Yin, Dongwei Wang, Cheng Liu, Xiaochun Zhai, Xianyi Wang, Yusen Tian, Qifei Du, Yueqiang Sun, Weihua Bai, Na Xu, Xiuqing Hu, Jian Shang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Calibration is one of the foundation of spaceborne Global Navigation Satellite System Reflectometry (GNSS-R) geo-physical parameter retrievals. This paper highlights that, besides of power calibration, accurate time delay calibration is also crucial to GNSS-R ocean wind retrievals as the computation of normalized bistatic radar cross section (NBRCS) requires the location of the specular point in the delay-Doppler Map (DDM). First, the specular delay tracking algorithm of Fengyun-3 (FY-3) GNSS-R missions is described, which particularly considers the geoid undulation and land topography. The sources and impacts of the specular delay error are discussed. Then, three methods that evaluate and calibrate the specular delay in the FY-3E and FY-3F in-orbit data are proposed: by sea water observations, sea ice observations and waveform retracking. Results from the three methods agree with each other, finding a difference in the specular delay tracking between FY-3E and FY-3F, which also differs for different GNSS systems. The calibration by sea ice observations was found to be the optimal method. Finally, the impacts of delay errors on the ocean wind retrieval are quantified by actual observations and simulations. A bias in the specular delay could cause several issues such as NBRCS inconsistency and a decrease of wind retrieval accuracy depending on the magnitude and sign of the bias.

title: Independent SAR System Calibration of Sentinel-1 C
authors: Patrick Klenk, Kersten Schmidt, Jakob Giez, Andrea Pulella, Matteo Nannini, Pau Prats-Iraola, Marco Schwerdt
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Sentinel-1 C is the third satellite of the Sentinel-1 mission. Launched in late 2024, it ensures seamless continuity of C-band SAR data for global monitoring of the Earth surface in the framework of the Copernicus program. In parallel to the commissioning of Sentinel-1 C by the European Space Agency (ESA), an independent verification of the system calibration has been performed by the German Aerospace Center (DLR) under an ESA contract. Based on an efficient calibration strategy, this paper describes the different activities performed and discusses in detail the results and main findings of DLR during the commissioning phase (CP) of Sentinel-1 C. The main goal is to provide a comprehensive compendium of the SAR calibration and performance status of S1C at the beginning of its operational mission: At the end of the commissioning phase we find that S1C achieves or even exceeds all SAR performance requirements put forth and is ready for its nominal mission operations.

title: Full-Waveform-Accurate 3D Inversion for Transient Electromagnetics via Parallel Spectral Methods
authors: Yao Wang, Jianhui Li, Yinjia Zhu, Hongzhu Cai, Junjie Wu, Xingchun Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Conventional three-dimensional (3D) inversion of transient electromagnetic (TEM) data predominantly employs time-stepping forward modeling, which suffers from inherent limitations in parallelization efficiency and high computational costs. These drawbacks become particularly pronounced when incorporating full transmitter current waveforms, especially those involving waveform repetitions. To address these challenges, this study proposes a 3D inversion method based on spectral forward modeling. By employing MPI+OpenMP hybrid parallel programming technology, efficient parallel computation of magnetic field responses and Jacobian matrices along the frequency dimension is achieved. Additionally, a convolution algorithm is incorporated to integrate the effects of waveform repetition into the forward modeling of full-waveform transmitting current. Validation with synthetic data examples demonstrates that, compared to traditional time-stepping methods, the spectral method exhibits significant advantages in both forward modeling and inversion efficiency. The study also reveals that full-waveform inversion can more accurately reconstruct subsurface electrical structures, with magnetic field (B-field) data inversion showing markedly higher sensitivity to identifying good conductors in low-resistivity environments compared to induced electromotive force (EMF) data. Furthermore, the inclusion of full-waveform information not only improves inversion accuracy but also significantly enhances overall computational efficiency by accelerating residual convergence and reducing iteration counts. In practical applications, inversion results from exploration data in the Qing-chengzi ore concentration area, Liaoning Province, indicate that while the overall geoelectric structures derived from EMF and B-field data inversion are consistent, the B-field data provide more accurate delineation of the vertical extent of deep good conductors. These findings align closely with previous research, fully demonstrating the unique advantages of magnetic field data in deep exploration.

title: Investigating How Brain Stimulation Mitigates Motion Sickness Using Spatiotemporal Nystagmus Parameters Captured in VR
authors: Gang Li, Mark McGill, Alana Grant, Katherina Poehlmann, Rory Holden, Stephen Brewster, Frank Polick
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Mitigating motion sickness with non-invasive cortical vestibular brain stimulation (vBS) is promising because it does not require redesigning VR content or altering the motion profile of a moving platform. Instead, it aims to enhance individuals' intrinsic resistance to motion sickness. However, the mechanism underlying its online efficacy remains unclear (Here, “online efficacy” means the observed motion sickness reduction during vBS not before or after vBS). Motivated by lower motion sickness susceptibility observed in congenital nystagmus patients, we hypothesized that reductions in motion sickness under vBS condition are associated with increased eye movement instability. Leveraging sham-controlled, sex-balanced eye-tracking data from an open-access database, this study presents the first evaluation of a 20 Hz-vBS approach targeting the vestibular cortices as a countermeasure to motion sickness, using spatiotemporal eye movement metrics derived from eye-tracking data collected in VR. Results show that reduced motion sickness under active vBS indeed increased eye movement instability temporarily and spatially, compared to sham vBS. We also found that the temporal metrics was more strongly associated with female participants, whereas the spatial metric was more strongly associated with male participants.

title: Recovering Sewing Patterns from 3D Garment Surfaces via BoxMesh Bridging_supp1-3680930.mp4
authors: Xiaoguang Han
abstract: Recovering sewing patterns from draped 3D garments is a challenging problem in human digitization research. Unlike draping from designed sewing pattern with mature physical simulation engines, the inverse process requires mapping complex garment surfaces back to parametric 2D patterns, which existing methods still struggle with. To this end, we propose a two-stage methodology that recovers sewing patterns from 3D garments via an intermediate representation, BoxMesh, which inherently encodes garment-level geometry and panel-level details in 3D space. In Stage I, a geometry-oriented auto-regressive model predicts BoxMesh from the input 3D garment. In Stage II, a semantic-aware auto-regressive model parses BoxMesh into parametric sewing patterns. This decomposition separately tackles geometric inversion and numerical reasoning, enabling more accurate recovery. Experiments have demonstrated that our method not only achieves state-of-the-art performance on the GarmentCodeData benchmark but also can be applied effectively to real scans and single-view images.

title: Exploring Freehand Selection Techniques of Polyhedron Faces in VR Environments
authors: Yifan Qim, Xuning Hu, Xinan Yan, Wenxuan Xu, Hao Zhang, Hai-Ning Liang, Jin Huang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Virtual reality (VR) allows users to observe and manipulate 3D geometry from multiple viewpoints. Most VR selection work, however, optimizes techniques for selecting entire objects. Selecting a single face on a polyhedron remains underexplored and is more challenging because the interaction must act on a local component while preserving the object's global structure. We introduce a design space tailored to this task with three dimensions: viewing strategy, disambiguation consistency, and interaction metaphor. Guided by this space, we design eight freehand techniques for polyhedral face selection. A within-subjects study with 16 participants evaluates these techniques across polyhedral complexity (two radii; face counts 4, 6, and 12). The results identify three complementary top techniques, reveal tradeoffs between viewing choices and geometric preservation, and yield concrete guidelines for matching techniques to target geometry and task demands. A follow-up study with complex, realistic models confirms the robust ness and practical usability of the three techniques. Together, these contributions shift attention from whole object selection to precise component selection in VR and provide actionable methods for 3D modeling, assembly, and texturing.

title: MultiSenseVR: An open multimodal dataset for human pose estimation and perception in interactive VR
