The model was trained based on cross-entropy loss and Adam optimizer and had an accuracy of about 100 percent, indicating that there was little misclassification between stages. All these findings suggest that the proposed method can be an efficient automated DR severity grading tool that would assist ophthalmologists in performing early diagnosis, disease progression, and treatment plans.

title: Post-training Quantization without BN Statistics: A Data Free Approach
authors: Akash Chavan, Sumit Kumar Jha, Sunny Raj
publish: 2025 International Conference on Machine Learning and Applications (ICMLA)
abstract: Post-training quantization (PTQ) without access to real data is enabling efficient model optimization and deployment in scenarios where privacy or proprietary constraints restrict the use of original datasets. Traditional data free quantization methods rely on Batch Normalization (BN) statistics from the trained full-precision model to generate a calibration dataset for quantization. However, this reliance on BN statistics limits their applicability to deep neural networks (DNNs) without BN layers. In this paper, we propose a calibration dataset generation algorithm that is agnostic to BN statistics, leveraging just the backpropagation to create synthetic images for PTQ. We also demonstrate that it is not necessary to include an image for every target category in the calibration dataset to get the representative activation ranges for quantization. Extensive experiments with both large and lightweight models on large-scale image classification tasks demonstrate that our method consistently improves quantization performance across various DNN architectures, especially in low-bit settings. Notably, in 4-bit quantization, we achieve an improvement of 3.31% in top-1 accuracy for the ResNet18 model and 3.82% for the InceptionV3 model compared to the state-of-the-art (SOTA) DSG method. Importantly, we use very few synthetic images for quantization compared to other methods.

title: Crop Pest and Disease Detection Based on a Multi-Dimensional Improvement of the YOLOv11 Model
authors: Hongbing Ren, Meili Zhou, Zongwen Bai
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Neural Networks, Information and Communication Engineering (NNICE)
abstract: Crop pest and disease detection constitutes a core technology within precision agriculture, where its efficiency and accuracy directly impact yield and quality. However, robust detection remains challenging due to the multi-scale distribution of targets, severe class imbalance, and complex background interference in field environments. To address these issues, this paper proposes a multidimensional enhancement of the YOLO11 architecture. Specifically, we integrate the BiFPN (Bidirectional Feature Pyramid Network) with the C3k2 convolutional module to optimise feature propagation and maximise extraction efficiency across different scales. Furthermore, we introduce a novel objective function, SILDELoss, explicitly designed to mitigate the adverse effects of class imbalance and hard samples. Experiments conducted on a self-constructed dataset demonstrate that the improved model achieves an mAP@50 of 77.4% while maintaining an inference speed of 1.1 ms per frame, fully meeting real-time detection requirements. Compared to the baseline and single-improvement models, our integrated framework exhibits significant advantages in detecting small targets (e.g., aphids) and ensuring class separability under occlusion, providing an efficient solution for automated field monitoring.

title: NNICE 2026 TOC
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Neural Networks, Information and Communication Engineering (NNICE)

title: Collaborative Opportunistic Maintenance Scheduling for Offshore Wind Farms Considering Cognitive Correction
authors: Chengze Rong, Zhenxiang Xiao, Liangwei Xue, Zhenyu Wu
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Neural Networks, Information and Communication Engineering (NNICE)
abstract: High operation and maintenance (O&M) costs and harsh marine environments pose significant challenges to offshore wind farms (OWFs). Existing maintenance strategies often rely on imperfect state information, leading to sub-optimal scheduling. To address these issues, this paper proposes a dynamic collaborative O&M scheduling framework utilizing a heterogeneous fleet of Crew Transfer Vessels (CTVs) and Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs). First, a reliability model based on the Weibull distribution is utilized to characterize the degradation process of wind turbines. Second, a cognitive correction mechanism is introduced, where UAV inspections dynamically update belief states and trigger event-driven re-planning when cognitive bias exceeds a safety threshold. Finally, a rolling horizon optimization model based on Deferral Opportunity Cost is established to balance deterministic dispatching costs against probabilistic future risks. Simulation results on a 40-turbine OWF demonstrate that the proposed method reduces total O&M costs significantly compared to blind scheduling and reactive strategies, effectively minimizing downtime while ensuring high reliability.

title: A Comprehensive Analysis of ML-Based Approaches for IC Yield Prediction
authors: Vishakha Bisouriya, Hrishikesh Das, Sandesh Jain, Manisha Pattanaik, Alok Kumar Kamal
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
abstract: Integrated Circuit (IC) yield prediction has become increasingly challenging due to complex and nonlinear interactions inherent in advanced semiconductor manufacturing processes. Existing deep learning-based approaches often suffer from high computational complexity and are sensitive to choice of various hyperparameters. To circumvent this limitation, we propose two low complexity machine learning (ML) approaches such as random forest and XGBoost for wafer-level yield prediction problem. Simulations performed over real manufacturing data indicate that the proposed random forest and XGBoost algorithms deliver better convergence performance compared to the existing support vector regression algorithm.

title: AgroBots: Revolutionizing Irrigation with Intelligence
authors: S. Thangalakshmi, M. Shunmugathammal, D. Joseph Jeyakumar, Khaja Mannanuddin, Nakka Lakshmi Srikanth, B.Venkataramanaiah
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Smart Electronics and Communication (ICOSEC)
abstract: Agriculture spends a remarkable amount of freshwater for watering the crops. This work proposes a novel approach for agricultural water management in irrigation by using robotics and the Internet of Things (IoT). Moisture level of the soil is periodically observed by soil moisture sensor which is attached with servo motor. Appropriate time irrigation is promised by the water pump and the relay attached with this soil moisture sensor that detects low moisture. IoT technology used in this system monitors the fitness of the crop and its present status could be monitored from remote location. Hardware of this project is having robot chassis which could move around with the help of motor drivers. This movement will help to navigate designated agricultural areas and irrigate precisely where it needs to be. Reducing water wastage, enhancing agricultural crop output, and reducing human involvement in irrigation are the major objectives of this work. Remote monitoring and control are made possible by the integration of IoT capabilities.

title: Applying LLMs to the Classification of Cybersecurity Incident Tickets in a Few-Shot Scenario
authors: Sebastião Alves de Jesus Filho, Alvaro Santana Santos, Rafael Dias Araújo, Rodrigo Sanches Miani
publish: 2025 International Conference on Machine Learning and Applications (ICMLA)
abstract: The growing complexity and volume of cybersecurity incidents have increasingly challenged Computer Security Incident Response Teams (CSIRTs), demanding more efficient solutions for alert triage and categorization. In this work, we investigate the use of large language models (LLMs), such as GEMINI 2.0 Flash and LLaMA 3 70B, for classifying real incident tickets based on the CERT.br taxonomy. We explore zero-shot, one-shot, and two-shot learning approaches to compare model performance under different input configurations. Our results demonstrate that incorporating just a single labeled example (one-shot) is sufficient to increase the average F1-score from approximately 45% to over 90%, highlighting the effectiveness of few-shot learning in this classification task. While the performance among models was similar, we find that the approach shows promise in significantly reducing the human effort required for alert triage. We also discuss limitations related to category ambiguity and data representativeness, and suggest future work, including expanding the dataset, refining the taxonomy, and evaluating new language models, potentially through local deployment for enhanced control and parameter tuning.

title: GAIN-Trans: A Hybrid GAN-Transformer Framework for Robust Missing Data Imputation in Stock Market Time Series
authors: Ananya Raj, Mahadev Ajagalla, Vaishnavi Bisen, Mukesh Kumar Bharti
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
abstract: Missing values in financial data arise from trading suspensions, illiquidity, and data-quality issues, and traditional statistical or purely reconstruction-based deep models often fail to preserve complex temporal dependence and distributional characteristics. GAIN-Trans extends the Generative Adversarial Imputation Networks paradigm by embedding both the generator and discriminator in Transformer encoders, allowing adversarial distribution matching while self-attention layers capture long-range temporal and cross-asset relationships. The generator combines observed entries and noise under a mask-aware Transformer to produce imputations, and the discriminator, equipped with a hint mechanism, learns to distinguish observed from imputed values, with training driven by a joint adversarial and reconstruction loss. Experiments on stock time series with varying missing rates compare GAIN-Trans against statistical, machine learning, GAN-based, and Transformer-only baselines using imputation error and downstream forecasting performance as metrics. Results indicate that GAIN-Trans achieves consistently lower reconstruction error and more realistic volatility and correlation structures, particularly at high missingness levels, demonstrating its effectiveness as a robust imputation tool for financial time series analytics. We further analyze training stability and computational overhead, showing that the proposed hybrid model remains tractable and stable for realistic financial datasets.

title: Neural Network–Based Temporal Alignment of Heterogeneous Multisensor Signals
authors: Lei Wang, Yuxiang Sun, Jun Bao
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Neural Networks, Information and Communication Engineering (NNICE)
abstract: Multi-sensor fusion is a critical foundation for collaborative robot perception and control systems. However, differences in sampling frequency, communication mechanisms, and timestamp accuracy among heterogeneous sensors often lead to temporal misalignment of multi-source data. This paper proposes a time alignment method based on feature-guided interpolation and neural network correction, addressing the time synchronization issue in CAN and serial mixed data acquisition. The method uses high-frequency reference signals to construct a unified time axis and adapts to correct the residual time offset of low-frequency pressure sensors. Experimental results show that the proposed method outperforms traditional alignment methods in terms of time consistency and physical reasoning, improving system robustness and stability.

title: Automated Fruits and Vegetables Detection: Enhancing Food Quality and Supply Chain Monitoring
authors: Suresh K, S. P. Santhoshkumar, P. Raja Packiam, Krishnamoorthi M, Saurabh Chandra, T. Nalini
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Smart Electronics and Communication (ICOSEC)
abstract: Food safety, sustainability, and quality assurance are seriously threatened by the quick deterioration of fresh produce in supply chains. The automated detection systems that combine cutting-edge sensing and imaging technologies for non-destructive quality monitoring are thoroughly examined in this paper. In order to facilitate data-driven tracing and decision support, emerging strategies that make use of data carriers, intelligent indicators, wireless sensor networks (WSN), and deep learning algorithms are investigated. Additionally, the potential of digital twin frameworks is emphasized as a revolutionary tool for simulating, forecasting, and managing the dynamic behavior of fruits and vegetables in real time. The suggested tactics provide a way to enhance food quality preservation, maximize supply chain effectiveness, and lower post-harvest losses by fusing automation, intelligent analytics, and real-time digital monitoring.

title: SQLBarber: A System Leveraging Large Language Models to Generate Customized and Realistic SQL Workloads
authors: Jiale Lao, Immanuel Trummer
publish: Proceedings of the ACM on Management of Data
abstract: None

title: MCP-AI: Protocol-Driven Intelligence Framework for Autonomous Reasoning in Healthcare
authors: Zag ElSayed, Craig Erickson, Ernest Pedapati
publish: 2025 International Conference on Machine Learning and Applications (ICMLA)
abstract: Healthcare AI systems have historically faced challenges in merging contextual reasoning, long-term state management, and human-verifiable workflows into a cohesive framework. This paper introduces a completely innovative architecture and concept: combining the Model Context Protocol (MCP) with a specific clinical application, known as MCP-AI. This integration allows intelligent agents to reason over extended periods, collaborate securely, and adhere to authentic clinical logic, representing a significant shift away from traditional Clinical Decision Support Systems (CDSS) and prompt-based Large Language Models (LLMs). As healthcare systems become more complex, the need for autonomous, context-aware clinical reasoning frameworks has become urgent. We present MCP-AI, a novel architecture for explainable medical decision-making built upon the Model Context Protocol (MCP) a modular, executable specification for orchestrating generative and descriptive AI agents in real-time workflows. Each MCP file captures clinical objectives, patient context, reasoning state, and task logic, forming a reusable and auditable memory object. Unlike conventional CDSS or stateless prompt-based AI systems, MCP-AI supports adaptive, longitudinal, and collaborative reasoning across care settings. MCP-AI is validated through two use cases: (1) diagnostic modeling of Fragile X Syndrome with comorbid depression, and (2) remote coordination for Type 2 Diabetes and hypertension. In either scenario, the protocol facilitates physician-in-the-loop validation, streamlines clinical processes, and guarantees secure transitions of AI responsibilities between healthcare providers. The system connects with HL7/FHIR interfaces and adheres to regulatory standards, such as HIPAA and FDA SaMD guidelines. MCP-AI provides a scalable basis for interpretable, composable, and safety-oriented AI within upcoming clinical environments.

title: On the Task of Detecting Defects in External Structures of Telecommunications Facilities
authors: Kseniya Chepenko, Yuri Leokhin, Timur Fatkhulin
publish: 2026 Systems of Signals Generating and Processing in the Field of on Board Communications
abstract: This paper examines existing and promising methods for detecting various defects in the external structures of telecommunications facilities. The purpose of this work is to define criteria for selecting appropriate methods for examining and analyzing defects in the external structures of telecommunications facilities. The relevance of this research is determined by the need to promptly identify and prevent potential problems caused by leaks, deformations, and loss of thermal insulation in telecommunications infrastructure facilities. Such phenomena lead to negative consequences, including deterioration in the technical condition of equipment, increased operating costs, and potential risks of emergency situations. The object of this study is the external structures of telecommunications facilities. The subject of this study is the criteria for selecting the appropriate defect detection method. The research methodology consists of methods of theoretical analysis, comparison, description, systematization, and generalization. Finally, conclusions are drawn and recommendations are given for selecting appropriate methods for detecting various defects in the external structures of telecommunications facilities.

title: GDFP-Net: A Parallel Feature Fusion-Based Network For Automatic Vessels Segmentation From Fundus Images
authors: Nana Qi, Shenao Zhang, Dongxiang Wang, Jinke Wang
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Neural Networks, Information and Communication Engineering (NNICE)
abstract: In clinical diagnosis, the curvature, shape and spatial distribution of retinal vessels are important criteria for diagnosing diabetes. Although deep convolutional neural networks (CNNs) have achieved good results in retinal vessel segmentation, the segmentation accuracy of thin vessels is still limited due to insufficient extraction of detailed features. To alleviate the problem of detail information loss, this paper proposes a global detail feature processing network (GDFP-Net), which consists of a global segmentation network (GSN), a detail enhancement network (DEN), and a feature processing network (FPN). Among them, GSN and DEN work in parallel to enhance the feature extraction capabilities of thick and thin vessels segmentation.

title: A Data-Driven Approach for Sleep Stage Detection Using Machine Learning and Feature Selection Algorithms
authors: Md Nahid Hasan, Md Sultan Mahmud, Clifford Jones, Saroj Baral, Khem Poudel
publish: 2025 International Conference on Machine Learning and Applications (ICMLA)
abstract: In the United States, more than 50 million individuals are affected by sleep disorders. Accurate detection of sleep stages is crucial for both personal health monitoring and social well-being. Accurate identification of sleep stages is essential for diagnosing sleep disorders and understanding sleep architecture. Traditional approaches based on polysomnography (PSG), while clinically reliable, are often costly, intrusive, and limited to laboratory settings. With the increasing availability of wearable sensors and advances in data-driven methods, machine learning (ML) offers a scalable and automated alternative for sleep stage classification. In this study, we develop and evaluate an ML framework for identifying sleep stages using physiological signals such as photoplethysmography (PPG), PSG, and PPG-PSG feature fusion. Particularly, we utilized nine statistical features with classical ML, stability selection, and PCA on top of the stability selection algorithm. Our analysis demonstrates that the PPG-PSG feature fusion achieved the highest classification accuracy of 97% in detecting sleep stages using LightGBM. Our separate analyses using the PPG and PSG showed that both modalities yielded nearly identical performance. The best classification performance was obtained with LightGBM, with an accuracy of 93.19% and 90.61% using PSG and PPG features, respectively.

title: Auto Bore Cast – A RNN Driven Intelligent Borewell Drill Estimator
authors: Elizabeth Rani G, Gowjesh P, Sakthimohan M, V. Aruna Janani, Southesh.S, Gowsika P
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Smart Electronics and Communication (ICOSEC)
abstract: Water is essential for life, and in many areas, people depend on borewells as their main source. However, drilling a borewell often feels risky. Some attempts succeed, while others fail, leading to significant financial losses and disappointment. This uncertainty arises because most drilling decisions are made without sufficient reliable information. To address this problem, we created Borewell Mapper, an easy-to-use tool that collects and analyzes data through RNN algorithm. It includes information about groundwater levels, and past borewell results. By processing this data, the system gives users practical advice on where a borewell is more likely to succeed. The goal is to reduce failures, lower costs, and encourage responsible groundwater use. With Borewell agents, farmers and communities can make smarter decisions based on data and feel more confident before they invest in borewell drill.

title: Human Pose Recognition Algorithm Based on Radar-Visual Data Fusion
authors: Jing Zhang, Jingqi Wang, Xiancun Zhou, Jiaqi Jiang
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Neural Networks, Information and Communication Engineering (NNICE)
abstract: With the rapid advancement of intelligent surveillance, smart transportation, and human-computer interaction technologies, the importance of high-precision human pose estimation has become increasingly prominent. Traditional single-sensor recognition methods exhibit significant limitations, whereas multimodal data fusion technology can effectively enhance the accuracy and robustness of pose discrimination. Specifically, LiDAR provides millimeter-level precision in 3D spatial information and depth perception, making it suitable for fine-grained modeling in complex scenarios. Millimeter-wave radar, on the other hand, demonstrates exceptional performance in adverse conditions such as rain, fog, and dust, maintaining stable detection capabilities.By fusing the complementary data from these two sensors, the system’s discriminative accuracy and adaptability in complex environments can be significantly enhanced. This research focuses on radar-vision multimodal data fusion technology. Leveraging millimeter-wave radar point clouds and LiDAR 3D data, we investigate fusion algorithms and human pose discrimination techniques. Experimental results conclusively demonstrate that the performance of multimodal data fusion technology significantly surpasses that of single-sensor methods. The findings provide theoretical support for next-generation intelligent surveillance systems and hold broad application prospects in fields such as security patrols, autonomous driving, human-robot collaboration, and smart elderly care.

title: Recent Advancements in Course Recommendation Systems in the Era of Large Language Models (2022–2025)
authors: Satyam Mittal, Ishita Malhotra
abstract: Course Recommendation Systems (CRS) help learners choose courses aligned with goals, yet face cold-start, limited interpretability, prerequisite feasibility, and fairness challenges. This review synthesizes peer-reviewed course recommendation research from 2022–2025 and reports improvements strictly within each study (baseline→best) to avoid invalid cross-paper pooling across datasets and protocols. The surveyed methods span session/set recommendation, temporal and knowledge graphs (KGs) augmented sequence models, and fairness-aware multi-graph learning, alongside CRS-adjacent large language models (LLMs)-based retrieval and re-ranking patterns discussed in recent education recommendation pipelines. We further refine the taxonomy with peer-reviewed evidence on prerequisite-aware recommendation, data-quality-aware KGs, and interpretable CRS grounded in catalog text or graph paths. Lastly, we summarize reported deployment considerations (retrieval→re-ranking, indexing, summarization, quantization) and identify open challenges in offline–online alignment, feasibility/data-quality reporting, and privacy-preserving, auditable deployments.

title: Self-Supervised Multisensory Pretraining for Contact-Rich Robot Reinforcement Learning_supp1-3681156.mp4
authors: Rickmer Krohn
abstract: Effective contact-rich manipulation requires robots to synergistically leverage vision, force, and proprioception. However, Reinforcement Learning agents struggle to learn in such multisensory settings, especially amidst sensory noise and dynamic changes. We propose MultiSensory Dynamic Pretraining (MSDP), a novel framework for learning expressive multisensory representations tailored for task-oriented policy learning. MSDP is based on masked autoencoding and trains a transformer-based encoder by reconstructing multisensory observations from only a subset of sensor embeddings, leading to cross-modal prediction and sensor fusion. For downstream policy learning, we introduce a novel asymmetric architecture, where a cross-attention mechanism allows the critic to extract dynamic, task-specific features from the frozen embeddings, while the actor receives a stable pooled representation to guide its actions. Our method demonstrates accelerated learning and robust performance under diverse perturbations, including sensor noise, and changes in object dynamics. Evaluations in multiple challenging, contact-rich robot manipulation tasks in simulation and the real world showcase the effectiveness of MSDP. Our approach exhibits strong robustness to perturbations and achieves high success rates on the real robot with as few as 6,000 online interactions, offering a simple yet powerful solution for complex multisensory robotic control.

title: FlockCounter: Automated Counting for Large-Scale Bird Flocks
authors: Tianjiang Hu, Weiming Yan, Zijie Sun
abstract: Automated bird counting is crucial for ecological assessment, yet current methods often lack accuracy with large-scale flocks. This paper proposes and develops FlockCounter, a novel approach for end-to-end flock counting that relies solely on synthetic training data. Our method consists of three stages. Labeled synthetic data of large-scale flocks is automatically generated by using a collective behavioral model with only a limited set of real-world bird flock trajectories. Each annotated image is sequentially enhanced into the visual realism via style transfer. Finally, we train a customized deep learning network exclusively on synthetic data to achieve accurate bird flock counting. As demonstrated in the testing results, FlockCounter achieves state-of-the-art cross-domain performance on real-world datasets, attaining 91.3% accuracy on ground-based images and 92.3% on aerial-view images, substantially outperforming existing methods. This work provides a scalable, annotation-free solution for accurate large-scale bird flock counting, supporting efficient ecological monitoring and conservation efforts.

title: On Weighted Optimality of Excitation Trajectory for Inertial Parameter Identification With Hierarchical Importance
authors: Ben Liu, Wei Zhang, Hua Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Control Systems Technology
abstract: This brief studies optimal excitation trajectory design for inertial parameter identification of rigid body systems. Despite extensive research on this classical problem, existing works mainly focus on designing a trajectory to ensure the accuracy of the inertial parameter estimation by treating each parameter equally important. In general, however, different tasks require different accuracy levels for different subsets of the inertial parameters. This characteristic, which involves identifying specific sets of parameters with higher accuracy for distinct tasks, has not received sufficient attention in existing works. To address this issue, we propose a novel weighted criterion to generate excitation trajectories that lead to the estimation of the inertial parameters that can adapt to different tasks requiring different accuracy levels. The proposed criterion has a nice coordinate-invariant property, which avoids the potential performance variance due to different selections of coordinate systems. It is also shown that the change of coordinates is equivalent to a coordinate-dependent weight assignment, which explains how the change of coordinates impacts the optimal excitation trajectory. Finally, experiment validations illustrate that, for different tasks, the proposed criterion outperforms other criteria used for the comparison in this brief.

title: Distributed Resource Allocation for Interference Mitigation in Heterogeneous WBANs: a Health-Adaptive Nonuniform Coloring Strategy
authors: Xiangling Peng, Xuxun Liu, Jian Su, Alex X. Liu
abstract: Interference mitigation is critical for ensuring re liable communication and high quality of service (QoS) in dense and heterogeneous wireless body area networks (WBANs). However, existing approaches often struggle with the scarcity of transmission resources and the heterogeneity of traffic demands. To overcome these challenges, this paper proposes a Health-Adaptive Nonuniform Coloring (HANC) strategy, a novel distributed resource allocation approach. HANC models interference relationships as a graph where structurally central vertices serve as local decision-makers to autonomously assign channel-time resources. This strategy incorporates a token bucket mechanism to quantify traffic demand and a health evaluation model based on vital signs and clinical risks, forming a composite vertex value that determines allocation priority. HANC employs a localized coloring dissemination mechanism to enable conflict-free propagation of allocation results without global synchronization. The key innovations of HANC lie in the prioritization assignment based on real-time patient status, the nonuniform resource allocation proportional to diverse demands, and the decentralized scheduling through local decision-makers. These features collectively enhance transmission reliability, re source utilization fairness, and network scalability in dynamic environments. Extensive simulations demonstrate that HANC outperforms existing schemes in channel utilization, interference reduction, throughput, packet delivery ratio, latency, and energy efficiency, while exhibiting strong robustness in high-density scenarios.

title: GOF-Based DMI Integrated Solution to Seamless Navigation for Autonomous Vehicles During GNSS Outages
authors: Wenhui Yang, Yong Li, Chris Rizos
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Autonomous vehicles rely on the integration of inertial navigation system (INS) and global navigation satellite system (GNSS) for most transportation applications. However, GNSS signals are often degraded in challenging environments such as urban canyons and tunnels, etc. To achieve seamless navigation with continuity and safety, it is necessary to deploy an auxiliary or backup navigation system that does not rely on GNSS. This paper proposes a Dual Micro-electro-mechanical INS (DMI) architecture comprising both rotating and fixed inertial measurement units (IMUs), and utilizes vehicle chassis information to aid the INS during GNSS outages. In terms of multi-sensor fusion, traditional optimal multi-sensor fusion takes the Centralized Kalman Filter (CKF) as a global optimal criterion. The Decentralized Kalman Filter (DKF), such as the well-known Federated Kalman Filter (FKF), aims to achieve the same accuracy as a CKF, while reducing the computational load. This paper innovatively applies an improved decentralized filtering method - Global Optimal Filter (GOF), and develops a GOF-based DMI integrated solution. This solution optimizes state estimation by fully utilizing multi-source data through spatial projection and orthogonalization. The field test results have demonstrated the GOF-based DMI integrated solution has significant advantages in most evaluation metrics. Specifically, it achieves accuracy improvements of 29.1% in position, 30% in velocity, 22.2% in attitude, and 58.3% in heading over the CKF-based DMI integrated solution. Compared to the FKF-based DMI integrated solution, the accuracy improvements are even more substantial: 70.4% in position, 36.3% in velocity, 70.5% in horizontal attitude, and 72.5% in heading. In addition, the introduction of extended KITTI evaluation metrics also indicates that the GOF-based DMI integration solution has significant advantages compared to the other two solutions.

title: Evolutionary Stackelberg Routing for Social Cost Reduction in Traffic Networks
authors: Andrea Bertolini, Lorenzo Castelli, Raffaele Pesenti
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: An evolutionary Stackelberg framework for a two-population routing game is proposed, in which a boundedly rational central controller manages a fleet of vehicles sharing the network with selfish drivers. The controller assigns routes to minimize social cost by managing her fleet via the E-Aloof strategy, a low-information, adaptive rule that requires only the observation of the instantaneous traffic distribution and no memory of past states; E-Aloof is an evolutionary extension of the greedy, static Aloof strategy. The drivers of the remaining vehicles behave selfishly and myopically, adapting routes according to the Stackelberg Replicator Dynamic by comparing their travel costs with those of randomly sampled vehicles. The work formulates the problem as a nonatomic routing game on a two-parallel-link network. It establishes the existence of asymptotically stable equilibria under standard assumptions of continuously differentiable, nondecreasing, convex cost functions. The conditions under which centralization improves performance are derived, showing that while Aloof is beneficial when at least half of the vehicles are centralized, E-Aloof requires fewer centralized vehicles depending on the cost functions. For linear costs, conditions are found under which the equilibria coincide with those of a full-information Stackelberg game.

title: Model-predictive Vibration Reduction of a Suspended Cable-Driven Parallel Robot_supp1-3681158.mp4
authors: Sophie ROUSSEAU
abstract: This letter focuses on the vibration reduction for Cable-Driven Parallel Robots. A Non-linear Model Predictive Controller (NMPC) is designed and implemented to adapt the control inputs sent to the robot actuators. This NMPC is based on an elasto-dynamic model of the robot, which is discretized and included in a constrained optimal control problem. Two experiments are conducted and the performance of the NMPC is compared with a Computed Torque Controller and a NMPC with an inelastic model. The first one is a trajectory following task and shows that the proposed controller significantly reduces the amplitude of the vibrations of the Moving-Platform along the trajectory and the second one focuses on a sudden braking situation and shows that the proposed control scheme manages to divide the amplitude of the post-braking oscillations by factors ranging from one and a half to twenty-two, demonstrating the interest of this controller for perturbation rejection.

title: AdaSch: Load-Adaptive Scheduling for Accelerating Real-Time Edge Video Analysis With Diverse Inference Modes
authors: Zheyi Chen, Jia Wen, Junjie Zhang, Lexi Xu, Jie Li, Geyong Min
abstract: Inference with spatio-temporal filtering plays a vital role in accelerating emerging edge video analysis. However, most existing solutions rely on a single inference mode, limiting their ability to cope with the heterogeneity and dynamic nature of edge systems. While combining multiple inference modes can enhance the real-time performance of video analysis, achieving efficient load scheduling across these modes remains challenging due to diverse device configurations and varying task requests. To address this important challenge, we propose AdaSch, a novel load-adaptive scheduling framework designed to accelerate real-time edge video analysis using diverse inference modes. First, we design a new inference mode selection mechanism based on Region-of-Interest (RoI) features in different scenarios of video analysis. Next, we develop a Model Predictive Control (MPC)-based RoI scheduling method, which predicts continuous RoI sequences and obtains the optimal scheduling using a linear programming solver. Finally, we design a probabilistic sparse self-attention mechanism to capture server-side load patterns, enabling inference scheduling with optimal load balancing by solving a relaxed convex optimization problem. Extensive experiments on the real-world testbed and video datasets demonstrate the superiority of AdaSch. Compared to state-of-the-art methods, AdaSch improves accuracy and Service Level Objective (SLO) satisfaction rate by approximately 19.20% and 42.94%, respectively. Moreover, AdaSch exhibits excellent adaptiveness across various scenarios while maintaining minimal overheads.

title: FedSecureFormer: A Fast, Federated and Secure Transformer Framework for Lightweight Intrusion Detection in Connected and Autonomous Vehicles
authors: Devika Sathyan, Vishnu Hari, Pratik Narang, Tejasvi Alladi, F. Richard Yu
abstract: The era of Connected and Autonomous Vehicles (CAVs) reflects a significant milestone in transportation, improving safety, efficiency, and intelligent navigation. However, the growing reliance on real-time communication, constant connectivity, and autonomous decision-making raises serious cybersecurity concerns, particularly in environments with limited resources. This highlights the need for security solutions that are efficient, lightweight, and suitable for deployment in real-world vehicular environments. In this work, we introduce FedSecureFormer, a lightweight transformer-based model with 1.7 million parameters, significantly smaller than most encoder-only transformer architectures. The model is designed for efficient and accurate cyber attack detection, achieving 93.69% classification accuracy across 19 attack types and improving performance on 10 attack classes, outperforming several state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods. To assess its practical viability, we implemented the model within a Federated Learning (FL) setup using the FedAvg aggregation strategy. We also incorporated differential privacy to enhance data protection. For testing its generalization to unseen attacks, we used a histogram-guided GAN with LSTM and attention modules to generate unseen data, achieving 88% detection accuracy. Notably, the model achieved an inference time of 3.7775 milliseconds per vehicle on the Jetson Nano, approximately 100× faster than SOTA models. These results position FedSecureFormer as a fast, scalable, and privacy-preserving solution for securing future intelligent transport systems.

title: Rateless Deep Joint Source-Channel Coding for Task-Oriented Image Communications
authors: Zijun Qin, Zesong Fei, Jingxuan Huang, Jing Wang, Xianhao Chen, Zhi Zhang, Ming Xiao
abstract: The advance of vehicle-to-everything (V2X) networks has led to many emerging data-intensive applications at the network edge. To meet the soaring data rate requirements of these applications, numerous coding schemes has been developed. However, the high heterogeneity of edge users bring challenges to these methods, including adaptation to performance requirements, coping with unknown or varying channels, as well as inefficient multicasting. In this paper, we address those problems by developing a rateless deep joint source-channel coding scheme featuring fine-grained control over rate and informativeness at the user. Towards this end, we first design a novel class of variational information bottleneck (VIB) by employing the multinomial-Gaussian (MG) distribution, to achieve rateless transmission over an erasure channel. We derived important results on the statistical properties of this latent distribution to facilitate efficient training of MG-VIB. Then, we apply this framework to multicasting, proposing MG-VIB-M to enhance adaptability and scalability. Simulations show that our proposed method is more flexible regarding rate-relevance tradeoffs, has greater robustness against channel imperfections, and reduces bandwidth requirements for task-oriented multicasting.

title: High Input Impedance Chopper-stabilized Analog Front-end Based on Variable-capacitor Calibration for Bio-consumer Electronics
authors: Feng Yan, Jingjing Liu, Weijie Ge, Kangkang Sun, Xinying Su, Junhao He
abstract: Analog front-end (AFE) circuits for bio-consumer electronics require ultra-high input impedance to ensure robust bio-potential acquisition under interference. This paper presents a chopping-stabilized AFE featuring an automatic input-impedance enhancement technique based on a positive feedback loop (PFL) with a continuously tunable variable-capacitor. An oscillation-detection-based calibration loop dynamically adjusts the PFL to compensate front-end parasitic capacitance, driving the system from an unstable condition to an optimal stable boundary with maximized input impedance while avoiding over-compensation. To improve calibration robustness, a frequency ripple suppression scheme is introduced, enabling precise control of the variable-capacitor independent of input frequency variations. The proposed variable-capacitance technique provides femtofarad-level resolution without relying on discrete capacitor arrays, achieving wide tuning range and high linearity. Fabricated in a 0.18 μm CMOS process, the AFE achieves an input impedance of 46.3 GΩ at 50 Hz, representing an 83× improvement over conventional approaches. The prototype occupies 0.71 mm², achieves a mid-band gain of 40 dB, an input-referred noise of 0.93 μV<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">rms</sub>, and a power consumption of 3.48 μW. Experimental EEG and ECG measurements using comfortable dry electrodes validate the effectiveness of the proposed AFE for bio-consumer electronic applications.

title: Embedded Vibration Monitoring and Robust Closed-Loop Current Demodulation Using an Eight-State Square-Wave Scheme in Spun QWP-Based Reflective FOCS
authors: Jundong Tian, Shunyang Liu, Wei Li, Li Xia
abstract: Spun quarter-wave plates (QWPs) can be co-fabricated and packaged with spun fiber sensing coils, which reduces fusion splices and improves device consistency. Their distributed birefringence and extended length also increase sensitivity to mechanical vibration. In reflective all-fiber optic current sensors (FOCS), vibration can change the interferometric visibility. This change acts as a time-varying equivalent closed-loop gain and can affect AC current demodulation. We develop an analytical polarization transfer model for a spun QWP and describe vibration as a time-varying perturbation of the dimensionless rotation parameter. We then propose an eight-state square-wave modulation with synchronous three-channel demodulation. Within one modulation period, it yields a current error signal, a half-wave-voltage drift observable, and a visibility observable. The visibility-related channel can be used to track the vibration-induced waveform. It can also be used to normalize the current error so that the effective loop gain is less sensitive to visibility changes. Tests from 0 to 30 Hz indicate that, under these low-frequency conditions, the sensing-coil phase disturbance is small in the reflective configuration. The dominant effect is visibility variation associated with the spun QWP. With the proposed normalization, the mean standard deviation of the extracted AC-current peaks and valleys decreases from 0.27 A to 0.094 A at 50 Hz and from 0.28 A to 0.089 A at 100 Hz. The method does not require additional components and can improve demodulation robustness in vibratory environments.

title: IPM-Net: An Interpretable Partial-level Matching Network for Few-shot Fine-grained Classification in Optical Remote Sensing Images
authors: Kaiwen Zhu, Song Qiu, Jingyi Cao, Yanan You, Yuanyuan Qiao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Deep learning has enabled AI-driven fine-grained object classification in optical remote sensing imagery with remarkable precision. However, insufficient training samples critically hinder their performance in data-scarce scenarios such as military operations and natural disaster response, significantly diminishing the practical utility of deep learning-based approaches. Consequently, few-shot fine-grained classification methods have garnered substantial attention due to their superior ability in knowledge learning and transfer under limited data conditions. Current approaches prioritize discriminative feature extraction and enhancement, but lack mathematical definition of fine-grained features and an explainable analysis of how these features influence decision-making processes. This deficiency prevents models from explicitly identifying critical features for knowledge transfer. In response to this limitation, we introduce the Interpretable Partial-level Matching Network (IPM-Net), the main goal is to match the query target’s partials in the support set, thus, building and visualizing the decision-making process. This network consists of three modules: a Partial-level Game-theoretic Interpretable Module(PGI), Cross-pose Semantic Alignment Module(CSA), and Refine-and-Rediscover Module(RaR). Experiments on a variety of fine-grained datasets—both remote sensing and natural images—confirm IPM-Net’s superiority in accuracy and interpretability. The source code is publicly available on GitHub: https://github.com/Silver2kw/IPM-Net.

title: Nonlinear Correction Method for Radiometric Consistency in Remote Sensing Images Based on Global Optimisation
authors: Litao Li, Haoqi Liang, Yonghua Jiang, Xin Shen
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Mosaicking multiple remote sensing images is an effective approach to rapidly expand the coverage of remote sensing imagery, and an indispensable process in large-area mapping using remote sensing imagery. Differences in sensor properties, imaging conditions, and processing algorithms cause radiometric variations, requiring consistency correction during mosaicking. However, most existing methods employ linear correction models based on statistical information between images, which are often inadequate for addressing nonlinear discrepancies caused by the above differences. This study proposes a nonlinear correction method for achieving radiometric consistency in multiple remote sensing images, based on global optimisation. A quadratic local constraint model, combined with a global mean-variance preservation constraint, ensures smooth transitions and prevents colour shifts. Validation with multi-temporal high-resolution images shows the method outperforms Wallis filtering, Histogram matching, linear correction, and TRR in handling nonlinear radiance differences. The mosaic products exhibit natural visual quality and smooth colour transitions, achieving colour balance and avoiding excessive radiometric correction. The method reduces the mean of absolute mean differences and the mean of absolute standard deviation differences by 30.5% and 44.5% versus uncorrected images, and by 17.5% and 6.1% versus linear correction. The color distance drops 81.2%, while the gradient loss remains low (0.64), preserving spectral fidelity and structural details. In terms of global radiometric stability, the method, maintaining the overall mean value above 93.4% of all the uncorrected images, an improvement of 10.3% compared to the linear method, achieving effective radiometric balance and consistency, and effectively enhancing the mapping quality of large-area remote sensing imagery.

title: DPMVectorNet: End-to-End Ship Detection, Heading, and Velocity Estimation via Dual-Point Motion Vectors in Satellite Imagery
authors: Xiunan Li, Peng Chen, Haitian Zhu, Lei Huang, Wentao An, Ling Zhu, Gang Zheng
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Ship motion analysis using remote sensing imagery is essential for maritime surveillance, traffic monitoring, and ocean management. Existing optical detection methods, however, have primarily concentrated on identifying static ship targets through horizontal or rotated bounding boxes, and therefore cannot effectively estimate dynamic attributes such as heading and velocity. In this work, we develop DPMVectorNet (Dual-Point Motion Vector Network), an end-to-end learning framework designed for the joint estimation of ship position, heading, and speed from medium-resolution multi-temporal satellite imagery. Specifically, the framework is evaluated on multi-temporal ASTER VNIR data (15 m spatial resolution), in which temporally adjacent observations are combined to construct RGB composite images, which are then processed by a convolutional neural network to capture and fuse multi-scale spatial features. Ship motion is represented through the temporal matching of two bow keypoints, from which the movement direction is derived, while the velocity is computed from their displacement and the corresponding imaging interval. Experiments conducted on multi-temporal ASTER data show that DPMVectorNet achieves an F1-score above 90% for ship detection, with a mean heading error below 2°, and a velocity deviation within 1 m/s when compared with Automatic Identification System (AIS) records. These results demonstrate that the proposed framework enables accurate ship detection and reliable heading and velocity estimation from medium-resolution imagery by effectively exploiting multi-temporal information.

title: Fault Isolation in Multi-Cell Series Battery System with Continuous Power Delivery
authors: Harzaan P, Ravishankar G, Gopalakrishnan R, Sri Vidhya D, Indumathi V, Chandrakumar E
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Smart Electronics and Communication (ICOSEC)
abstract: The dependability of multi-cellular series battery structures could be a key difficulty with programs that come equipped with electric car motivators, clean-up strength generation garage and uninterrupted strength supply systems. A single cell failure in series configuration may jeopardize the all percentage of the battery, and cause energy loss and inadequate performances and capability protection. To handle such an assignment, this paper presents a framework of fault isolation to determine stretching the non-stop transport of strengths at the time of cellular fault conditions. The project gadget combines three complementary strategies. First, fast-acting bypass switches are located all through the man or woman cells, to without delay circuit contemporary all through faulty devices, avoiding astonishing interruptions. Second, redundant/ spare cellular modules are integrated to dynamically update failed cells, thus maintaining both voltage and system potential. Third, an AI-enabled monitoring and prediction machine continuously evaluates the fitness fame of every mobile the usage of voltage, temperature and impedance information and enable early () fault detection and proactive isolation. The combination of those techniques presents a robust and scalable solution to increase fault tolerance, safety and increase the operating lifetime of battery systems. Simulation studies prove the feasibility of proposed technique, allowing the demonstration of fast fault isolation, electricity shipping and the correct prediction of cell degradation. The framework is suitable primarily for the performance of undertakings-crucial strength storage programs, in which the reliability and safety cannot be compromised.

title: An Imbalance-Aware Machine Learning Approach to Credit Card Fraud Detection Using SMOTE & AdaBoost
authors: Priyanka Kaushik, Atul Kumar Mishra, Anjna Kumari, Rahul Pradhan, Payal Gosai, Mahalakshmi Sampath
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
abstract: Online banking & transactions have caused a high adoption rate of credit cards, which has made life easier but on the other hand, it has caused the increase of fraudulent practices & financial losses. Conventional rule based fraud detection systems are not able to keep up with the changing trends of fraud. The paper will present a machine learning-based fraud detection model based on Support Vector Machine, Random-Forest, Logistic Regression & Decision Tree models. SMOTE & AdaBoost are used to solve class imbalance & maximum accuracy of detection. The experiments show that the suggested method will greatly enhance the detection of scam credit card transactions. The model demonstrates a high possibility of effective fraud detection in dynamic & complicated web-based transaction setup to improve the safety of online payment systems.

title: Revolutionizing Accessibility with Multi-Modal Text-to-Speech Conversion
authors: Malathi.M, P. Nagarajan, N. Ashokkumar, M.Bharathi, Rahul Krishnan, Kavitha Thandapani
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Smart Electronics and Communication (ICOSEC)
abstract: With the help of OCR features, this article discusses the development of accessibility enhancements with the invention of a new multi-modal text to speech (TTS) system which allows users to easily convert text from images and web articles into audio format. Students, professionals, and just about anyone can access the system and takes full advantage of the features it offers. Using translation and language detection, the system guarantees that the extracted text is coherent and linguistically appropriate. The system addresses two very important problems: first, users who do not speak the language of the content can translate it to the language they prefer, and second, in a dynamic and fast paced society where every second counts, users can transform articles or online newspaper links into audio format designed for easy listening. The clear alterations in OCR accessibility encourage equal opportunities and inclusivity, allowing users to engage with information when needed.

title: Traffic Signal Control Using Reinforcement Learning
authors: Sneha Shrigiri, Shreya Patil, Unnati Lunawat, Sanika Karanjekar, Riddhi R. Mirajkar, Rishikaysh Kaakandikar
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive study of intelligent traffic signal control using reinforcement learning (RL). We review the need for adaptive signal control in modern urban environments and propose a system that leverages RL algorithms (Q-learning, SARSA, and PPO) to optimize intersection timing. A block diagram of the system architecture is provided, and each algorithm is described with pseudocode. Experiments in a SUMO simulation environment demonstrate comparative results, showing significant reductions in vehicle delay and queue lengths. We also discuss hardware/software requirements and a conceptual GUI for system interaction. Our findings highlight the potential of RL-based controllers to significantly improve traffic flow compared to traditional fixed-time or actuated systems.

title: Swin Transformer with Bert Bi-Directional Co-Attention Framework for Visual Question Answering
authors: Smriti Chand, Dinesh Kumar, Manjeet Kumar
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
abstract: The goal of Visual Question Answering (VQA), a cross-disciplinary task that combines computer vision and natural language processing, is to produce precise answers to questions about a particular scene that are presented in natural language. Convolutional neural networks serve as the foundation for visual feature extraction in most previous studies. In this work, we present a Swin-BERT co-attention VQA framework that combines a bidirectional encoder representation from transformers (BERT) model for capturing question semantics with the hierarchical feature extraction capability of the Swin Transformer for images. In order to efficiently integrate and refine visual and textual features, the proposed model uses a bi-directional co-attention mechanism. These features are then aggregated through pooling, combined via fusion, and classified over the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1, 0 0 0}$</tex> most common answer categories. A reduced amount of the VQA-v2 dataset-25,498 Q/A pairs for training and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3,400 \mathrm{Q} / \mathrm{A}$</tex> pairs for validation-was used for the experiments. The strength of integrating hierarchical visual transformers with transformer-based language models via a co-attention strategy is demonstrated by the suggested framework's accuracy of 72.61 %. This synergy significantly enhances cross-modal reasoning and demonstrates the importance of combining textual and visual elements to improve performance when answering visual questions.

title: Joint Degradation-Aware Arbitrary-Scale Super-Resolution for Variable-Rate Extreme Image Compression
authors: Xinning Chai, Zhengxue Cheng, Xin Li, Rong Xie, Li Song
abstract: Recent diffusion-based extreme image compression methods have demonstrated remarkable performance at ultra-low bitrates (0.1<0.1 bpp). However, most approaches require training separate diffusion models for each target bitrate, resulting in substantial computational overhead and hindering practical deployment. Meanwhile, recent studies have shown that joint super-resolution can serve as an effective approach for enhancing low-bitrate reconstruction. However, when moving toward ultra-low-bitrate regimes, these methods struggle due to severe information loss, and their reliance on fixed super-resolution scales prevents flexible adaptation across diverse bitrates. To address these limitations, we propose ASSR-EIC, a novel image compression framework that leverages arbitrary-scale super-resolution (ASSR) to support variable-rate extreme image compression (EIC). An arbitrary-scale downsampling module is introduced at the encoder side to provide controllable rate reduction, while a diffusion-based, joint degradation-aware ASSR decoder enables rate-adaptive reconstruction within a single model. We exploit the compression- and rescaling-aware diffusion prior to guide the reconstruction, yielding high-fidelity and high-realism restoration across diverse compression and rescaling settings. Specifically, we design a global compression–rescaling adaptor that offers holistic guidance for rate adaptation, and a local compression–rescaling modulator that dynamically balances generative and fidelity-oriented behaviors to achieve fine-grained, bitrate-adaptive detail restoration. To further enhance reconstruction quality, we introduce a dual semantic-enhanced design. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ASSR-EIC delivers state-of-the-art performance in extreme image compression while simultaneously supporting flexible bitrate control and adaptive rate-dependent reconstruction.

title: Hybrid Deep Learning based Architecture for Cattle Disease Classification
authors: Isan Agniswar Banerjee, Aritra Rana, Shreya Roy, Koyena Paul, Md. Ashifuddin Mondal, Subhram Das, Papri Ghosh
publish: 2025 International Conference on Sustainable Technology in Energy and Power Systems (STEPCon)
abstract: In veterinary research, early and accurate detection of diseases in cattle is important for maintaining animal health and production. Traditional medical tests are often time-consuming and require expertise. This study investigates a method that combines convolutional autoencoders (CAE) and convolutional neural networks (CNN) to identify cattle diseases in order to improve accurate and correct diagnosis. CAE is used for unsupervised feature extraction, thereby reducing noise and highlighting critical patterns in disease datasets. Hierarchical learning is then used by CNN Classifiers to get high classification accuracy. The model is trained and tested on a benchmark dataset that contains images and metadata about common cattle diseases. Experimental results on detailed data of cattle images show that the hybrid model outperforms the traditional CNN model in terms of accuracy, with the hybrid CNN model demonstrating an accuracy of 92% and a RMSE value of approximately 0.35. The hydrid deep learning based architecture has proved to be a useful approach for cattle disease classification.

title: Bio-Inspired Variance Control: Decoupling Signaling Mode from Power Constraints in Neuromorphic Systems
authors: Rakesh Sengupta
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
abstract: Advanced intelligent systems and IoT networks face a critical trade-off between signaling flexibility and power efficiency. Traditional rate-coding schemes often require shifting the mean activity to transmit information, incurring high energy costs that threaten system stability. Drawing inspiration from the Log-Normal firing regimes of the cerebral cortex, we propose a novel coding mechanism: Homeostatic Variance Control. We mathematically demonstrate that by modulating the variance of input noise (a proxy for neuromorphic gain), a system can dramatically shift its modal (most frequent) operating point toward sparsity while pinning its median resource consumption to a fixed homeostatic setpoint. We validate this mechanism using a Linear-Nonlinear-Poisson (LNP) simulation, showing that it enables a seamless transition between dense and sparse coding regimes without altering the baseline power budget. This bioinspired architecture offers a rigorous framework for designing resilient, energy-efficient Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) for resource-constrained cyber-physical applications.

title: Fake Currency Detection using Image Processing and Machine Learning
authors: Payel V L, S L Saran Kumar, Chayanam Karthik, Bhavana V
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
abstract: Free circulation of fake money is a significant threat to the financial systems. The present paper contains a multifaceted and fully automated approach to the detection of fake Indian 500 and 2000 notes based on image processing and machine learning. The system derives both structural and visual characteristics i.e. SSIM scores of templates at template areas, Hough Transform based pattern bleed lines, and area based serial number characteristics. A Random Forest classifier is used to train on the features and classify the notes as the real or the fake ones. The model has a total accuracy of 98 per cent on <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$500 / 2000$</tex> denominations. Precision, recall, and F1-score equal 0.97, 1.00, 0.99 for fake and 1.00, 0.89, 0.94 for real in case of notes of 500. The scores that the fake yield against the real are respectively <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.97,1.00$</tex> and 0.99 and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.00,0.90$</tex> and 0.95 respectively in the case of Rs. 2000 notes. A real-time detection interface that is based on uploading image and classifying the image is created. The presented technique, which is based on edge finding and object identification in a supervised machine, is better than the classical template matching techniques and operates effectively in both controlled and various situations. Besides, the system is light and can run on low resources which ensures its implementation on mobile devices or in the public kiosks. The transparency in the decision making is provided due to the use of interpretable features that makes the user understand why a note has been labeled as genuine or counterfeit. Compared to the previous counterfeit detection methods, this paper proposes an interpretable pattern of feature movement based on the use of SSIM patterns, bleed-line edge density,and serial-number structure, which is lightweight and can achieve real time deployment without deep learning.

title: A Reconfigurable Metasurface for Real-Time Dynamic Control of EM Wave in Microwave Systems
authors: Patinavalasa Megh Sainadh, Saptarshi Ghosh
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: This letter presents a polarization-insensitive reconfigurable metasurface (RMS) that switches among multiple electromagnetic (EM) states using a field-programmable gate array (FPGA), tested in a parallel plate waveguide (PPW). The proposed geometry comprises metallic patterns and p-i-n diodes on the top and bottom of a dielectric substrate, enabling four combinations: <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">off–off, off–on, on–off</small>, and <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">on–on</small>. In the <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">off–off</small> mode, transmission is achieved at 5.07 GHz with an insertion loss of 1.4 dB, whereas an absorption occurs at 5.2 GHz in <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">off–on</small> mode. The reflective behavior is observed in <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">on–off</small> and <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">on–on</small> modes, operating up to 6 and 8 GHz, respectively. The design is analyzed using circuit modeling and evaluated under normal and oblique incidence. A 1-D array is fabricated and integrated with the FPGA to regulate the diodes in different modes. The prototype is measured using the PPW technique, and the experimental results closely align with simulations, demonstrating the effectiveness of the dynamically controlled RMS design in microwave systems.

title: Zero-Shot Low-Light Enhancement via Coupled Retinex-Scattering Frequency Flows
authors: Ayan Sar, Chiranjit Dutta, Tanupriya Choudhury
abstract: The letter presents a new zero-shot low-light image enhancement framework, Retinex-Scattering Frequency Flows (RSFF), which is based on signal processing theory instead of black-box deep learning. RSFF combines a partial differential equation (PDE)-based Retinex decomposition with a multi-scale wavelet-scattering frequency flow to cross-scale propagation of priors in illumination. In contrast to data-intensive supervised networks or computationally expensive diffusion models, RSFF trains physically interpretable operators in a per-image and zero-shot way without paired training examples. We give guarantees of energy conservation and stability of deformation in the scattering domain through theory. Numerous experiments on four benchmarks show that RSFF sets state-of-the-art zero-shot performances at 21.8 dB PSNR on the LOL dataset, outperforming the state-of-the-art zero-shot algorithms by 2.9 dB and matching the state-of-the-art supervised performance, yet RSFF is <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$160 \times$</tex-math></inline-formula> faster than current diffusion-based methods.
