title: Impact of Deep-Level Traps on Carrier Mobility in β -Ga 2 O 3 MOSFETs
authors: O. Maimon, N. A. Moser, P. R. Shrestha, M.-Y. Kim, S.-M. Koo, K. J. Liddy, A. J. Green, K. D. Chabak, Sujitra Pookpanratana, Qiliang Li
abstract: Beta-phase gallium oxide (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\beta $</tex-math> </inline-formula>-Ga<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{{3}}\text {)}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> is a promising semiconductor for high-power and high-frequency electronics due to its ultra-wide bandgap (UWBG) and high breakdown field. However, the carrier mobility in <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\beta $</tex-math> </inline-formula>-Ga<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>-based metal-oxide-semiconductor field-effect transistors (MOSFETs) remains significantly lower than its theoretical limit, often attributed to the presence of deep-level traps. In this work, an electro-optical measurement technique is used to study ionized impurity scattering by deep-level traps in lateral depletion-mode <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\beta $</tex-math> </inline-formula>-Ga<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub> field-effect transistors (FETs). Using the conductance method and photo-assisted capacitance-voltage (PCV) measurements with sub-bandgap illumination from 730 nm (1.7 eV) to 280 nm (4.4 eV), the density of interface trap states (D<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\text {it}}\text {)}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> in <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\beta $</tex-math> </inline-formula>-Ga<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub> FETs was determined between 0.4 and 4.4 eV below the conduction band (E<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\text {C}}\text {)}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\beta $</tex-math> </inline-formula>-Ga<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>. D<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">it</sub> peaks near the band edges, similar to conventional material systems. Based on the results, traps located between E<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">C</sub>–4.0 eV and E<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">C</sub>–3.4 eV are primarily responsible for carrier scattering, reducing the mobility by 50%–75% in these devices with increasing distance from the interface. The increased mobility degradation further from the interface is attributed to traps located in the bulk.

title: Non-Dominated Sorting Dynamic Programming-Based Multi-Objective Driving Path Planning in Closed Parks
authors: Kegang Zhao, Ziqian Huang, Jie Ye, Haiyan Dai, Qing Liu, Chijun Wang, Xiaolin Tang, Yanwei Liu, Wei Zhong
abstract: In increasingly complex parking lot environments, driving path planning needs to balance multiple objectives such as efficiency and safety. Aiming at the problems of difficult weight determination and single solution in traditional weighted sum multi-objective optimization, this paper proposes a multi-objective driving path planning method based on the Non-Dominated Sorting Dynamic Programming (NSDP) algorithm. In grid map path planning, NSDP has more effective solutions compared with other algorithms, providing a wider selection space for path planning. In parking lot path planning, first, the A* algorithm is used to generate path guide points and discretize them. Variable-curvature Dubins curves are adopted to connect state points. After calculating the cost of each optimization objective, NSDP is used to screen optimal solutions. Then, the entropy weight-Topsis method is applied to recommend paths, and finally the conjugate gradient method is used to smooth the paths. The results show that the proposed method is verified in the parking lot scenario: the recommended driving paths effectively balance multiple objectives, and the curvature is second-order continuous.

title: A 22.7–30.6/35.8–43.2GHz Linear Power Amplifier With Transformer-Based Band-Selective Network
authors: Qin Chen, Yue Liang, Depeng Cheng, Haoxiang Wang, Lianming Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: This article presents a frequency-reconfigurable dual-band linear power amplifier (PA) using the transformer-based band-selective network (TFBN) for 5G millimeter-wave (mm-Wave) applications. The TFBN features large frequency shifting, wide instantaneous bandwidth, and enhanced out-of-band rejection ability, but suffers from high design complexity due to a large number of design parameters. To tackle this issue, the frequency-dependent equivalent circuit models of the TFBN are proposed, indicating that the TFBN can be approximated as magnetically coupled resonator (MCR) networks in low-frequency and high-frequency (LF and HF) modes. Leveraging these models, the TFBN reconfigurable frequency range, instantaneous fractional bandwidth (FBW), and gain flatness can be analyzed theoretically, and a TFBN design flow for the PA interstage matching network is provided. To enhance the PA amplitude-to-amplitude (AM-AM) performance, a capacitively coupled cold-FET linearizer is adopted and analyzed with a theoretical model. As a proof-of-concept, a frequency-reconfigurable dual-band PA is implemented in a 65-nm CMOS process, which can support the LF and HF bands of 22.7–30.6and 35.8–43.2GHz, respectively. Across LF/HF bands, the PA can achieve 13.2–14.5/13.3–14.85dBm OP<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1dB</sub>, 15.8–17.2/15.1–16.6dBm P<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">sat</sub> with 17.7%–25.9%/15%–21% peak power-added efficiency (PAE). With 400-MHz 64-QAM signals, the PA can achieve 10.7–11.6/10–11.7dBm average output power with −25dB EVM<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">rms</sub> and the corresponding ACPR of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${\lt }{-}29$</tex-math> </inline-formula>/−27dBc over 24–30/37–44GHz, respectively.

title: Sliding Finite Window Codes: Near-Optimality and Q-Learning for Zero-Delay Coding
authors: Liam Cregg, Fady Alajaji, Serdar Yüksel
abstract: We study the problem of zero-delay coding for the transmission of a Markov source over a noisy channel with feedback and present a reinforcement learning solution which is guaranteed to approach optimality. To this end, we formulate the problem as a Markov decision process (MDP) where the state is a probability-measure valued predictor/belief and the actions are quantizer maps. This MDP formulation has been used to show the optimality of certain classes of encoder policies in prior work, but their computation is prohibitively complex due to the uncountable nature of the constructed state space. Based on recent results for partially observed MDPs, we present an approximation of the belief MDP using a sliding finite window of channel outputs and quantizers. Under an appropriate notion of predictor stability, we show that the lowest distortion achievable by such a sliding finite window policy approaches the true lowest distortion as the window length increases. We give sufficient conditions for predictor stability to hold. Finally, we propose a Q-learning algorithm which provably converges to the optimal policy and provide a detailed comparison of the sliding finite window scheme with another approximation scheme which quantizes the belief MDP in a nearest neighbor fashion, as well as other coding schemes from the literature.

title: Balancing Accuracy-Latency Tradeoff in Hybrid Approximate-Delayed Computing Systems
authors: Panagiotis Charatsaris, Maria Diamanti, Eirini Eleni Tsiropoulou, Symeon Papavassiliou
abstract: Distributed edge computing, empowering Compute First Networking (CFN), can be enhanced with additional computing paradigms to further minimize latency and ameliorate task processing through edge-cloud collaboration. This paper envisions hybrid approximate-delayed computing across the edge-cloud continuum to optimize task processing efficiency for user-owned consumer devices. In this context, we study the joint problem of task offloading to the edge-cloud system and task scheduling at the edge. The users have two offloading options: approximate computing at the edge or delayed but accurate processing in the cloud. The objective is to balance task accuracy and latency among the two computing options. To this end, a fully distributed solution concept is introduced, where the users and the edge system interact hierarchically to determine the task offloading and scheduling, respectively. On the one hand, the users shape their edge-cloud offloading decisions autonomously by participating in a Satisfaction Game between them, targeting to meet a minimum accuracy-latency tradeoff. On the other hand, the edge system optimizes the scheduling of the tasks intended for approximate processing using a Genetic Algorithm. An iterative process between the users and the edge unfolds to conclude the equilibrium point of the hierarchical game between the users and the edge, referred to as SatisfOptimizing Game. Numerical results show the effectiveness of the proposed solution in optimizing resource utilization across the edge-cloud system, guaranteeing user accuracy-latency requirement satisfaction and edge system energy consumption minimization.

title: Patient-Specific In-vivo Dynamic Motion of Ascending Thoracic Aortic Aneurysms from Cine CTA
authors: Rodrigo Valente, André Mourato, Alda Carvalho, José Xavier, Moisés Brito, Stéphane Avril, António Tomás, José Fragata
abstract: Ascending Thoracic Aortic Aneurysm (ATAA) is a potentially fatal cardiovascular condition that may result in dissection or rupture. As a general rule, current guidelines recommend surgical intervention when the aneurysm diameter exceeds a critical threshold, typically around 55 mm in average-risk patients. However, acute complications have been reported in patients with smaller aneurysms, underscoring the need for more accurate and individualised risk stratification criteria. In this work, we propose a novel method to extract the motion of the ATAA from Cine Computed Tomography Angiography cCTA data, enabling the derivation of multiple motion-related variables and the assessment of their clinical significance. This method involves a multi-view 2D U-Net segmentation, followed by an algorithm to calculate nodal deformation between independent segmentations at each time-step of the cardiac cycle. The motion is described generally across the ATAA and then specifically focused on the Aortic Root AR. The analysis was performed on 82 patients and, afterwards, a Principal Component Analysis PCA was performed on 28 metrics to understand which have a relevant impact on inter-patient variability. This showed that mean pressure, weight and centreline length are the most relevant variables to understand patient's variability considering PCA loadings. These results can offer new insights into ATAA biomechanics, supporting the development of future risk stratification models and clinical decision-making.

title: Drift-Resilient Hybrid Feature Learning Framework for Accurate Mixed Industrial Gas Classification Using Chemical Sensor Arrays
authors: Ghazala Ansari, Rupali Singh, Sachin Kumar, Ravi Kumar
abstract: The increased demand for reliable gas sensing in factory systems has prompted the development of smart sensory systems capable of overcoming errors and signal distortion caused by environmental changes. This paper introduces drift-resistant hybrid feature learning to classify mixed industrial gases, specifically the ethylene-carbon monoxide (CH-CO) and ethylene-methane (CH-CH) mixtures with the help of metal-oxide semiconductor (MOX) sensor arrays. The suggested framework combines both the statistical descriptors, which include the steady-state signal behavior, and the deep temporal-spatial embedding, which is trained by a one-dimensional CNN-GRU architecture. To generate these complimentary representations, a single feature space is produced, which is more interpretable and resilient. Optuna was used to conduct Bayesian search on the models hyperparameters and Extra Trees and LightGBM ensembles were used to conduct classification. The results of the experiments carried out on 180 time-series recordings with labels proved that the fused model attained average accuracies of more than 90% and best fold accuracies of 99% and surpassed a number of advanced machine learning and deep learning baselines. Synthetic baseline drift (0.8-1.4) was also used to test long-term reliability and the framework retained accuracies greater than 96% at all levels of drift. Shapley additive explanations (SHAP) also shed some light by determining kurtosis, skewness, and mid-frequency temporal response as the main discriminative sensor channels. The findings verify that the suggested framework provides a computationally efficient, interpretable and drift-resilient solution that can be used in real-time in an industrial context. In addition to gas analysis, the hybrid approach demonstrates a high potential of more general applications related to non-stationary multivariate sensor data, that is, environmental monitoring, biomedical diagnostics, and process control systems.

title: SWMT: A Sliding Window Merkle Tree with Delayed Writes for Scalable Blockchain State Management
authors: Nianzu Sheng, Tong Zhou, He Zhao, Xiaofeng Li, Jinlin Xu
publish: Proceedings of the ACM on Management of Data
abstract: None

title: Regulatory Contextual Integrity: Adaptively Regulating Privacy as Contextual Integrity
authors: Sebastian Benthall, Ido Sivan-Sevilla
abstract: Data economy harms are legitimized by flawed regulatory paradigms for privacy and slow responses to privacy violations. We propose an adaptive regulatory system based on contextual integrity with three learning cycles: designing regulations, real-time monitoring and enforcement, and ongoing evaluation.

title: Automated Human Activity Recognition Using Multiple Contactless Ultra-Wideband Radar Signals and Time–Frequency Representations
authors: Swarubini P J, Nagarajan Ganapathy
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Radar signals-based human activity recognition (HAR) is a promising contactless approach for healthcare. However, these signals are highly nonstationary, exhibiting short-duration and dynamic variations that are difficult to discriminate. In recent studies, time–frequency representations (TFRs) are employed to capture both temporal and spatial information. Although TFRs are employed to capture the temporal-spatial dynamics of signals, TFR-based radar micro-Doppler signatures are less explored. In addition, TFR-based machine learning methods can provide improved feature learning. In this study, we attempt to analyze high-resolution TFRs namely, mel-spectrogram (Mel), smoothed pseudo Wigner–Ville distribution (SPWVD), short-time Fourier transform (STFT) and superlet transform (SLT) to explore the dynamics of radar signals for HAR. For this, a publicly available radar database (N=15 subjects) consisting of nine different activities are considered. The radar signals are preprocessed and segmented according to the nine activities. These radar segments are subjected to STFT, Mel, SPWVD and SLT. Ten features are extracted from each TFR and employed to various classification algorithms, namely support vector machine (SVM), k-nearest neighbours (kNN), decision tree (DT), random forest (RF), logistic regression (LR), XGBoost (XGB), AdaBoost (ADB), light gradient boosting machine (LGBM), CatBoost (CatB), and MobileNetV2. Extensive experiments are conducted for five-activity (stationary, translation, in-place movements, fall, standing up) and three-activity recognition tasks (mobility, in-place and fall) and evaluation metrics are obtained. The results show that SPWVD-based TFR performed best among all the TFRs. The combination of SPWVD-MobileNetV2 provides the highest overall effectiveness, obtaining an F1-macro (83.90%) and balanced accuracy (84.60%) for the three-class task and 69.09% and 70.15% for five-class task. The SPWVD–CatB combination achieved comparable performance to deep learning, with an F1-macro of 79.76% and a Matthews correlation coefficient of 66.53% for the three-class task. The findings demonstrate that SPWVD, combined with MobileNetV2, yields optimal performance across multiple activity classes suitable for deployment on mobile edge devices. These results indicate that the proposed approach could be extended to clinical and real-world monitoring.

title: Unsupervised False-Alarm-Controllable Change Detection in Heterogeneous Remote Sensing Images Based on Copula Theory
authors: Weiming Li, ChengYang Zhang, Xueqian Wang, Gang Li, Pramod K. Varshney
abstract: Change detection (CD) in heterogeneous remote sensing images plays a crucial role in earth observation tasks, such as disaster monitoring and destruction assessment. Recent advancements in heterogeneous CD studies have substantially enhanced the capability to detect changes, but existing methodologies frequently lack effective control mechanisms for increasing false alarms when facing different heterogeneous scenes. Consequently, even with a high detection rate for changes, the real changes co-exist with lots of false alarms, thereby reducing the reliability and practical utility of the CD results. To address this issue, inspired by the insight of adaptive thresholding for false alarm control in constant false alarm rate (CFAR) detection, we propose a copula theory-based CD framework, named FAR-Aware-Copula-CD, to control false alarm rate (FAR) in heterogeneous CD. In the proposed FAR-Aware-Copula-CD, the heterogeneous CD problem is represented as a binary hypothesis testing problem. Then, the binary hypothesis testing problem is solved by a generalized likelihood ratio test based on copula theory, which effectively characterizes change statistics based on superpixel-level dependence within various heterogeneous image pairs. Finally, the decision thresholds of the copula-based change statistics are determined so as to satisfy the FAR constraint and ensure that the final CD result approaches a prespecified false alarm rate. Our FAR-Aware-Copula-CD provides a new approach for implementing controllable false alarms in heterogeneous CD tasks. Experimental results on four real-world datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed method.

title: Ku-band Tile-based Metal-only Mechanically Controlled Two-dimensional Beam-Scanning Circularly Polarized Reflectarray
authors: Yi-An Lin, Zhi-An Zhou, Tzyh-Ghuang Ma
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: This communication presents a Ku-band metal-only, mechanically controlled, two-dimensional beam-scanning circularly polarized reflectarray (CPRA) based on tile-based unit cells (UCs). Each UC (or tile), laser-cut on a piece of stainless steel, is designed with two pairs of orthogonally placed semi-annular slots to ensure stable reflection response under oblique incidence. A metal frame with high mechanical strength is designed to secure all tiles with plastic screws, while the rotation of the UCs for phase compensation can be conveniently controlled with a pair of tweezers. The experimental results demonstrate that the CPRA, centered at 15 GHz, provides a broadside peak gain of 29.8 dBic, an aperture efficiency of 52.3%, and 1-dB/3-dB bandwidths of 12.7% and 19.3%. The main beam can be steered two-dimensionally to 50o and 60o with a scanning loss of 2.4 and 4.8 dB, respectively. A comparison table is provided to highlight the uniqueness and advantages of the proposed design over existing state-of-the-art counterparts.

title: A 0.4V 1.9-nW Bulk-Driven Cascode OTA Achieving 75-dB DC Gain With Replica-Bias Assisted PVT Robustness
authors: Riccardo Della Sala, Giovanni Nicolini, Alessandro Trifiletti, Giuseppe Scotti
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers
abstract: This work presents a bulk-driven operational transconductance amplifier (OTA) designed to operate from a 400mV supply while maintaining nanowatt-level power consumption. The proposed single-stage cascode topology achieves a differential gain close to 75dB, combining a body-driven input pair with output-resistance enhancement through a positive-feedback mechanism. Areplica-bias loop is incorporated to improve robustness to PVT variations without increasing current consumption. The OTA was implemented in a 180nm TSMC CMOS technology, and post-layout simulations demonstrate strong resilience to PVT and device mismatch variations. Afabricated prototype was experimentally characterized, showing a measured DC gain consistent with simulations, a CMRR of 44.5dB and PSRR of 42.5dB at 1Hz, and a voltage offset of approximately 4.5mV, while consuming 1.9 nW of power. Time-domain tests further confirm the OTA’s dynamic performance, including a measured slew rate of 1.02 V/ms for a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$200~\mathrm {mV_{pp}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> step input. Overall, the results validate the proposed architecture as an energy-efficient and compact solution suitable for ultra-low-power analog front-ends in biomedical and autonomous sensing applications.

title: A Novel PID Design Method via Model-Based Reinforcement Learning Algorithms
authors: Hozefa Jesawada, Amol Yerudkar, Yang Liu, Navdeep Singh, Carmen Del Vecchio
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: This paper introduces a novel framework that bridges advanced reinforcement learning (RL) with traditional PID control by converting model-based RL policies into interpretable PID gains. By combining inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) with Kullback–Leibler divergence minimization, our method aligns sophisticated control strategies with the simplicity and robustness of PID controllers. In doing so, the proposed approach maintains the transparency and simplicity of PID controllers while incorporating the adaptability, data-driven optimization, and long-horizon planning capabilities of RL. Compatible with both model-based and model-free RL algorithms, the approach has been validated through extensive simulations on benchmark systems and real-world experiments on the Robotarium platform, demonstrating resilience against disturbances, parameter uncertainties, and noise. By blending the strengths of reinforcement learning with the practical familiarity of PID control, the proposed framework offers a data-efficient, scalable, and transparent solution for enhancing PID controller design in complex and dynamic environments.

title: MUFNet: A Mamba-Based Uncertainty-Aware Fusion Framework for Fine-Grained Satellite Hyperspectral-LiDAR Tree Species Mapping
authors: Xueqin He, Kaijian Xu, Shuzhou Wang, Ping Zhao, Yuqi Zhang, Juanjuan Bi
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Satellite hyperspectral imagery (HSI) fused with LiDAR enables large-area, fine-grained tree-species mapping in subtropical forests. However, current satellite HSI-LiDAR fusion remains limited because non-task-oriented spectral reduction may weaken discriminative narrowband and inter-band cues, while weakly coupled fusion cannot adequately address cross-modal inconsistency in satellite observations. To address these issues, we propose MUFNet, a mamba-based uncertainty-aware fusion framework that integrates multi-stage cross-modal interactions with uncertainty-guided prediction aggregation under end-to-end task-driven representation learning. We validate MUFNet in a representative subtropical region and benchmark it against six state-of-the-art (SOTA) models. Experimental results demonstrate that MUFNet achieves the best mapping performance, with an overall accuracy (kappa) of 93.34% (91.17%). Moreover, MUFNet significantly outperforms all competing methods, improving overall accuracy (kappa) by 2.85%–11.45% (6.45%–15.49%). In addition, MUFNet exhibits good predictive reliability, achieving an ECE of 0.0073, and maintains a favorable performance–efficiency trade-off with only 0.400 M parameters and 0.086 GFLOPs. Ablation studies further confirm the complementarity of the three fusion stages and the contributions of the proposed task-driven representation learning, cross-modal interaction, and uncertainty-aware routing components. This work provides a transferable technical pathway for fine-grained tree species mapping under multi-source remote sensing, offering methodological support for biodiversity conservation and forest resource management.

title: QIMarker: Can Watermark Embedding Improve Image Quality?
authors: Chuan Qin, Zixiang Wei, Na Wang, Fang Cao, Ching-Chun Chang, Xinpeng Zhang, Chin-Chen Chang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Robust image watermarking plays a crucial role in copyright protection and digital content authentication. However, most existing methods inevitably introduce visual artifacts and structural distortions, leading to noticeable degradation in image quality. To address these limitations, this paper introduces QIMarker, an image enhancement–guided multi-task learning framework for robust image watermarking. Unlike existing methods that minimize distortion with respect to the original image, QIMarker redefines the optimization objective by aligning the watermarked image with its enhanced counterpart, thereby transforming watermark embedding from a quality-degrading process into a quality-promoting one. Specifically, it integrates a U-Net–based embedding network enhanced with attention mechanisms and a dedicated image enhancement branch for joint feature optimization. This design allows the watermark embedding to contribute positively to image reconstruction, yielding perceptually refined outcomes while maintaining high robustness. Extensive experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of QIMarker in both visual quality and robustness. It improves structural similarity (SSIM) by up to 0.13 compared with existing methods, and achieves watermark extraction accuracy above 97% under common attacks such as noise addition, compression, scaling, and rotation.

title: Engineering AIOps Controllers for High-Performance Software Operations: an Action Design Research Study
authors: Fernando Piris Font, Stefano Fossati, Marco Tonnarelli, Damian A. Tamburri, Roel Engelen
abstract: Efficient memory allocation is critical for controlling and optimizing High-Performance Computing (HPC) infrastructures. This action design research study aims to identify the best principles, practices, and patterns for improving the precision and efficiency of control and optimization mechanisms in large-scale HPC infrastructure. To achieve this, we explore the use of Artificial Intelligence Operations (AIOps), which applies artificial intelligence to manage software infrastructure operations. In this context, we propose Job Prophet (JP), a machine-learning-based application. JP is designed to regulate and continuously optimize HPC resource management, enhancing operational efficiency and performance. The tool integrates continuous training, inference, monitoring, and CI/CD pipelines, ensuring adaptability to dynamic workloads. By leveraging historical execution data from heterogeneous sources, JP enhances scheduling efficiency while maintaining model reliability through continuous retraining and monitoring. We evaluate JP on real-world HPC workloads, demonstrating significant improvements in resource utilization and job performance. Our findings highlight the benefits of AI-driven memory allocation and provide insights into scalable, automated machine-learning integration within HPC environments.

title: Inverse-Designed Broadband and Compact $TE\_{N}$ to $TE\_{0}$ Mode Converters via Optimized Bezier Curves
authors: Ziyin Wei, J. Stewart Aitchison
abstract: We present a scalable design strategy for compact, broadband mode converters capable of transforming arbitrary high-order transverse electric (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$TE\_{N}$</tex-math></inline-formula>) modes into the fundamental <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$TE\_{0}$</tex-math></inline-formula> mode on a silicon-on-insulator platform. The architecture employs a “split-phase-combine” mechanism: the higher-order mode is spatially separated into fundamental mode components, phase-synchronized, and subsequently recombined. The device comprises a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1\times (N+1)$</tex-math></inline-formula> input splitter, an adiabatic phase shifter, and a multimode interference (MMI) combiner. By parametrizing the device geometry with optimized cubic Bezier curves, we achieve a compact footprint with large operating bandwidth, while effectively maintain low losses. We validate the scalability of this approach by designing converters for <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$TE\_{1}$</tex-math></inline-formula>, <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$TE\_{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula>, and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$TE\_{3}$</tex-math></inline-formula> modes. The optimized devices exhibit mode conversion efficiencies of 98.3%, 95.8%, and 86.8%, respectively, across a broad 100nm bandwidth (1500–1600nm). Tolerance analysis further confirms the robustness of the designs against systematic fabrication deviations of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\pm 10$</tex-math></inline-formula> nm, suggesting their suitability for high-density mode-division multiplexing in next-generation optical interconnects.

title: Sensitivity at the Edges of the Finger Enables Mixed Reality Force Feedback
authors: Jung-Hwan Youn, Yanjun Chen, Ali Israr, Craig Shultz
abstract: We introduce a new paradigm for rendering highforce sensations by stimulating the edges of the fingerpad rather than the central contact surface. This non-blocking approach preserves natural finger use, enabling simultaneous physical and virtual interactions. To investigate its feasibility, we proposed a mechanotransduction mechanism and conducted biomechanical and psychophysical studies. Our results show that the fingerpad edges are nearly as sensitive as the center under low forces, become roughly 117% more sensitive under moderate forces, and maintain sensitivity at higher forces. The edges also exhibit increased stiffness and tolerate higher forces before discomfort. Furthermore, the left edge exhibited approximately 143% higher sensitivity than the right edge in the right hand. Using these insights, we built a compact wearable device that provides multiDOF force feedback without blocking the fingerpad and demonstrated its use in VR/AR scenarios. These findings support edgebased stimulation as a viable approach for high-force, scalable haptic feedback in mixed reality and teleoperation systems

title: Two Phase Multi-Task Learning for Cybersickness Prediction and Adaptive Reduction
authors: A E M Ridwan, Purnota Saha, John Quarles, Rifatul Islam
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Cybersickness, a motion sickness like discomfort, is a major barrier to the usability of virtual reality (VR) systems. While prior work has focused mainly on predicting cybersickness severity, practical mitigation requires not only detecting how sick a user feels but also deciding whether a countermeasure is beneficial and determining its appropriate intensity. In this paper, we propose a two phase multitask learning framework that jointly models cybersickness severity, blur effectiveness, and blur intensity. In Phase 1, we pretrain temporal deep learning backbones on two single label datasets with only severity annotations. In Phase 2, we pro gressively finetune the models on a multi-label dataset containing severity, blur effectiveness, and blur level labels. We evaluate three backbone architectures a Time-Series Transformer, Deep Temporal Convolutional Network, and TS-Mamba under a 10-fold block aware cross validation scheme. Results show that two phase training significantly outperforms single phase baselines, with the Time Series Transformer achieving best performance (FMS MAE = 0.57, R² = 0.87; Blur Level MAE = 0.49, R² = 0.95; Blur Preference ACC = 99.5%). Unlike prior rule based reduction frameworks that rely on static heuristics, our approach provides a data-driven “detect–decide–dose” pipeline that adapts blur mitigation dynamically to individual users. This demonstrates that single label pre training is an effective strategy for developing multitask VR safety models under limited labeled data.To our knowledge, this is the first framework that unifies cybersickness prediction and adaptive reduction in a single model.

title: GenTouchVR: Generating a Touchable Virtual Reality Environment from a Single Image
authors: Jaejun Park, Soyeon Nam, Jeongwoo Kim, Uison Ju, Seungmoon Choi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Creating interactive VR content typically requires extensive modeling effort, making scalable production difficult. We present an automated computational pipeline that converts a single image into an interactive 3D scene with plausible kinesthetic feedback. Our approach leverages a Large Language Model (LLM) to detect objects in the image and infer haptic properties from visual and textual cues. These extracted results are then utilized to synthesize 3D models and optimize haptic properties for perceptual distinguishability, while combining them into a complete interactive environment. A user study shows that the generated VR scenes provide compelling visuo-haptic experiences, highlighting the potential of our method for scalable multisensory world generation. To our knowledge, this is the first system to automatically produce VR scenes with force feedback from a single image, pointing toward a practical direction for lowering the barriers to creating haptically enriched VR content.

title: Advertisement
authors: N/A

title: Monitoring the Long-term VIIRS Calibration Performance at the NIR and SWIR Bands for Ocean Color Remote Sensing
authors: Menghua Wang, Wei Shi, Lide Jiang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: In this study, we propose a new technique to monitor and evaluate the performance of the on-orbit radiometric calibration for the Visible Infrared Imaging Radiometer Suite (VIIRS) onboard the Suomi National Polar-orbiting Partnership (SNPP) for deriving global ocean color (OC) products. Based on the fact that the open ocean is black (i.e., no normalized water-leaving radiance nLw(λ) contributions) at the near-infrared (NIR) and shortwave infrared (SWIR) bands, and atmosphere and ocean conditions in the central regions of ocean gyres are generally clear and stable with the stable interannual and decadal variability, we compute the Rayleigh-corrected reflectance ρrc(λ) and its de-cycled reflectance Δρrc(λ) from VIIRS-SNPP, and use them to evaluate the best ocean regions to monitor the long-term sensor calibration performance at the NIR and SWIR bands. Of the six studied ocean locations in the Marine Optical Bouy (MOBY) in Hawaii, South Pacific Gyre (SPG), North Pacific Gyre (NPG), South Atlantic Gyre (SAG), North Atlantic Gyre (NAG), and Indian Ocean Gyre (IOG), the SPG is the most optimal site for the least trending of ρrc(λ) and Δρrc(λ) with significantly low interannual variations, and nearly flat trending due to its relative long-term stability for atmosphere properties. The slopes of the trending (2012–2024) from VIIRS-SNPP-measured Δρrc(λ) are 0.42%, 0.10%, 0.21%, 0.32%, and 0.37% per year at its spectral bands of 745 nm, 862 nm, 1238 nm, 1601 nm, and 2258 nm, respectively. On the other hand, significantly downward Δρrc(λ) trending at the MOBY site and strong interannual Δρrc(λ) variations at the IOG site are attributed to the multi-year aerosol optical thickness (AOT) variations at these sites. From VIIRS-SNPP-measured ρrc(λ) and Δρrc(λ) at the SPG in 2012–2024, we show that the sensor has been well calibrated for both the NIR and SWIR bands, as well as for the visible bands. Stable and high-quality Level-1B data from other two VIIRS sensors on the NOAA-20 and NOAA-21 satellites are also evaluated. Results demonstrate that, with well radiometric calibrations, highly consistent mission-long OC products at the SPG site can be derived for three VIIRS sensors.

title: Hardware implementation of configurable chromatic dispersion model for real-time optical fiber communication simulation systems
authors: Jing Jiang, Chen Xie, Ming Chen
abstract: Compared to conventional experimental setups, hardware-based real-time optical fiber simulation significantly reduces verification costs by digitally modeling optoelectronic devices while enabling dynamic parameter adjustments, providing exceptional flexibility and scalability. Moreover, real-time simulation outperforms traditional software-based methods in speed, serving as a more efficient platform for comprehensive digital signal processing algorithm validation. Optical fiber, as the core component of fiber-optic communication systems, exhibits chromatic dispersion (CD) in direct detection optical transmission systems, causing frequency-selective power attenuation that severely degrades the received signal performance. Precise CD modeling is thus critical for high-fidelity optical channel emulation. In this paper, we propose a field programmable gate array (FPGA)-based online configurable hardware CD model implementation scheme enabled by block convolution approaches. A 40-GSa/s CD model with 128 parallel input/output channels is implemented in a single FPGA chip, and its parameters can be adjusted via the joint test action group interface. The performance of the CD model is investigated in a Matlab and FPGA co-simulation platform, where the intensity-modulated optical orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM) signal is offline generated for the input of the hardware CD model, and the output of the CD model is fed into a direct-detection OFDM receiver emulated by Matlab. The results show that the proposed hardware CD models with overlap-add/overlap-save methods can achieve a deviation of 1×10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-2</sup> in terms of normalized root-mean-square error over more than 800 km of standard single-mode fiber transmission, compared to one equivalently implemented with a full-length fast Fourier transform-based circular convolution method with a sufficient-length impulse response sequence using Matlab. Also, similar on-chip resource overheads are observed for the proposed two hardware CD models. Moreover, similar bit error rate performance is achieved in the OFDM systems supported by the hardware and software CD models.

title: Ultra-High Sensitivity Fiber Optic Microphone Based on Geometry-Induced Mechanical Compliance
authors: Xuzhe Zhang, Yuan Gao, Huanmo Zhou, Yangyang Wan, Xuhui Yu, Zuyuan He
abstract: Mandrel-based fiber optic microphones (FOMs) provide an effective approach to enhancing acoustic detection sensitivity. However, their high sensitivity often depends on thinner walls and softer materials, which limits their reliability in practical applications. To overcome this limitation, this work presents a new method, introducing the concept of geometryinduced mechanical compliance in FOM design and proposing a corrugated-structure FOM with ultra-high sensitivity. The proposed FOM achieves an average sensitivity of −119.15 dB re 1 rad/μPa/m over 0.1–4.5 kHz and a peak sensitivity of −105.49 dB re 1 rad/μPa/m at 1.88 kHz. Compared with conventional FOMs fabricated with the same material, wall thickness, and outer radius, the sensitivities are improved by approximately 18.35 dB and 21.35 dB, respectively. Furthermore, it outperforms recently reported state-of-the-art FOMs by approximately 10 dB in average sensitivity and 18 dB in peak sensitivity. Experimental results further substantiate that the FOM exhibits excellent performance in practical acoustic signal detection and can clearly capture acoustic features. The results demonstrate its strong potential for fiber optic acoustic sensing applications and provide a new design concept for next-generation ultra-high sensitivity FOMs.

title: Towards Robot Skill Learning and Adaptation with Gaussian Processes
authors: A K M Nadimul Haque, Fouad Sukkar, Sheila Sutjipto, Cedric Le Gentil, Marc G. Carmichael, Teresa Vidal-Calleja
abstract: General robot skill adaptation requires expressive representations robust to varying task configurations. While recent learning-based skill adaptation methods refined via Reinforcement learning (RL) have shown success, existing skill models often lack sufficient representational capacity for anything beyond minor environmental changes. In contrast, Gaussian process (GP)-based skill modelling provides an expressive representation with useful analytical properties; however, adaptation of GP-based skills remains underexplored. This paper proposes a novel, robust skill adaptation framework that utilises GPs with sparse via-points for compact and expressive modelling. The model leverages the demonstrated trajectory's first and second analytical derivatives to preserve the skill's kinematic profile. We present three adaptation methods to cater for the variability between initial and observed configurations. Firstly, an optimisation agent that adjusts the path's via-points while preserving the demonstration velocity. Second, a behaviour cloning agent trained to replicate output trajectories from the optimisation agent. Lastly, an RL agent that has learnt to modify via-points whilst maintaining the kinematic profile and faster feed-forward adaptation. Evaluated across three tasks (drawer opening, cube-pushing and bar manipulation) in both simulation and hardware, our proposed methods outperform every benchmark in success rates. Furthermore, the results demonstrate that the GP-based representation enables all three methods to attain high cosine similarity and low velocity magnitude errors, indicating strong preservation of the kinematic profile. Overall, our formulation provides a compact representation capable of adapting to large deviations from a single demonstrated skill.

title: Misconception-Aware Prompting Can Generate Authentic Analytical Multiple-Choice Questions
authors: Machi Shimmei, Masaki Uto, Yuichiroh Matsubayashi, Kentaro Inui, Aditi Mallavarapu, Noboru Matsuda
abstract: Although automatic multiple-choice question (MCQ) generation has been extensively studied, generating MCQs that require deep reasoning remains underexplored. This study aims to develop a framework for automatically constructing Analytical Multiple-Choice Questions (Analytical MCQs) using a pre-trained large language model. Analytical MCQs present sentence-level assertions as choice items and are designed to evaluate students’ higher-order reasoning about complex subject matter. One of the main challenges in this task lies in generating plausible yet incorrect assertions (i.e., distractors) that reflect students’ misunderstandings. To address this challenge, we utilize students’ written responses to open-ended questions, which offer insights into their conceptual understanding of a topic. Building on this foundation, we introduce AnaQuest, a prompting technique that integrates these open-ended responses into the generation of Analytical MCQs. AnaQuest analyzes these responses, identifies common errors, and generates incorrect assertions alongside correct ones. We evaluated the quality of AI-generated assertions by comparing their Item Response Theory (IRT) parameters, collected from exams where the generated questions were used, with those of certified human-crafted assertions. The analysis showed that assertions generated by AnaQuest more closely resembled human-crafted ones in both discrimination power and difficulty level, compared to those generated by a baseline ChatGPT model that does not incorporate student misunderstandings. To our knowledge, this is the first study to demonstrate the importance of using knowledge of students’ misconceptions to generate high-quality Multiple-Choice questions.

title: Intelligent Bank Customer Churn Prediction: An AI Ensemble Approach Using Neural Networks and TabPFN Foundation Models
authors: Nauman Pathan, Vishwajeet Patil, Ayushi Pahadia, Smita Bharne
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
abstract: Banking institutions need customer churn prediction to ensure their profits remain secure and to increase customer retention. The proposed paper introduces a new AI ensemble method that integrates Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs) with TabPFN base models to improve customer churn prediction in banks. We use a domain-specific learning approach that leverages the potential of a custom neural network, combined with the novel and highly effective generalization capabilities of TabPFN, a foundation model for tabular data introduced in recent work. With an ensemble system, the accuracy on the standard bank customer churn data stands at 91.3%, whereas with a single model method, the accuracy is at 68.8%. The system comprises processes for large-scale data preprocessing, weighted ensemble methods, and a professional dashboard for real-time analytics. The experimental findings demonstrate better performance than current techniques, with practical results of this achievement evident in banking customer retention processes.

title: Honest Teacher Knowledge Distillation: A Framework for Uncertainty-Aware Model Compression Using Gaussian Processes
authors: Arjay R. Alba, Jocelyn F. Villaverde
abstract: We introduce Honest Teacher Knowledge Distillation (HTKD), an uncertainty-aware distillation framework that transfers both knowledge and confidence from a teacher to a compact student by integrating Gaussian Process (GP)–based predictive uncertainty into the learning objective. Unlike conventional knowledge distillation that treats teacher outputs as uniformly reliable, HTKD explicitly models uncertainty over the teacher’s feature representations and logits, and adaptively down-weights ambiguous supervision. This enables reliability-aware knowledge transfer even in fully supervised settings, where teachers remain imperfect on visually ambiguous or rare samples. HTKD is evaluated using a ResNet-101 → ResNet-18 teacher–student pair across datasets of varying scale—MS COCO, PASCAL VOC, and Oxford-IIIT Pet—using detection accuracy and calibration metrics. HTKD improves mAP over the non-distilled student by +4.3 on COCO, +4.5 on VOC, and +4.8 on Oxford-IIIT Pet, while achieving the lowest Expected Calibration Error and Brier Score across all datasets. A progressive loss-function ablation confirms monotonic gains from feature-only KD to the full GP-weighted formulation, along with faster and more stable convergence. Robustness is further assessed under Gaussian and motion blur corruptions, where HTKD degrades significantly slower than single-teacher KD and achieves trends comparable to multi-teacher KD, while retaining a single-teacher training cost. These results demonstrate that uncertainty-aware distillation enhances accuracy, calibration, and robustness, making HTKD suitable for reliable edge and real-world deployment.

title: Collaborative SOH Estimation for Lithium-ion Batteries by Integrating both Charging and Discharging Profiles
authors: Zhijun Liu, Fu Jiang, Yue Wu, Weirong Liu, Heng Li
abstract: Accurate estimation of the state of health (SOH) for lithium-ion batteries is crucial to ensure the safe and stable operation of electric vehicles. Existing studies have mostly relied solely on either charging or discharging data, with few attempts to integrate both charging and discharging data. This reliance limits the models’ generalization capability, making it difficult for them to adapt to the complex and variable operating conditions encountered in the real world. To address this limitation, this paper proposes a multi-domain collaborative learning method for battery SOH estimation by integrating both charging and discharging domains. First, a parallel feature extraction network is employed to extract battery aging features from the charging and discharging domains, respectively. Second, the maximum mean discrepancy method is introduced to reduce feature distribution discrepancies between charging and discharging domains, thereby alleviating the feature drift problem caused by data distribution differences. Finally, a cross-domain feature fusion mechanism is designed to dynamically adjust the feature weights from different domains. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves outstanding performance in real-world scenarios, with an R2 reaching 0.9141. Compared to methods relying solely on the charging domain or discharging domain, the R2 value is improved by 14.4% and 122.6%, respectively. Moreover, the MAE and RMSE are as low as 0.0191 and 0.0248.

title: A Robust Model Predictive Current Control Using Cascaded Structure of Noise-Resilient Augmented Observer with Adaptive Data-Driven Iterative Harmonic Identification for PMSM Drives
authors: Jiaxing Ye, Mingyi Wang, Shun Cai, Xing Liu, Ruiqi Xu, Zifeng Chen, Yuwen Xu, Zhongyao Chen, Zhong Gao, Chengming Zhang, Liyi Li
abstract: A robust model predictive current control (MPCC) strategy is proposed for motor drive systems, in which a cascaded architecture is innovatively constructed by integrating a noise-resilient augmented observer (NAO) with an adaptive data-driven iterative harmonic identification (ADIHI) mechanism. Firstly, to address dominant aperiodic disturbances in the ultra-local motor model, an enhanced NAO is developed, where the integral of noisy measured current is deliberately introduced as an augmented state variable. This novel augmented state effectively decouples the adverse interaction between high observer gains and high-frequency current measurement noise, enabling inherent low-pass filtering properties of the integrator operator and significantly improving noise immunity without sacrificing the disturbances estimation accuracy. Secondly, based on the NAO-estimated disturbances and ultra-local model, a high-pass-filtered harmonic internal-model component is extracted and further refined through proposed ADIHI scheme. By incorporating an adaptive forgetting factor and an iterative identification mechanism, ADIHI achieves accurate online harmonics extraction. Moreover, an adaptive forgetting factor updating strategy driven by the variation rate of harmonic identification error is designed to enhance robustness against operating condition variations. Finally, overall closed-loop system stability of the proposed MPCC framework is rigorously established using the Nyquist stability criterion. Explicit analytical expressions for control parameters are derived under stability constraints, and systematic stability regions and parameters tuning guidelines are provided. Extensive experimental results on a motor test bench demonstrate the effectiveness and superiority of proposed current control strategy.

title: A two-stage method for aircraft engine fault detection based on change point
authors: Junying Hu, Huan Xu, Ke Zhang
abstract: Complicated non-linear relationships exist among the subsystems of an aircraft engine. For a large amount of signals collected by monitoring points in a short time, the traditional fault detection method cannot efficiently complete the task. On one hand, the intricate coupling relationships among signals complicate the analysis. On the other hand, individually examining each signal is not only time-consuming but also inefficient. Taking the aircraft engine as the research object, a two-stage method for aircraft engine fault detection based on change point is proposed, posing a formidable challenge to fault detection efficiency. First, in the feature extraction stage, locally linear embedding (LLE) method is used to extract the features of the data. Then, the fault points are found based on the maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) between kernel embeddings of sample distributions in the recognition stage. To demonstrate the performance of the two-stage method for fault detection based on change point, experiments on a real case study of aircraft engine fault detection are conducted. The results on real data sets show that the two-stage method for fault detection based on change point has high accuracy.

title: Retraction Notice: Lifetime Enhancement Using Sleep Switch Over In Wireless Sensor Networks
authors: A. Karthikeyan, M. Aravinth, K.S. Hamshini, M. Indumathi
publish: 2019 5th International Conference on Advanced Computing & Communication Systems (ICACCS)
abstract: Expanding the endurance of the system and using the equity to its greatest utmost is the serious issue in Wireless Sensor Network (WSN). The remote sensor hubs in sensor establish are fueled utilizing battery-mechanized batteries. Nonetheless, giving vitality to hubs in the inaccessible condition is a noteworthy issue in WSN. Consequently WSN needs another vitality effective calculation to upgrade the system existence. In a sensor hub, the sending and accepting module expends more vitality when contrasted with different modules. In this paper, a Battery Restoration based Lifetime Endurance (BRLE) calculation is talked about, which considers battery voltage bend for planning the sending and receiving module of the sensor hubs.The Markov display assist in deciding the condition of the sensor hub as Cluster head and Cluster Member dependent on battery recuperation process. By booking the sending and receiving module dependent on the battery terminal voltage, recuperation factor and separation between the hubs, the lifetime of the system is upgraded.Trial execution demonstrate that the calculation beats the others by expanded lifetime and expanded throughput. The BRLE diminishes the difficulty and vitality gap issue, keeping away from misfortune in network with the sink.

title: Retraction Notice: Energy Optimized Node Level Trust Based Data Transaction in Wireless Sensor Network
authors: A. Menaka, R. Jagadish, M. Murali, R. Bharathwaj, G. Abinesh
publish: 2020 6th International Conference on Advanced Computing and Communication Systems (ICACCS)
abstract: Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN), is widespread used by various fields due to its advantages and application. The major issue focused in a network to increase its performance is energy consumption and secure data transaction without any loss. In our proposed work, secure data transaction is focused through analyzing node trust. The challenge-response model promotes a focus on assessing trust for oneself; And linking it to its sub-center using the proposed self-survey and self-affirmation calculations, respectively. Hence node level trust is evaluated through self scrutiny and self attestation. Self scrutiny is analyzing every nodes self integrity level and self attestation evaluates the trust level of nodes that are included in transaction. In addition to this energy optimization has been implemented, to obtain this Energy Efficient Sensor Routing (EESR) protocol is implemented. This algorithm considers minimum path selection, energy utilization and node trust. To increase the performance based on energy factor, sleep and a wake approach has been used. Compared to existing method our proposed work attains maximum performance in both data delivery and network lifetime parameters.

title: Retraction Notice: Implementation of Image Enhancement and Image Segmentation In Disease Diagnosis (Lung Cancer)
authors: P A Aisiri, B Ramya, D J Chaithanya, S Anitha
publish: 2021 7th International Conference on Advanced Computing and Communication Systems (ICACCS)
abstract: Recently, image processing structures have been routinely used in a few clinical areas for improving prior different validation and therapy thresholds, particularly in different mischief tumours, such as cell breakdown in the lungs, where the time factor is critical to identify the affliction in the patient as quickly as possible. Cell breakdown in the lungs has been attracting the thought of clinical and sciatic organizations in the latest years because of its high inescapability lined up with the inconvenient treatment. Estimations from 2008 show that cell breakdown in the lungs, all through world, is the one that attacks the best number of people. Early disclosure of cell breakdown in the lungs is critical for productive treatment. There are very few methods open to recognize harmful cells. Here procedure for division, for instance, watershed is used to recognize the infection cell and to find better approach out of them.

title: Graph-Based Representation Learning with Beta Uncertainty for Enhanced Multimodal Emotion Recognition_supp1-3678321.pdf
authors: Yeon-kug Moon
abstract: The performance of multimodal emotion recognition (MER) systems critically depends on their ability to effectively integrate information from heterogeneous sources. However, existing fusion methods including attention-based mechanisms, auxiliary regression, and sampling-based approaches often lack efficient strategies for managing uncertainty in the presence of noisy or conflicting inputs across modalities. To address this limitation, we propose a novel framework grounded in an Uncertainty-Aware Fusion Mechanism. Our system employs a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture with specialized Transformer encoders to learn modality-specific representations. Within the fusion module, each modality expert predicts the parameters of a Beta distribution, yielding a bounded reliability score within the [0, 1] interval. In contrast to computationally intensive sampling techniques or generic Gaussian-based models, our method enables efficient, single-pass confidence estimation. These Beta-derived reliability scores dynamically modulate the contribution of each modality, attenuating the influence of less reliable sources. The resulting uncertainty-weighted representation is subsequently refined by a Dynamic Graph Convolutional Neural Network (DGCNN), which captures high-order, sample-specific relationships among modalities. We evaluate our approach on three diverse benchmarks: the DEAP dataset for physiological, the MELD and IEMOCAP datasets for conversational emotion recognition. Experimental results demonstrate significant improvements over state-of-the-art methods, highlighting the effectiveness of the Beta-distribution-based fusion strategy for robust emotion recognition.

title: Quizzing: Exploring the comprehensive benefits of quizzing in college classrooms
authors: Jean Mandernach

title: Feature Optimization Approach Arcface-Based Surveillance Face Recognition
authors: Manaschai Aonon, Phasit Charoenkwan, Phudinun Singkhamfu, Jirapat Ruathaitong
publish: 2026 Joint International Conference on Digital Arts, Media and Technology with ECTI Northern Section Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Computer and Telecommunication Engineering (ECTI DAMT &amp; NCON)
abstract: Security cameras are crucial for smart cities and identification, but face recognition faces challenges including varying illumination, pose variations, occlusions, and inconsistent resolution. This research proposes a feature optimization approach to enhance deep learning-based face recognition under challenging environments. Multiple optimization strategies including PCA, Grey Wolf Optimizer, Chi-squared, ANOVA, and Mutual Information were evaluated on ArcFace and FaceNet architectures. A two-stage verification system combining similarity thresholding with Rank-1 verification improves recognition accuracy. Experimental results on surveillance data from Chiang Mai, Thailand demonstrate that ArcFace with PCA retaining 95% variance achieved optimal F1 score of 0.714, substantially outperforming FaceNet with ANOVA_k100 (F1 <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=0.500)$</tex>, while reducing dimensionality from 512 to 180 dimensions. Surveillance conditions require substantially lower similarity thresholds <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(0.30-0.45)$</tex> than controlled environments due to cumulative image quality degradation. Comparative evaluation confirms ArcFace's superior base representation for surveillance applications. F1 score proves essential for evaluation due to severe class imbalance in surveillance scenarios.

title: Selection of Suitable Battery Technologies for Electric Vehicles Using Entropy Weight and EDAS Methods
authors: Muhamad Rasydan Mokhtar
publish: 2026 Joint International Conference on Digital Arts, Media and Technology with ECTI Northern Section Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Computer and Telecommunication Engineering (ECTI DAMT &amp; NCON)
abstract: The transportation sector can be considered one of the leading contributors to global warming. Conventional vehicles that use an internal combustion engine (ICE) will produce carbon emissions through the tailpipe. Conversely, a modern vehicle called an electric vehicle (EV) has no tailpipe emissions. The battery is one of the most important components of an EV system because it stores energy. As a consequence, the battery choice is a crucial item for long-term consideration. Hence, this study proposes the integration of two Multi Criteria Decision Making (MCDM) methods, namely entropy weight and Evaluation based on Distance from Average Solution (EDAS), to choose suitable battery technologies among four different types of batteries. All these batteries (graphene, lithium metal, solid state, and aluminium - air) will be assessed according to the nine criteria, which are cost-effectiveness, performability, safety, life, weightlessness, recyclability, maintainability, manufacturability, and fast charging. The result reveals that solid-state batteries are the best energy storage technology, as they have very high performability and safety for sustainable transportation. The findings of this study will help manufacturers and researchers ascertain the effectiveness of MCDM in achieving the best battery technologies for EVs in the future.

title: Signal Processing Techniques for Enhanced Performance of FMCW Radar Altimeter
authors: Reshma Sreekumar, Sreelal Sreedharan Pillai, Vani Devi Mani
publish: 2026 IEEE 18th International Conference on Advanced Trends in Radioelectronics, Telecommunications and Computer Engineering (TCSET)
abstract: In aerospace navigation, the Radar Altimeter plays an indispensable role in ensuring the navigation accuracy and safety of the aerial vehicle. In this work, we present signal processing techniques that can be implemented on an FMCW Radar Altimeter (RA) to offer better accuracy in altitude estimation. Accuracy and resolution play a crucial role in guaranteeing the safety of aerial vehicles, especially during the landing phase. Altitude in FMCW-RA is computed by estimating the frequency of the beat signal obtained by mixing the transmitted and received FMCW radar signal. A signal processing algorithm based on the Chirp Z transform (CZT) with interbin interpolation for improved accuracy of beat frequency estimation, along with a pre-processing stage to compensate for the performance degrading factors like random noise, is presented here. In this study, we validate the effectiveness of this algorithm using C-band RA and an altitude simulator ALT8000 and the performance is quantified in terms of Root Mean Square Error (RMSE).

title: Using ADDIE Model to Develop Digital Educational Game for Teaching Permutation Concept
authors: Natasha Dejdumrong, Phacharawat Eakgawatphokhin
publish: 2026 Joint International Conference on Digital Arts, Media and Technology with ECTI Northern Section Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Computer and Telecommunication Engineering (ECTI DAMT &amp; NCON)
abstract: This study presents the development and evaluation of a digital educational game designed to enhance students' understanding of permutation concepts in mathematics. The game was developed using the ADDIE instructional design model to ensure systematic alignment between learning objectives and gameplay mechanics. It incorporates intrinsically integrated game-based learning principles to promote logical reasoning and active problem solving. A pretest-posttest experimental design was employed to examine the effectiveness of the game on students' conceptual understanding, engagement, and problem-solving performance. The results indicate that the proposed game-based learning approach leads to improved learning outcomes compared to traditional instructional methods. These findings support the use of educational games developed under the ADDIE framework as an effective approach for teaching abstract mathematical concepts.

title: Applying the ADDIE Model to Develop a Phygital Pretend Play Learning Kit for Enhancing Wellbeing in Early Childhood Education Through Playful Learning
authors: Devanny Gumulya, Vanessa Elaine
publish: 2026 Joint International Conference on Digital Arts, Media and Technology with ECTI Northern Section Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Computer and Telecommunication Engineering (ECTI DAMT &amp; NCON)
abstract: Playful learning through pretend play can significantly enhance children's well-being in early childhood education (ECD) by fostering imagination, creativity, and joyful exploration. In recent years, the integration of physical and digital tools has gained traction to enhance children's pretend play experiences. This study explores the research and development of a phygital (physical + digital) pretend play learning kit with the theme of Pilot Occupation, using the ADDIE framework, a five-step instructional design model (Analyze, Design, Develop, Implement, Evaluate) commonly applied to create effective learning materials. Following a research-through-design approach, the study develops prototypes as outcomes of the ADDIE model while also identifying key indicators for an effective phygital learning kit. For the physical aspect, integrating diverse materials is crucial to stimulating children's imagination and fostering creative play. In the digital component, self-paced learning features should allow children to explore at their own time and pace, promoting autonomy and self-regulation. Additionally, personalization is essential to adapt the experience to individual preferences, needs, and learning styles. Incorporating gamification elements, such as challenges, rewards, and levels, enhances motivation and engagement. To ensure a seamless blend between physical and digital tools, a multi-sensory approach is necessary, where tangible materials provide tactile engagement while digital components offer auditory and visual stimuli, creating a holistic learning environment. This research contributes to the field well-being of ECD by providing insights into the design and development of phygital learning kits that support early childhood learning and establishing clear indicators for their successful implementation.

title: Application of Convolutional Neural Networks for Enhancing Gemstone Classification Performance
authors: Lukkana Poempool, Phornsin Pumpakar, Chiabwoot Ratanavilisagul, San Ratanasanya
publish: 2026 Joint International Conference on Digital Arts, Media and Technology with ECTI Northern Section Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Computer and Telecommunication Engineering (ECTI DAMT &amp; NCON)
abstract: Gemstones have different values and characteristics, such as color, shape, and texture. These differences make manual classification and counting difficult and time-consuming, especially when gemstones have similar appearances. In practice, gemstone classification is still mainly performed by humans, which can lead to errors caused by fatigue or repeated counting. This study applies image processing and deep learning techniques to improve the accuracy and efficiency of gemstone classification and counting under controlled imaging conditions. The OpenCV library is used to adjust brightness, reduce noise, and detect object edges, while a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) is used to learn gemstone features such as color, brightness, and texture. Moreover, the HSV color model is applied to reduce the effect of lighting and shadows that can affect classification accuracy. Experimental results show that the proposed method provides better classification and counting performance than the compared methods, achieving an accuracy of 99.70%. The results indicate that the proposed system can effectively reduce human-related errors and processing time, and it demonstrates potential applicability as a laboratory-scale, proof-of-concept solution for automated gemstone inspection.

title: Reliability in Industry-Academia Assessment: A Framework for Validating Competencies in IT Education
authors: Prasara Jakkaew, Pruet Putjorn, Nacha Chondamrongkul
publish: 2026 Joint International Conference on Digital Arts, Media and Technology with ECTI Northern Section Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Computer and Telecommunication Engineering (ECTI DAMT &amp; NCON)
abstract: High-stakes competency assessments involving mixed-expert panels face inherent reliability challenges. This study investigates an assessment paradox in a hackathon-style evaluation where a psychometrically sound rubric yielded unreliable outcomes. A post hoc analysis of a national IT talent program in Thailand revealed that while the assessment instrument demonstrated excellent internal consistency (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\alpha=0.912$</tex>), interrater reliability for absolute agreement was poor (ICC <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=$</tex> 0.276). The discrepancy was diagnosed as systemic Scale Disagreement, where experts applied divergent severity standards. To address this, a six-step protocol using Zscore standardization was applied to normalize scores. The rectified results were perceived as fair by the learners. The findings suggest that while pre-assessment rater calibration is a necessary preventive measure, a post hoc statistical rectification protocol serves as a critical safety net to ensure the validity and fairness of certification.

title: Classifying Burmese (Myanmar) Medical Reviews Using Synthetic Data Approach
authors: Hein Minn Thu, Pyae Bhone Moe, Nyein Chan Ko Ko, Aye Hninn Khine
publish: 2026 Joint International Conference on Digital Arts, Media and Technology with ECTI Northern Section Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Computer and Telecommunication Engineering (ECTI DAMT &amp; NCON)
abstract: Burmese (also known as the Myanmar language) remains a low-resource language for Natural Language Processing, particularly in the healthcare domain, where labeled corpora are limited due to privacy and ethical concerns. This study investigates the feasibility of using synthetic data for medical review sentiment classification in Burmese. We construct synthetic medical review corpora by translating English medical review datasets into Burmese using both large language models and machine translation models. The resulting datasets are evaluated on downstream sentiment classification tasks using Logistic Regression and Support Vector Machine models with both syllable-level and word-level features. Experimental results show that synthetic reviews translated by Google Translate achieved the highest F1-score of 0.71 on the Medication Review Dataset, while Gemini-2.5-pro yielded an F1-score of 0.45 on the Medical Condition Dataset. These findings demonstrate that, despite occasional translation errors and hallucinations, synthetic data can serve as a foundation resource for Burmese healthcare NLP. This work provides the first benchmark for Burmese medical review classification and highlights the importance of combining synthetic data generation with expert validation for reliable low-resource digital health application.

title: An Explainable Dynamic Few-Shot Learning Framework for GERD and Polyp Detection in Endoscopic Imaging
authors: Md. Hasan Imam Bijoy, Dewan Aminul Islam, Susmoy Biswas, Mohammad Shamsul Arefin, Md Zahid Hasan, Mohammad Shorif Uddin
publish: 2025 International Conference on Machine Learning and Applications (ICMLA)
abstract: Diagnosing gastrointestinal (GI) disease from endoscopic images remains a challenging task due to high intra-class variability, inter-class similarity, and limited annotated data. To address these issues, this study proposes a novel Dynamic Few-Shot Learning (DFSL) framework that integrates the representational strength of convolutional backbones with a dynamic metric-based classifier to perform accurate classification under low-data regimes. We benchmark our approach using DenseNet-201, ResNet-50, and ViT-B/16 across multiple few-shot settings (1 to 5 shots, up to 4-way classification). Among them, DFSL with ResNet-50 consistently outperforms state-of-the-art backbones, achieving a peak accuracy of 97.6% in the 4-shot scenario. Our experiments reveal that ResNet-50's residual connections enable more robust spatial encoding and generalization across variations in the number of shots. To further enhance interpretability and clinical relevance, Grad-CAM and TCAV were employed to visualize the decision rationale and validate the model’s focus on disease-specific regions. A comparative analysis with supplementary confusion matrices reveals that DFSL-ResNet-50 achieves superior class-wise performance, with reduced misclassification of visually similar diseases. While ViT-B/16 and DenseNet-201 exhibit fluctuating accuracy under scarce data, our model maintains consistent performance. The proposed DFSL framework, with its potential for clinical deployment, presents a significant advancement in developing scalable, interpretable, and accurate diagnostic systems, instilling optimism for its real-world application.

title: Blockchain Enabled Secure Data Sharing for Distributed Healthcare Systems
authors: G. Bhanu Prasad, M. Venkateswara Rao
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
abstract: The increasing adoption of data-driven healthcare analytics is hindered by strict privacy regulations, data heterogeneity and the inability of institutions to centrally share sensitive multimodal patient data. Current methods of distributed learning cannot adequately support a high degree of privacy and auditability and high predictive accuracy at the same time, which restricts their application in a real healthcare environment. This study was to develop and test a blockchain-based, privacy-aware multimodal learning infrastructure that allows sharing data safety and jointly training models in distributed health care organizations. The experiments were carried out on multimodal healthcare datasets of publicly available healthcare information, among which are structured electronic health records, medical imaging, and physiological time-series data, and are simulated across healthcare nodes. The proposed framework integrates federated learning with permissioned blockchain governance, differential privacy, trusted execution environments, zero-knowledge consent verification, and a multimodal Perceiver-based architecture. The performance, privacy and system-level metrics were measured and compared to the centralized and hybrid federated baselines. The most predictive performance (AUROC <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=0.925)$</tex> was centralized baseline, but with high privacy leakage with the accuracy of membership inference equal to 0.78. The suggested framework had performed nearly centrally (AUROC <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=0.913$</tex>, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{PR}-\text{AUC}=0.76$</tex>) but the accuracy of membership inferences had dropped to 0.19. This was accomplished at a moderate system overhead and took 1450 GPU-hours, and a mean round latency of 12s. The results demonstrate that secure, auditable, and privacy-preserving multimodal learning in distributed healthcare environments is feasible without substantial loss of clinical utility. The proposed structure offers a feasible basis of reliable cross-institutional medical analytics in an actual administrative setting.

title: Predictive Modeling of Air Pollution using Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Dr.G.Revathy, Dr.S.Gokulraj, Dr.N.Seethalakshmi, Dr.R.Sudha
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Smart Electronics and Communication (ICOSEC)
