abstract: Shale liquid permeability measurement equipment is a core measurement device in shale gas development, and its operating status directly determines the accuracy of permeability data and the rationality of development plans. When the equipment operates under complex operating conditions, it generates a large amount of time-series operating data with spatiotemporal coupling characteristics, and there are strong nonlinear relationships between the data, which increases the difficulty of fault diagnosis. To this end, a fault detection method based on the Convolutional Neural Network - Long Short-Term Memory (CNN-LSTM) hybrid algorithm is proposed for SMP-200 shale permeability meters (GRI-95/0496) and GB/T34533-2017-compliant equipment. Firstly, 5 typical faults (e.g., sensor drift) are defined per industrial standards, and key parameters like injection pressure are selected as diagnostic indicators; then, key parameters such as injection pressure, instantaneous flow rate, and pressure difference at both ends of the core are selected as fault diagnosis indicators, and the variation laws of characteristic parameters under various faults are calculated; finally, the preprocessed time-series characteristic parameters are used as input to construct a CNN-LSTM hybrid fault detection model. The CNN layer extracts the spatial correlation characteristics of the data, the LSTM layer captures the temporal dependence, and the cross-entropy loss function is introduced to optimize the model parameters, realizing accurate fault detection of the equipment. Experimental results show that the algorithm has an average recognition accuracy of 98.7% for five typical faults (e.g., pressure sensor drift), outperforming LSTM-CNN, LightGBM, and other models. The recognition rate of early minor faults is improved by 15.3 percentage points compared with a single algorithm, and the maximum diagnosis delay is ≤0.8 seconds, confirming accurate and reliable fault detection.

title: Performance Analysis of Boost Converter Under Nonlinear Scenarios
authors: Vineet Tomar, Dheeraj Joshi
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
abstract: This paper presents a closed-loop PI-controlled boost converter analyzed under two distinct nonlinear scenarios: saturation & dead zone & its constraints. It incorporates duty cycle limits as saturation, while the dead zone captures regions where small control signals produce no response. Simulation shows that despite the different nonlinearities, the PI controller ensures fast voltage tracking, zero steady-state error & robust converter operation, providing insight into realistic challenges in power electronic control. Matlab is used for simulation purposes.

title: Corrigendum: A Comprehensive Benchmark on Spectral GNNs: The Impact on Efficiency, Memory, and Effectiveness: [Experiments & Analysis]
authors: Ningyi Liao, Haoyu Liu, Zulun Zhu, Siqiang Luo, Laks V.S. Lakshmanan
publish: Proceedings of the ACM on Management of Data
abstract: None

title: CDNet: Consistency-Driven Semi-Supervised Object Detection for Remote Sensing
authors: Yuhan Sun, Shengyang Li, Jian Yang, Han Wang, Yuxuan Liu, Zhuang Zhou, Qi Wen, Jianing You, Kailun Zhang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Remote sensing images exhibit arbitrary orientations, uneven sample distributions, and large scale variations. Existing semi-supervised detectors mainly handle rotation yet neglect how distribution and scale impair pseudo-label quality and positive–negative sample assignment. These limitations manifest in three ways: (1) Inconsistent features. Classification and regression features often mismatch, leading pseudo-labels selected based on classification scores to poorly align with ground truths. (2) Inconsistent sample distributions. A fixed selection threshold yields unstable pseudo-labels due to uneven object distributions, injecting noise into training. (3) Inconsistent discriminative regions. Objects with extreme aspect ratios often have key features at the periphery, causing them to be mislabeled as background. To tackle these issues, we propose CDNet, a novel Consistency-Driven semi-supervised object detection network for remote sensing with four designs: (i) A task-feature alignment module align classification and regression features, allowing classification scores to better reflect pseudo-label quality. (ii) A GMM-based adaptive thresholding strategy that adjusts pseudo-label selection criteria across training stages to cope with uneven distributions. (iii) A sample assignment consistency constraint method and (iv) a scale consistency loss to regularize high-aspect-ratio and scale-varied objects. Extensive experiments on DOTAv1.5 and DIOR-R demonstrate CDNet's superiority over state-of-the-art methods.

title: All-Optical Microwave Frequency Division by Harmonic Frequency Locking
authors: Mohammad AlMulla
abstract: A microwave frequency divider based on an intensity-modulated, optically injected semiconductor laser is demonstrated. The division mechanism arises from the interaction between the externally applied modulation frequency and the period-one (P1) oscillation frequency induced by optical injection, allowing for fractional division of the input microwave signal. The achievable frequency division factors are determined by the numerators of the harmonic frequency-locked ratios, which follow a Farey-sequence relationship between the modulation frequency and the locked P1 oscillation frequency. This enables controlled generation of microwave outputs at rational subharmonics of the input frequency. Experimental results demonstrate division factors of 2, 3, 4, 5, and 7 by tuning the optical injection conditions. The divided microwave signals retain the spectral purity and stability of the injected modulation while providing access to lower, application-relevant frequencies. The proposed approach offers a simple, compact, and fully optical method for microwave frequency division and synthesis, eliminating the need for high-frequency electronic dividers or complex optoelectronic feedback loops.

title: Gradient Embedding and Modal Complementarity Networks for Audio-Video Depression Level Prediction
authors: Mingyue Niu, Zhongping Zhang, Chang Gao, Yan Zeng, Ning Fan, Shiqing Zhang
abstract: Physiological studies show distinct audio-video behaviors correlate with depression levels, leading some researchers to use Transformer architecture to predict depression levels. The positional embedding in Transformer assigns a positional marker to each element, but the multi-head attention mechanism operates at the token level and is difficult to examine the temporal changes of elements. This brings limitations in capturing dynamic differences reflecting depression. Besides, previous works adopt element-wise operation, fully connected layers or attention mechanisms for modal complementarity. However, they don't specify what the supplemental representation is, and don't explore to what extent the supplemental representation is accepted. This hinders the model to characterize depression cues. Therefore, we propose the Gradient Embedding (GE) and the Modal Complementarity (MC) modules. The GE module embeds the gradient into sequences to perceive the dynamics of elements, thereby helping to capture depression-related temporal patterns. Besides, we stipulate the supplementary representation as the difference between two modal representations. In this way, the MC module quantifies to what extent the supplemental representation is accepted by a weighted combination of unimodal representation and supplemental representation, thereby achieving effective modality complementarity. Experimental results (RMSE = 6.42/6.23, MSE = 5.06/5.00) on AVEC 2013 and AVEC 2014 datasets illustrate the progressiveness of our method.

title: OFERA: Blendshape-driven 3D Gaussian Control for Occluded Facial Expression to Realistic Avatars in VR
authors: Seokhwan Yang, Boram Yoon, Seoyoung Kang, Hail Song, Woontack Woo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: We propose OFERA, a novel framework for real-time expression control of photorealistic Gaussian head avatars for VR headset users. Existing approaches attempt to recover occluded facial expressions using additional sensors or internal cameras, but sensor-based methods increase device weight and discomfort, while camera-based methods raise privacy concerns and suffer from limited access to raw data. To overcome these limitations, we leverage the blendshape signals provided by commercial VR headsets as expression inputs. Our framework consists of three key components: (1) Blendshape Distribution Alignment (BDA), which applies linear regression to align the headset-provided blendshape distribution to a canonical input space; (2) an Expression Parameter Mapper (EPM) that maps the aligned blendshape signals into an expression parameter space for controlling Gaussian head avatars; and (3) a Mapper-integrated Avatar (MiA) that incorporates EPM into the avatar learning process to ensure distributional consistency. Furthermore, OFERA establishes an end-to-end pipeline that senses and maps expressions, updates Gaussian avatars, and renders them in real-time within VR environments. We show that EPM outperforms existing mapping methods on quantitative metrics, and we demonstrate through a user study that the full OFERA framework enhances expression fidelity while preserving avatar realism. By enabling real-time and photorealistic avatar expression control, OFERA significantly improves telepresence in VR communication. A project page is available at https://ysshwan147.github.io/projects/ofera/.

title: FlashANNS: GPU-Driven Asynchronous I/O Pipelining for Eliminating Storage-Compute Bottlenecks in Billion-Scale Similarity Search
authors: Yang Xiao, Mo Sun, Ziyu Song, Bing Tian, Jie Sun, Jie Zhang, Zeke Wang, Zonghui Wang, Wenzhi Chen, Fei Wu
publish: Proceedings of the ACM on Management of Data
abstract: None

title: A Pose-only Geometric Constraint for Multi-Camera Pose Adjustment
authors: Shunkun Liang, Banglei Guan, Bin Li, Qifeng Yu, Yang Shang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Multi-camera systems offer rich observation capabilities for visual navigation and 3D scene reconstruction; however, the resulting feature redundancy often compromises computational efficiency. This challenge is particularly pronounced during bundle adjustment, where the non-linear optimization of both system poses and scene points incurs substantial computational overhead. To address this challenge, this paper introduces a pose-only geometric constraint for multi-camera systems and proposes a corresponding pose adjustment algorithm. Specifically, we use generalized camera model to establish a unified representation of the multi-camera system. Building upon this model, we formulate the multi-camera pose-only constraint, which implicitly represents a 3D scene point using two base observations and their associated poses, thereby achieving a pose-only representation of the projection geometry. Subsequently, we introduce a multi-camera pose adjustment algorithm that eliminates 3D points from the parameter space, thereby achieving efficient and focused pose optimization. Experimental results on both synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that the proposed algorithm outperforms baseline bundle adjustment methods in computational efficiency, while maintaining or even improving pose estimation accuracy

title: Counteracting the Active Area Reduction Caused by Explicit Guard Rings in High-Voltage CMOS SPADs
authors: Ivan Berdalović, Borna Požar, Paula Bartulović, Patrik Sočković, Tomislav Suligoj
abstract: Novel structures of circular single-photon avalanche diodes (SPADs) with different active junction radii (9 and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\text{4}~{\mu }$</tex-math> </inline-formula>m) have been fabricated in a commercial 180-nm high-voltage (HV) CMOS process. The devices were connected to an active quenching and recharge circuit (AQRC) fabricated in the same technology, capable of operating at excess voltages up to 20 V, and characterized in terms of dark count rate (DCR), afterpulsing probability (AP), and position-dependent photon detection probability (PDP). The device with the larger radius exhibits state-of-the-art noise performance with a DCR per unit area below 0.2 Hz/<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$ {\mu }$</tex-math> </inline-formula>m<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^{\text {2}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> at <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\textit {V}_{\textbf {EX}}=\text {5}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> V, but the device with the smaller radius shows a further improvement in DCR by more than a factor of 10, which clearly does not scale with area. Technology computer-aided design (TCAD) simulations have been performed to explain the differences in breakdown voltage and DCR between the two structures, and the record-low DCR of the smaller device was linked to a significant decrease in effective active area, caused by the electric field decrease due to charge sharing between the explicit guard ring (GR) and the multiplication region, as confirmed by position-dependent PDP measurements over the entire device. Nevertheless, it is demonstrated that the active area can be increased by applying a higher excess voltage while achieving a central PDP above 20% at a wavelength of 635 nm, making the device an attractive candidate for ultralow-noise applications where a high fill factor is not a critical requirement.

title: Seems like a Touch? Comparing Emotional Experience and Illusion of Touch of Visual and Haptic Presentations of Mediated Social Touch in VR
authors: Julius Tietenberg, Timo Fürtges, Maic Masuch
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Social virtual reality (VR) applications enable users to experience highly immersive mediated interactions and social experiences. Especially embodied nonverbal communication, like mediated social touch, is mostly unique to social VR and potentially a major factor in enabling fulfilling emotional experiences at a distance. In our within-subjects study (N = 28), we compared feedback from wearable vibrotactile haptic devices with two established visual presentation forms of social touch: clipping and pseudo-haptics. Our findings suggest that the addition of haptic feedback can elicit significantly higher emotional arousal and result in a stronger illusion of touch than visual presentations alone. In contrast to existing literature, there was no evidence of a difference between the immersive and emotional experience of clipping and pseudo-haptics in our sample. In addition, we also investigated whether simply wearing haptic feedback devices during a social touch interaction, i.e., without active haptic feedback, affects the illusion of touch and user experience. However, there was no evidence of a significant effect in the sample studied. A free copy of this paper, including the appendix, is available at https://doi.org/10.17605/OSF.IO/4FYKQ.

title: A Mixed-Methods Comparative Study of Remote and Traditional SDR Laboratories Via Bloom's Taxonomy, Surveys, and Focus Group
authors: Rania Hussein, Zhiyun Zhang, Marcos Inoñan, Pablo Orduña
abstract: Hands-on experimentation with Software-Defined Radios (SDRs) is critical for teaching radio frequency (RF) and wireless communication concepts in Electrical and Computer Engineering (ECE) and Computer Science (CS) curricula. While traditional radios require costly hardware and have fixed configurations, SDRs offer a flexible, software-programmable alternative. Remote SDR laboratories further enhance accessibility by enabling students to conduct experiments anytime, anywhere—reducing institutional costs and offering flexibility. While SDR remote laboratories offer significant benefits, current research lacks systematic comparisons of their effectiveness in developing cognitive skills versus traditional labs and short-term skill persistence. This study evaluates the RHL-RELIA (Remote Engineering Lab for Inclusive Access), a novel SDR remote laboratory integrating popular hardware and software to support remote experimentation. A controlled split-group experiment was conducted with 36 students (primarily 4th-year undergraduates and graduate students with minimal communication theory background) in a capstone course at a public university in the United States. Lab 0 introduced foundational SDR skills, while Lab 1 compared learning outcomes between students using RHL-RELIA and traditional SDR lab kits. In Lab 2, all students used RHL-RELIA to assess short-term persistence. Student reports were evaluated using a Bloom's taxonomy rubric spanning the Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze, and Evaluate levels. Results revealed that the RHL-RELIA group outperformed the in-person cohort in Lab 1 across four cognitive domains (Remember, Understand, Analyze, and Evaluate). Longitudinal analysis of Lab 2 showed sustained gains: students who initially used RHL-RELIA retained higher performance in Remember, Understand, Apply, and Evaluate compared to the control group, suggesting long-term cognitive benefits. Pre- and post-lab online surveys were administered to collect structured student feedback, complemented by a focus group study to gather qualitative insights into their learning experiences. Thematic analysis revealed positive perceptions of RHL-RELIA, with students emphasizing its usability and flexibility. These findings suggest that RHL-RELIA has advantages over traditional in-person SDR labs in fostering both immediate learning outcomes and short-term knowledge retention.

title: Occlusion-Free Conformal Lensing for Spatiotemporal Visualization in 3D Urban Analytics
authors: Roberta Mota, Julio D. Silva, Fabio Miranda, Usman Alim, Ehud Sharlin, Nivan Ferreira
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: The visualization of temporal data on urban buildings, such as shadows, noise, and solar potential, plays a critical role in the analysis of dynamic urban phenomena. However, in dense and geographically constrained 3D urban environments, visual representations of time-varying building data often suffer from occlusion and visual clutter. To address these two challenges, we introduce an immersive lens visualization that integrates i) a view-dependent cutaway de-occlusion technique and ii) a temporal display derived from a conformal mapping algorithm. The mapping process first partitions irregular building footprints into smaller, sufficiently regular subregions that serve as structural primitives. These subregions are then seamlessly recombined to form a conformal, layered layout for our temporal lens visualization. The view-responsive cutaway is inspired by traditional architectural illustrations, preserving the overall layout of the building and its surroundings to maintain users' sense of spatial orientation. This lens design enables the occlusion-free embedding of shape-adaptive temporal displays across building facades on demand, supporting rapid time-space association for the discovery, access and interpretation of spatiotemporal urban patterns. Guided by domain and design goals, we outline the rationale behind the lens visual and interaction design choices, such as the encoding of time progression and temporal values in the conforming lens image. A controlled user study compares our approach against conventional juxtaposition and X-ray spatiotemporal designs. Results validate the usage and utility of our lens, showing that it improves task accuracy and completion time, reduces navigation effort, and increases user confidence. From these findings, we distill design recommendations and promising directions for future research on spatially-embedded lenses in 3D visualization, urban analytics, and related domains.

title: Dual-Frequency Category-Guided Recalibration Network for Remote Sensing Image Semantic Segmentation
authors: Mohan Liu, Shuli Cheng, Anyu Du
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: In remote sensing images, confusion between adjacent categories commonly occurs due to similar local textures across different land-cover types, where category boundaries often exhibit gradual transitions rather than clear separations. Furthermore, intra-class variability induced by complex environmental further weakens inter-class discriminability, making it difficult for conventional models to sufficiently distinguish adjacent categories.To order to tackle this challenge, this paper proposes the Dual-Frequency Category-Guided Recalibration Network (DFCGRNet), leveraging spatial and frequency domain modeling.The Dual-Frequency Interaction (DFI) module separates high- and low-frequency components via a customized wavelet transform, enhancing boundary details while preserving global structural coherence.Within the Frequency-Category Collaborative Enhancement (FCCE) module, the Wavelet Frequency Enhancement (WFE) unit adaptively strengthens boundary cues between adjacent categories through discrete wavelet modulation.The Category-Guided Semantic Recalibration (CGSR) module computes category centroid vectors for each category and constructs an inter-class cosine similarity matrix promote adjacent category distinction.Consequently, the DFCGRNet is empowered to enhance discriminative capacity between adjacent categories. Empirical evaluations show DFCGRNet achieves mIoU scores of 85.30%, 87.67%, and 55.11% on the Vaihingen, Potsdam, and LoveDA datasets, surpassing a range of prevailing approaches in segmentation accuracy. The complete implementation is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/liumh12/DFCGRNet</uri>.

title: Interaction Under Whole-Body User Rotations in VR Space
authors: Filip Škola, Fotis Liarokapis
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Virtual reality (VR) enables interactions beyond real-world limitations, yet many such capabilities remain underexplored due to concerns about user comfort and increased disorientation and cybersickness. This study examined how altering the user's virtual pitch orientation influences interaction performance and subjective ratings. In a within-subject design, 30 seated participants experienced 12 virtual tilts from moderate to extreme (±180°). We assessed comfort, simulator sickness, perceptual responses, and performance. Our results revealed no significant increases in nausea, disorientation, or decreased comfort, with mild tilts performing equivalently to baseline. Notably, even extreme tilts maintained low nausea levels. Performance effects were mixed: forward tilts yielded similar or slightly improved performance, whereas backward tilts modestly impaired performance, though not significantly. These findings suggest that VR experiences featuring virtual body orientations distinct from the user's physical posture are feasible without compromising comfort or performance, enabling novel opportunities in simulation, interface design, visualization, creative content creation, and VR gaming.

title: Asymptotic Analysis of Max-Min SINR in Downlink MISO System with Multi-Weighted Power Constraints
authors: Shasha Liu, Abla Kammoun, Hayssam Dahrouj, Mohamed-Slim Alouini
abstract: This work addresses the fundamental problem related to the characterization of the parameters of optimal precoders at the asymptotic regime, a topic of increasing interest given the upsurge in massively connected networks and their emerging applications. The paper, particularly, focuses on the downlink of a single-cell multi-user system, where a base station (BS) equipped with M antennas communicates with K single-antenna users. We then propose a low-complexity design for the optimal linear precoder (OLP) that maximizes the minimum signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR) while ensuring multi-weighted power constraints. To achieve this, we consider the asymptotic regime where M and K grow large while maintaining a fixed ratio. Utilizing tools from random matrix theory (RMT), we derive explicit deterministic approximations for the parameters of the OLP and the allocated powers for users. One of the paper highlights is that the approximations provide deeper insight into the structure of the optimal precoders, predict the active weighted power constraints, and significantly reduce the implementation complexity in large-scale networks. Another paper highlight is how to analyze best scenarios where the power weights for some users are exactly zero, a case not addressed in [1]. We revisit the original OLP, presenting an implementation that accommodates zero-power weights and propose its equivalent low-complexity asymptotic design. Numerical results are used to validate the asymptotic analysis is accurate in the finite system regime and investigate the impact of weighted power constraints on the system energy efficiency (EE).

title: ITS-ShipFormer: An Informative Token Selection Former for SAR Ship Recognition_supp1-3661083.pdf
authors: Wei Pu
abstract: Ship automatic target recognition (ATR) in synthetic aperture radar (SAR) images plays a crucial role in maritime domain awareness. However, the sea clutter interference may make the model fail to focus on informative ship target regions for recognition. All existing SAR ship ATR models use the entire SAR ship image as input. But not all regions in SAR ship images contribute positively to recognition. However, the intraclass diversity and interclass similarity make the SAR ship ATR task more challenging. In this study, we propose a novel transformer-based architecture that addresses the two core challenges, named informative token selection former (ITS-ShipFormer). ITS-ShipFormer selects and guides the model’s attention to the informative token of the ship target regions. The ITS-ShipFormer consists of the multihead dynamic local convolution (MHDLC) block in the early stages, transformer blocks equipped with a sea clutter suppression module (SCSM) in the latter stages, and a discriminative hybrid loss. SCSM automatically distinguishes the informative ship tokens and useless sea clutter tokens by two carefully designed strategies. In response to the other challenge, MHDLC is designed to enhance the feature extraction ability, and the hybrid discriminative loss is proposed to add constraints on the CLS token and the informative tokens simultaneously. The experimental results on benchmark datasets OpenSARShip and FUSAR-Ship jointly verify the effectiveness of our design. Different from previous works introducing the transformer structure in ITS-ShipFormer utilizes the overlooked innate and unique advantages of the transformer structure to address our challenge.

title: AvatarEdit: Optical Flow-Guided Consistent Talking Head Avatar Editing_supp1-3680003.pdf
authors: Haijie Yang
abstract: Pre-trained conditional diffusion models have shown impressive potential in image editing. While these methods can produce satisfactory editing results, they often suffer from inconsistencies in appearance, expression, and temporal coherence, especially in facial images. These issues arise from the independent editing of individual images and the inherent loss of temporal continuity during the editing process. In this paper, we introduce AvatarEdit, a novel framework designed for generating and editing consistent virtual avatars. Unlike traditional image editing methods, AvatarEdit leverages the intuitive and efficient optical f low technique to establish correspondences across different views and frames, thereby enhancing editing consistency. Specifically, given a set of avatar images rendered by a pre-trained 3D Gaussian Splatting model, we propose a multi-view consistency module that selects reference views based on optical flow variations. These selected frames are then used to provide complementary view consistency for the target image, ensuring a unified edited appearance. Additionally, we introduce a temporal consistency module that incorporates optical flow and depth changes from source images into the diffusion process, refining the final edited virtual avatar images to improve temporal stability. Extensive experiments demonstrate that AvatarEdit outperforms state-of the-art methods in terms of appearance, expression, and temporal consistency in the edited avatars.

title: Joint Optimization of Computation Offloading and Resource Allocation in Heterogeneous UAV-Assisted Edge Computing: A Game-Theoretical Approach_supp1-3680337.pdf
authors: Guangming Cui
abstract: In UAV-assisted edge computing systems, existing research inadequately addresses multi-resource heterogeneity, encompassing computational resource differences and energy consumption coefficient variations among edge servers. This heterogeneity causes critical load imbalance: energy-efficient servers attract excessive users despite limited capacity, while high-capacity servers with higher energy costs remain underutilized, degrading performance for latency-sensitive applications. To address this challenge, this paper formulates the joint offloading and resource allocation as an NP-hard optimization minimizing user costs, reducing resource competition, and maximizing server utility. We propose SOTNRA (Server Offloading Target Node selection and Resource Allocation), a hierarchical framework based on a two-layer game structure combining potential and Stackelberg games. The first stage employs a potential game-based server selection algorithm (PGSSA) considering load balancing constraints with proven convergence. The second stage uses a momentum-based gradient ascent algorithm (MBGA) for optimal pricing and offloading ratios. Extensive experiments demonstrate superior performance in reducing user costs while maintaining load balancing compared to single-dimensional heterogeneity approaches.

title: Power Loss Modeling of SiC/Si Hybrid Switch Considering Commutation
authors: Yongsheng Fu, Hai-Peng Ren
abstract: The hybrid switches constituting parallel connected SiC-MOSFET and Si-IGBT (SiC/Si HyS) devices have been widely studied in recent years due to the high performance-to-cost ratio. There exist some successful applications for SiC/Si HyS although the comprehensive loss model of the HyS has not been fully investigated. This is an obstacle to reliability design and Electro Magnetic Interference (EMI) analysis. The comprehensive loss modeling is a challenge since the complicated commutation between SiC-MOSFET and Si-IGBT due to parasitic parameters, together with flexible multiple triggering patterns of the HyS. In this paper, the mathematical model of the current dynamic commutation process of the HyS for the commonly used triggering patterns considering the parasitic inductance and intrinsic capacitance is analyzed comprehensively for the first time, in order to accurately describe the current dynamic behavior of the SiC/Si HyS. Based on this, the power loss models of SiC/Si HyS including the commonly considered switching loss, conduction loss, etc, and the special loss of the HyS, such as commutation loss with respect to different triggering patterns, are formulated. It improves the accuracy of power losses prediction for both the SiC/Si HyS and its internal SiC-MOSFET and Si-IGBT, The parasite circuit parameters active in commutation process are identified for the first time to show the complexity of the inherent commutation process and their influence on power loss. Spice simulation model is compared to show the uniqueness and indispensability of the modelling process. A 2kW SiC/Si HyS Buck converter test bench is also built to evaluate the power loss model experimentally, the results verify the correctness and accuracy of the analytical loss model. The work not only has theoretical significance but is also very important for the thermal management of the converter with high power levels in extreme environments and temperature balance control of the independent power device.

title: Respiration Rate Estimation Using ECG and PPG Data: Open Source Implementations Focused on Wearables_supp1-3679610.pdf
authors: Chryssalenia Koumpouzi
abstract: Respiratory rate (RR) is an indicator of various psychological and pathological conditions and needs to be monitored easily and reliably. Emerging trends and recent advancements indicate that wearables are the future of continuous noninvasive vital measurement for healthcare. Numerous recent studies have focused on estimating RR from the electrocardiogram (ECG) or photoplethysmogram (PPG), which can be acquired non-invasively with minimal hardware and without obstructing spontaneous breathing patterns. While easy and flexible in acquisition, such signals may suffer from poor signal quality, which in turn results in unreliable estimation of RR, an issue that can be addressed by evaluating the quality of the obtained estimates. In this work, we propose a novel method for RR estimation based on the cross-correlation function that demonstrates improved estimation properties compared to most existing open-source methods achieving 0-bias and limits of agreement (LOAs) from -5.37 to 5.44 bpm. We propose the use of variance as an estimate quality metric and demonstrate how this approach results in higher accuracy. Two types of fusion are designed and evaluated, stressing the importance of quality evaluation and estimate combination in improving accuracy, reaching LOAs between -1.44 and 0.9 bpm. Finally, we stress the importance of signal duration in estimation accuracy, and demonstrate how longer segments result in lower error, validating this finding on multiple open-source methods. Our results are evaluated on three publicly available datasets, including CapnoBase, a benchmark database on respiration.

title: PRISM: Navigating Cost–Accuracy Trade-offs for NL2SQL
authors: Gaurav Tarlok Kakkar, Yeounoh Chung, Fatma Özcan, Steve Mussmann, Joy Arulraj
publish: Proceedings of the ACM on Management of Data
abstract: None

title: Deep Learning-based Segmentation for Assessment of Kidney Tumour Ablation Therapy in CT Images
authors: Maryam Rastegarpoor, Derek W. Cool, Aaron Fenster
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Kidney tumor ablation is a minimally invasive treatment for Renal Cell Carcinoma (RCC). Manual segmentation of the kidney ablation zone (KAZ) is time-consuming, skill-dependent, and variable, making accurate assessment of treatment efficacy challenging. We propose a deep learning-based workflow for KAZ segmentation in CT images by using residual connections, multi-scale fusion, and a proposed channel-aware block. It involved predicting on 2D slices sampled radially, followed by reconstructing and evaluating the ablated volume within the kidney. Using the segmented KAZ produced by our Channel-Aware-ResUNet++ (CAResUNet++) model, we identified the margin within the kidney, which is critical for assessing ablation success. The deep learning model was trained and evaluated on a local dataset from the academic health network (London, Canada), including annotated KAZ from 76 patients' CT images. Quantitative analysis demonstrated that the proposed pipeline achieved promising performance metrics, including 85±08% DSC, 5.21±2.94mm Hausdorff distance, and 1.82±1.01mm Mean Absolute boundary Distance (MAD) for the whole KAZ. Analysis of the predicted margin within the kidney resulted in a mean MAD and Mean Signed boundary Distance of 1.38mm and 0.52mm, respectively, indicating its robustness, reliability, and applicability in clinical settings

title: Modelling Different Penetration Rates of Automated Eco-Driving Electric Vehicles in an Urban Area
authors: Jakub Krol, Bani Anvari, Craig Rafter, James Fleming, Xingda Yan, Roberto Lot
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Eco-driving strategies have proven to be effective in providing energy savings for the vehicle that is utilising them. This paper explores the underinvestigated impact of eco-driving vehicles on other network participants adhering to conventional driving styles. An eco-driving strategy designed for an electric vehicle that trades off energy savings with naturalistic driving without relying on vehicle-to-infrastructure or vehicle-to-vehicle communication is extended using Gaussian Process Regression for real-time predictive speed optimization. It enables the assessment of its network effects in two scenarios: 1) a platoon and 2) an urban network simulation with mixed-mode traffic and varying demand levels. Initial validation involves an eco-driving vehicle responding to a platoon leader, influencing following vehicles governed by the Intelligent Driver Model (IDM). Further analysis introduces eco-vehicles into an IDM-governed network under different traffic conditions. Energy savings of up to 21% were achieved in cars following a vehicle that prioritises energy savings and, of up to 13% if they followed a vehicle that attempts to balance energy savings and conventional driving style. In the urban scenario, positive effects on other users are observed in high density traffic even if only a small number of eco-vehicles are present in the network. The largest energy savings achieved in conventional vehicles were 5.1% and were obtained for high-density traffic and a network consisting of 25% of eco-vehicles.

title: Generation Expansion Planning Framework Integrating Battery Energy Storage Systems into the Screening Curve Method
authors: Matheus T. De Sousa, Eugênio L. F. Fortaleza, Yan T. De Oliveira, José Oniram De A. Limaverde Filho, Matheus B. S. De Castro, William H. C. Sánchez, Thiago F. De Souza
abstract: Generation expansion planning is crucial to ensure a long-term, least-cost electricity supply. The screening curve method is a widely used tool for this purpose, valued for its straightforward approach. At the same time, battery energy storage systems have become increasingly important for peak shaving, supporting the integration of renewable energy, and reducing dependence on thermal units. Despite these advantages, current formulations of the screening curve method do not account for the role of energy storage systems, limiting their utility as a decision-support tool for modern low-carbon systems. This study proposes a new approach that incorporates battery operation into the screening curve framework for identifying the least-cost generation mix. The method employs a root-finding peak shaving strategy and utilizes a genetic algorithm for battery optimal sizing, while maintaining analytical simplicity. Two case studies for the Brazilian system, with and without renewable intermittent sources, are used to evaluate the approach, with total cost as the performance criterion. Results indicate that battery integration consistently reduces total system costs by up to 0.68% annually, primarily driven by an average 20% reduction in thermal start-up events. Furthermore, mathematical validation against mixed-integer linear programming benchmarks demonstrates that the proposed heuristic achieves an optimality gap within 0.37%. These findings validate the proposed approach as an effective decision-support framework, offering policymakers and system operators a transparent means to assess the economic synergy between storage and generation.

title: Multi-Modal 3D Semantic Segmentation with Capsule-Weighted Knowledge Distillation
authors: Hafsa Benallal, Nadine Abdallah Saab, Jamal Riffi, Hamid Tairi
abstract: Accurate 3D semantic segmentation of outdoor environments is a fundamental requirement for autonomous systems, yet it remains challenging owing to the heterogeneous nature of sensor data. LiDAR sensors provide precise geometric information but lack semantic richness, whereas RGB cameras capture detailed appearance cues but suffer from unreliable depth estimation. Existing multi-modal fusion approaches typically apply uniform fusion strategies across all spatial regions, implicitly assuming equal feature reliability, which limits their effectiveness in complex scenes. We address this limitation by proposing a confidence-aware fusion strategy that uses capsule network activations to weight the knowledge transfer between 2D and 3D representations. Our key observation is that capsule vector magnitudes naturally encode feature presence confidence, which we leverage to guide adaptive fusion without requiring additional networks or supervision. The framework processes RGB images through a capsule-based encoder and LiDAR point clouds through a sparse point-voxel encoder. A four-stage fusion module then combines these modalities: first extracting capsule confidence scores, then weighting 2D features accordingly, projecting them to 3D space, and finally performing confidence-guided knowledge distillation from the 3D domain to enhance 2D feature learning. Confidence-aware fusion consistently outperforms uniform baselines across all evaluated categories. Experiments on SemanticKITTI confirm this hypothesis, achieving 66.8% mIoU on the test set and 70.6% on the validation set, with a 2.0% improvement over baselines while maintaining computational efficiency with only 21.4M parameters. These results validate confidence-aware fusion as a simple yet effective approach for multi-modal 3D semantic segmentation in autonomous driving applications.

title: HTSR-Pollen: Handwritten Text Synthesis and Recognition System to Overcome Data Scarcity
authors: Arthur F. S. Neto, Byron L. D. Bezerra, Alejandro H. Toselli
abstract: Offline Handwritten Text Recognition (HTR) systems recognize and transcribe handwritten text from scanned images into digital formats. The field has become important due to the need for document digitization and data entry automation in various industries. Accurate recognition requires large and varied datasets for training optical models, where collecting and labeling these datasets is often time-consuming and impractical. To address this challenge, data augmentation and transfer learning are commonly used. Nevertheless, these traditional methods may lead to overfitting and performance degradation when data are scarce. This work proposes integrating Conditional Generative Adversarial Networks (CGANs) for data synthesis into optical model training to improve handwriting recognition in data-scarce scenarios. To validate our proposal, we conducted a study that included: (i) an exploration to establish an optimal configuration for traditional data augmentation; and (ii) extensive experiments using seven datasets. In addition, these datasets were partitioned into training subsets to simulate diverse data-scarcity conditions. Averaged over all subsets and optical models, data synthesis achieved the highest reductions compared to the baseline trained from scratch without augmentation. It reduced the Character Error Rate (CER) by 41.1% and the Word Error Rate (WER) by 28.1%. Transfer learning achieved reductions of 34.4% in CER and 23.5% in WER. Lastly, traditional data augmentation achieved reductions of 13.8% in CER and 12.3% in WER. These findings highlight the importance of data synthesis for improving HTR systems, particularly in data-scarcity contexts.

title: Exploring Zero-Shot Text Simplification for Bengali Science Text using Large Language Models
authors: Piyal Roy, Rajat Pandit, Sudip Kumar Naskar
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
abstract: The increasing presence of complex scientific concepts and terminologies in Bengali school textbooks presents significant challenges for students, especially those from low-resource learning environments. In this paper, we explore automatic text simplification as a natural progression toward making these texts more accessible. We investigate the potential of Large Language Models (LLMs) to simplify scientific definitions in Bengali without requiring additional training data, adopting a zero-shot evaluation framework. Four advanced LLMs (GPT-5, Google Gemini, mT5_multilingual_XLSum, and mBART-large-50) are employed in this work. The study benchmarks their simplification performance across multiple criteria, including lexical reduction, syntactic simplification, semantic adequacy, and preservation of scientific meaning. Experimental results demonstrate that while transformer-based multilingual models like mT5 and mBART exhibit consistent structural simplification, GPT-5 and Gemini offer higher fluency and semantic fidelity. This work highlights the feasibility of using zero-shot LLMs for text simplification in low-resource educational settings and provides a comparative framework to guide future research in Bengali text simplification.

title: Predicting and Identifying Low Ground Water Level Areas: A Machine Learning Approach
authors: G. Anuradha, Mrudhula Amarthaluri, Ch. Hoksha Santhosh Kumar, K. Manju Harini
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
abstract: Groundwater depletion is an escalating concern, especially in regions where water scarcity threatens agriculture, drinking water supply, and long-term sustainability. Traditional monitoring techniques often fall short in providing timely and location-specific insights for groundwater management. This study proposes a machine learning-based framework to predict and identify low groundwater level areas in Krishna (erstwhile) district of Andhra Pradesh, using historical bi-seasonal (May and November) groundwater level data from 2019 to 2023. Three regression models—Support Vector Regression (SVR), Random Forest (RF), and Gradient Boosting (GB)—were trained on normalized data and evaluated using Mean Absolute Error (MAE), Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), and R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>. Among these, the Gradient Boosting model achieved the best performance, with an RMSE of 2.34, MAE of 1.20, and R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> of 0.99, indicating high predictive accuracy. Predicted 2024 values were compared against historical averages to detect potential depletion zones. The results were spatially visualized in QGIS using village-level geolocation data. This approach offers a scalable and data-driven tool for proactive groundwater assessment, enabling more informed decision-making for water conservation in vulnerable regions.

title: Imbalance-Aware 3D Anomalous Body Semantic Segmentation: A SMOTE-Guided Conditional Generative Data Augmentation Approach
authors: Chenkai Nie, Shenglin Li
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Neural Networks, Information and Communication Engineering (NNICE)
abstract: To mitigate the low detection rates of minority-class anomalies caused by severe class imbalance in conventional geological anomaly detection, we propose a joint data-augmentation framework that integrates SMOTE oversampling with a Conditional Generative Adversarial Network (CGAN). The framework targets minority-sample scarcity in two stages. First, SMOTE expands the minority training set by synthesizing new samples through local-neighborhood interpolation among minority instances. Second, CGAN learns the class-conditional distribution of minority data and generates additional samples that remain consistent with the original seismic feature space, thereby improving both the diversity and fidelity of minority examples. Experiments are conducted on real 3D seismic data from a Dutch survey area, with geological anomalies delineated by domain experts. Model training and evaluation follow a sliding-window strategy to extract 3D patches from sub-regions. Performance is assessed primarily using minority-class IoU2 and Recall2 on the test set. Relative to Weighted Cross-Entropy, the proposed method improves IoU2 by approximately 54.6%; relative to Focal Loss, IoU2 increases by approximately 56.3%, while also achieving superior performance on precision-related metrics. Ablation studies further confirm the contribution of each component: under identical parameter settings, SMOTE-only and CGAN-only variants yield IoU2 values of 0.3468 and 0.3735, respectively, both markedly below the combined approach. These results demonstrate that SMOTE and CGAN are complementary rather than substitutive, and their integration provides an effective strategy for mitigating sample imbalance in anomaly-detection tasks.

title: Quantifying Grid Resilience Under Wind Events: A Multi-Utility Analysis of Outage Risk
authors: Wenqi Zhang, Reiko Matsuda-Dunn, Vincent Westfallen, Abodh Poudyal
abstract: Understanding how wind events affect electric grid infrastructure is critical for developing risk-informed resilience strategies. This paper presents a quantitative assessment of wind-driven outages across multiple anonymized utilities operating in diverse climatic and topographic regions of the United States. Using multi-year outage records integrated with bias-corrected wind data, the study develops metrics that capture how distribution system performance responds to wind intensity, duration, and spatial exposure. Outage frequency, affected customers, and restoration duration are aggregated to derive resilience indicators such as cumulative impact curves and wind-outage response thresholds. Comparative analyses highlight how local exposure conditions influence outage behavior across regions. The results provide actionable insights into the sensitivity of electric distribution networks to wind stress and demonstrate a scalable, data-driven framework for quantifying outage risk. The approach supports the development of consistent resilience metrics that can inform future utility planning and risk-informed investment decisions.

title: LLMs as Agentic Cooperative Players in Multiplayer UNO
authors: Yago Romano Martinez, Jesse Roberts
publish: 2025 International Conference on Machine Learning and Applications (ICMLA)
abstract: LLMs promise to assist humans, not just by answering questions, but by taking direct, agentic action to support a desired outcome. However, this has not been investigated in strategic settings with the LLM acting as a non-player character (NPC) in support of the player’s goal. We ask, can a large language model based agent help another accomplish their strategic goal as an active participant? We test this question by engaging an LLM in UNO, a turn-based card game, asking it not to win but instead help another player to do so. We develop a tool that allows decoder-only LLMs to participate as agents within the RLCard game environment. We evaluate models ranging from small (1B parameters) to large (70B parameters) and explore how model scale impacts performance. We find that while all models were able to successfully outperform a random baseline when playing UNO, few were able to significantly aid another player.

title: Personalized Alzheimer's Disease Treatment Planning Through Predictive Modeling and Explainable Artificial Intelligence
authors: Saurabh Pratap Singh Rathore, Mohd. Farman Ali, Amarprit Singh, Ajitesh Kumar, Aman Joshi, Deep Biswas
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
abstract: This study presents a predictive modeling framework designed to support personalized treatment planning for Alzheimer's disease (AD) through the use of machine learning and natural language processing (NLP). Alzheimer's disease is a steadily worsening neurodegenerative condition that demands individualized therapeutic strategies for effective care. The proposed system combines a fine-tuned OPT-350M language model with an XGBoost classifier to determine dementia severity, achieving a high classification accuracy of 97.36%. The framework incorporates important clinical and genetic indicators such as Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE) scores, Clinical Dementia Rating (CDR), APOE4 genotype status, and polygenic risk scores (PRS) to produce tailored treatment recommendations. To improve model performance and ensure interpretability, the approach employs advanced methods including polynomial feature generation, hyperparameter tuning, and SHAP-based explainability analysis. In addition, the system offers both medication-based and lifestyle-focused intervention suggestions, enabling transparent, data-driven clinical decision support. Overall, this framework shows strong promise in assisting healthcare providers with delivering patient-specific care, enhancing clinical outcomes, and improving the quality of life for individuals living with Alzheimer's disease.

title: NORA: A Nephrology-Oriented Representation Learning Approach Towards Chronic Kidney Disease Classification
authors: Mohammad Abdul Hafeez Khan, Twisha Bhattacharyya, Omar Khan, Noorah Khan, Alina Aziz Fatima Khan, Mohammed Qutub Khan, Sujoy Ghosh Hajra
publish: 2025 International Conference on Machine Learning and Applications (ICMLA)
abstract: Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) affects millions of people worldwide, yet its early detection remains challenging, especially in outpatient settings where laboratory-based renal biomarkers are often unavailable. In this work, we investigate the predictive potential of routinely collected non-renal clinical variables for CKD classification, including sociodemographic factors, comorbid conditions, and urinalysis findings. We introduce the Nephrology-Oriented Representation leArning (NORA) approach, which combines supervised contrastive learning with a nonlinear Random Forest classifier. NORA first derives discriminative patient representations from tabular EHR data, which are then used for downstream CKD classification. We evaluated NORA on a clinic-based EHR dataset from Riverside Nephrology Physicians. Our results demonstrated that NORA improves class separability and overall classification performance, particularly enhancing the F1-score for early-stage CKD. Additionally, we assessed the generalizability of NORA on the UCI CKD dataset, demonstrating its effectiveness for CKD risk stratification across distinct patient cohorts.

title: A Φ-OTDR Vibration Localization Method based on Differential Adaptive SG-Spectral Subtraction Algorithm
authors: Jinghua Luo, Bin Li, Fan Zhang
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Neural Networks, Information and Communication Engineering (NNICE)
abstract: To address the challenge of environmental and system noise degrading the signals of phase-sensitive optical time-domain reflectometry (Φ-OTDR), this paper proposes a vibration localization method based on differential adaptive Savitzky-Golay (SG) spectral subtraction to effectively suppress random noise in non-vibrating regions. The approach first enhances the dynamic features of vibration events through temporal differencing. It then employs an adaptive long- and short-window SG filtering strategy that dynamically selects the filter window size according to the temporal energy difference at different spatial positions. Finally, secondary noise reduction is performed in the frequency domain using spectral subtraction. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method successfully preserves vibration peak characteristics, strongly suppresses background noise, significantly improves vibration localization performance, and achieves a localization signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of 39.67 dB.

title: A Dual-Branch Dynamic Fusion Framework for Unified Multi-Domain Topic Modeling
authors: Xinyue Zhang, Lin Zhang
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Neural Networks, Information and Communication Engineering (NNICE)
abstract: Cross-domain topic modeling is challenging due to expression heterogeneity, short-text sparsity, and inconsistent semantic structures across domains. This paper proposes COT-TopicNet, a dual-branch topic modeling framework for unified multi-domain text modeling. The framework integrates a discrete TSCTM branch, which enhances topic discriminability via contrastive learning and discrete bottlenecks, with a continuous FASTopic branch that performs semantic alignment using Optimal Transport and Sinkhorn distance. A dynamic fusion module is further introduced to adaptively combine discrete statistical signals and continuous semantic embeddings through cross-attention and quantization gating.Experiments on three heterogeneous datasets, including long-form news articles, short social media posts, and extremely short conversational texts, demonstrate that COT-TopicNet consistently outperforms representative baseline methods across different paradigms. The proposed model achieves significant improvements in topic coherence on short texts while maintaining high topic diversity on long texts. Qualitative analysis and ablation studies further verify the effectiveness of the dual-branch architecture and the dynamic fusion mechanism.Overall, COT-TopicNet provides a robust and effective solution for unified topic modeling in heterogeneous multi-domain scenarios.

title: Distribution Phasor Measurement Unit with Edge Computing for Enhanced Distribution Grid Monitoring and Power Quality Applications
authors: Brijesh P, Arya G Lal, Anoop P, Ajeesh A, Manju A S, Aby Joseph
abstract: Distribution systems were historically lagged behind transmission system on its observability, measurement and monitoring technologies. The economics of monitoring system is much welcoming in transmission system due to its relatively less nodes for monitoring, high value infrastructure and quantity of power transfer. The measurements required for a distribution system needs to be more precise and faster when compared with that of a transmission line. Per node infrastructure investment in distribution system will be very less when compared to transmission system. Distribution infrastructure is generally the major contributor of power system outages. Also, penetration of large number of renewable energy sources made the distribution power flow into a bi-directional from its legacy unidirectional and predictable nature. The urge for green transportation system introduced an unpredictable and variable energy demand in addition to the 'masked' loads created by RES. The Distribution Phasor Measurement Unit (D-PMU) is a promising technology for distribution grid monitoring. This paper presents a comprehensive framework for a D-PMU integrated with edge computing capabilities, specifically designed for power quality monitoring and analysis applications.

title: Comprehensive Evaluation of CNN Architectures on CIFAR-10 Dataset
authors: Gagandeep Sethi, Amar Singh
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Smart Electronics and Communication (ICOSEC)
abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive evaluation of six Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architectures—three custom-designed models with varying depths and three widely used pre-trained models (ResNet18, VGG16, and MobileNetV2)—on the CIFAR-10 image classification dataset. The study analyzes each model’s performance using classification accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, training/test loss, parameter count, and training time. All models were trained under consistent experimental conditions using the PyTorch framework. Among the evaluated models, ResNet18 achieved the highest accuracy of 89.16%, followed by the 7-layer custom CNN and MobileNetV2. The analysis reveals that deeper custom CNNs improve performance significantly, while pre-trained models offer superior accuracy when properly fine-tuned. In contrast, VGG16 failed to converge due to architectural mismatch with the low-resolution CIFAR-10 images. The study further highlights trade-offs between model complexity and performance, providing guidance for selecting CNN architectures based on application constraints and computational resources.

title: Automated Tulsi Leaf Disease Classification Using a Fine-Tuned ResNet50-Based Deep Convolutional Network
authors: Goldy Verma, Srinivas Aluvala, Neha Sharma, Sonali Gupta, Vartika Aarwal
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
abstract: Holy Basil (Ocimum sanctum), also referred to as Tulsi, is a medicinal plant that is commonly used in traditional healthcare systems. Bacterial and fungal infections and pests cause diseases to plants which impact on plant health and quality of medicines. The present paper proposes automated classification of the Holy Basil leaf disease with the help of the deep convolutional network based on ResNet50 with fine-tuning. A Kaggle image dataset of bacterial, fungus, pest-infested, and healthy leaf samples of plants is used in open-source. The image preprocessing and data augmentation are used to enhance the generalization of the model. Fine-tuning of higher layers of the ResNet50 architecture using transfer learning identifies disease-specific pattern of visuals. The experiments prove that the overall classification accuracy is 98%, being higher than the baseline deep learning models. This is confirmed by the overall assessment with regard to accuracy, recall, F1-score, and confusion maturity to support the usefulness and dependability of the suggested method to diagnose mistakes in medicinal plants.

title: Cross-Modal Retrieval and Retrieval-Augmented Inference for IoT-Enabled Clinical Decision Support
authors: Tejeswar Reddy Velpucharla, Subhash Bhagavan Kommina
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
abstract: Early clinical decisions increasingly rely on heterogeneous IoT streams (wearables, bedside waveforms, point-of-care imaging), yet current multimodal systems remain vulnerable to retrieval errors and unsupported LLM assertions. This paper proposes a topology-aware cross-modal retrieval and retrieval-augmented inference pipeline that fuses image and time-series embeddings via a domain-adapted contrastive dual encoder, regularizes embedding neighbourhood geometry with persistent homology, and conditions a frozen LLM through a BLIP-2-style Q-former for evidence-grounded generation. Evaluation on MIMIC-CXR and ROCO-style radiology corpora demonstrates substantial gains: Recall@1 <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\cong 0.78$</tex> (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx+9$</tex> percentage points vs. a BiomedCLIP baseline) and mAP @ 10 <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx$</tex> 0.72, together with an absolute +0.15 increase in evidencecoverage (0.81 vs. 0.66), corresponding to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx 23 \%$</tex> relative reduction in unsupported LLM assertions. Key contributions are: (i) a persistent-homology embedding regularizer for modality-invariant geometric alignment; (ii) an IoT-aware hybrid retrieval index and pruning strategy for bounded latency; and (iii) comprehensive empirical validation including ablations and clinician-adjudicated concordance analyses on MIMIC-derived datasets.

title: Neuro-Symbolic Agents for Explainable Autonomous Reasoning in Complex Environments
authors: K.Thilagavathi, Sajja Suneel, R. Divya, G.Kavitha, Gopinath D, R.Naveenkumar
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Smart Electronics and Communication (ICOSEC)
abstract: The Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus (ARC) challenges conventional systems by eliminating explicit rules and requiring human-like conclusions. This study presents a novel hybrid neuro-symbolic architecture in which deep neural perception is combined with logic-based symbolic reasoning through a defined interface. This model utilizes a Convolutional Neural Network to convert input grids into feature embeddings and a logic-based inference system using symbolic predicates from Prolog. Unlike conventional, black-box models, system makes all decisions clear, maintaining traceability while adhering to human interpretability. This study evaluated the framework with experimental tests on ARC dataset and received positive results, with a very high F1 score of 0.916 and classification accuracy of 0.93, producing useful symbolic explanations. The system also surpassed performance on previously existing neuro-symbolic baselines. This framework demonstrates how neuro-symbolic forms of intelligence can combined to solve high-dimensional forms of reasoning while retaining interpretability, making it ideal for safety-critical, and cognitively-led AI applications.

title: ClimateInformer: An AI-Driven Educational Tool for Scenario-Based Climate Change Prediction and Awareness
authors: Vedant Shah, Ronit Kuila, Rushil Sengupta
abstract: Climate change presents an urgent global challenge, yet widespread misinformation and a lack of customized educational tools significantly impede public understanding and proactive engagement. Existing climate models often lack the real-time adaptability and user-specific scenario exploration. This paper introduces ClimateInformer, a web-based educational tool that integrates user-defined scenarios with a Large Language Model (LLM)-driven carbon emission estimator. The tool enables users to explore hypothetical climate scenarios by inputting custom text, which is then interpreted by an LLM to predict carbon emission changes based on the user input. This is then fed into a tuned machine learning regression model, which predicts future temperature shifts with approximately 91% accuracy compared to real data. The final temperature is fed back into LLMs to generate specific mitigation strategies. Unlike static carbon footprint calculators or preset simulation models, ClimateInformer offers personalized, interactive insights. The model demonstrates consistent predictive accuracy, and initial human trials across 20 different participants have shown a 10.87% increase in climate literacy among participants. ClimateInformer is a simple educational tool developed for middle and high school students to explore and reproduce. To the best of our knowledge, it is the first climate literacy tool that allows users to interactively explore scenarios and learn specific mitigation strategies.

title: Analysing the Impact of Paralleling Battery Banks with Different SOH on Performance and Degradation of Banks
authors: Shreesakshi Gachhi, Rakhee Kallimani, Sushant Mutagekar, Tushar Birje
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
abstract: Lithium-ion batteries have become the cornerstone of modern energy storage systems, widely used in electric vehicles (EVs), renewable energy integration, and consumer electronics. In large-scale applications, multiple cells are often connected in parallel to enhance capacity and reliability. However, when these cells have different State of Health (SoH), they exhibit dissimilar electrochemical behavior, leading to uneven current distribution, thermal instability, and accelerated degradation. This review explores the critical impact of SoH variation among parallel-connected lithium-ion cells on the overall performance and life-cycle of the battery bank. A comprehensive analysis of literature spanning the past decade is presented, highlighting key findings from both experimental and simulation-based studies. The review identifies major challenges such as current mismatch, capacity fade, and thermal runaway risks arising from SoH heterogeneity. Furthermore, existing balancing techniques, modeling strategies, and limitations in current Battery Management Systems (BMS) are examined. Finally, the review outlines future directions for intelligent cell balancing, predictive degradation modeling, and hardware-in-the-loop testing. The insights provided form a foundational understanding for researchers and engineers aiming to design safer and more reliable parallel battery configurations.

title: Gated Cross-Modal Prototype Stabilization for Text-Driven Domain Generalization
authors: Yuwen Pan, Zhijie Wang, Jinbao Wei, Zhi Li
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Neural Networks, Information and Communication Engineering (NNICE)
abstract: Domain generalized semantic segmentation aims to achieve robust dense prediction under unseen domain shifts without access to target-domain data. Recent text-driven segmentation frameworks leverage pretrained vision-language models to provide class-level semantic priors, demonstrating improved generalization. However, most existing approaches treat textual representations as static anchors or rely on limited cross-attention mechanisms, which restrict effective text-pixel interaction and may lead to semantic drift when textual prototypes are adaptively updated by visual context. In this paper, we propose a gated cross-modal prototype stabilization framework that explicitly balances visual adaptation and semantic stability. To enhance cross-modal interaction, we introduce a lightweight gated cross-modal adapter, which injects text-conditioned responses into pixel features through a gated residual modulation, allowing adaptive and reliable fusion of textual semantics and visual representations. Moreover, as textual prototypes are progressively refined under visual guidance, we impose a von Mises–Fisher constraint on the textual prototypes to stabilize their semantic directions on the hypersphere, effectively preventing domain-induced semantic drift without restricting intermediate adaptation. Extensive experiments on standard domain generalized segmentation benchmarks demonstrate that our method consistently improves robustness under severe domain shifts.

title: An Intelligent Energy Efficient Dynamic Load Balancing Algorithm Using Machine Learning for Cloud Computing Environment
authors: Annwesha Banerjee Majumder, Sushma Mallick, Tanusree Saha, Rupashri Barik, Trishita Ghosh, Chowdhury Md Mizan
publish: 2025 International Conference on Sustainable Technology in Energy and Power Systems (STEPCon)
abstract: The process of distributing workloads among computing resources in a cloud computing environment and methodically balancing the network traffic that accesses those resources is referred to as cloud load balancing. Load balancing is essential in cloud computing to provide high availability, performance, and scalability of applications by allocating traffic among several servers or resources. In this paper an intelligent dynamic load balancing algorithm has been proposed for cloud environment. The proposed intelligent dynamic load balancing algorithm optimizes server selection by evaluating energy consumption, CPU capacity, and predicted response time. Using Linear Regression, it predicts response time based on CPU Utilization, Memory usage, Storage usage, Network Bandwidth, Service Latency of multi cloud service composition dataset which achieved 132.294501 MAE. Unlike traditional methods, it dynamically evaluates server suitability using real-time metrics such as load, energy use, and urgency. This methodology can help in proper server utilization by equivalently distributing loads in servers.

title: Enhanced Power Quality in Cuk-Derived Bridgeless PFC Rectifier Using T2-FLC
authors: Deepan Raj R, Dinesh Kannan S, Balamurugan R, Radhamani R
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Smart Electronics and Communication (ICOSEC)
abstract: This paper investigates a Cuk-derived bridgeless power factor correction (PFC) converter for single-phase 220 V AC input supply, modeled and evaluated using MATLAB/Simulink. By doing away with the requirement for a front-end diode bridge, the bridgeless construction reduces conduction losses and boosts efficiency. The converter functions in discontinuous conduction mode (DCM), which intrinsically facilitates input current shaping while minimizing sensor needs. An Integral Type-2 Fuzzy Logic Controller (IT2FLC) is utilized to control the output DC voltage while improving dynamic response across a broad range of load and source conditions. In comparison to traditional PI control, the suggested controller exhibits greater resilience, providing faster transient recovery and less steady-state error.According to simulation results, the input current minimizes total harmonic distortion (THD) and achieves near-unity power factor by closely following the source voltage waveform. The measured THD values are well within the limits prescribed by IEC 61000-3-2 standards, ensuring compliance for practical deployment. The overall findings highlight that the combination of Cuk-derived bridgeless topology and intelligent fuzzy control provides a reliable and efficient solution for modern AC–DC conversion applications such as electric vehicle chargers and distributed renewable energy systems.

title: Smart City Streetlight System using K-Means Clustering and Decision Tree for Dynamic Energy Management
authors: Akabarsaheb Babulal Nadaf, K. Srinivasa Rao, K. Padmaja, T. Hussain, Ankur Walia, Manisha Amol Bhendale
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Smart Electronics and Communication (ICOSEC)
abstract: Urban Street lighting expends much energy, as conventional systems are not adaptable to varying environmental and traffic circumstances. The proposed study presents a hybrid smart streetlight system that incorporates K-Means clustering for urban zone categorization and a Decision Tree model for adaptive light regulation. The technology enhances energy efficiency by combining regions according to pedestrian and traffic patterns and utilizing decision trees for real-time management, all while maintaining safety standards. A comparative examination demonstrates enhanced performance compared to rule-based, Naïve Bayes, and Random Forest models, attaining an accuracy of 97.4%, an F1-score of 96.8%, and an AUC-ROC of 98.1%. The approach's scalability and minimal latency facilitate practical implementation in smart cities, providing an intelligent solution for sustainable urban energy management.

title: Hybrid energy harvesting study by Solar Cages in natural and artificial conditions
authors: M. Ashraf Khan, Allie R. Welch, Aiden W. Harris, Brian Maricle, Matthew M. Vannette
abstract: Solar energy is one type of renewable energy harvested globally as a clean source: a source without carbon emissions. However, to make harvesting sustainable, a balance of energy harvest and food production is warranted since solar plants can reside on including cultivable land. This study focuses on numerical results regarding sustainable energy harvesting. Miniature solar cages have been modeled and built for the study. As the dynamic nature of weather posed a challenge in studying the impact on the availability of illumination inside the cage, tests were performed under artificial conditions. This study attempts to relate the data from natural conditions and artificial conditions. Therefore, a controlled environment, using artificial lighting, was adopted. Owing to the minimal presence of background or indirect light, a significant reduction of illumination, 55%, has been observed for a low level of light surrounding the cage. This reduction can be translated to sustainable energy harvesting and allows for the determination of the minimum energy needed for plant growth. The study under artificial conditions sets the extreme limit of maximum energy blocked by the cage structure, specifically in simultaneous recording of data of illumination in terms of voltages.

title: Perception-based multiplicative noise removal with Diffusion models
authors: An Vuong, Thinh Nguyen
publish: 2025 International Conference on Machine Learning and Applications (ICMLA)
abstract: We present a novel approach to perform multiplicative noise removal, utilizing the recent developments of diffusion models. We show that multiplicative noise, which commonly appears in images produced by synthetic aperture radar (SAR), laser, or optical lenses, can be well-modeled by a Geometric Brownian process in the logarithmic domain. This process admits a time-reversal stochastic differential equation (SDE), which is utilized to perform noise removal. We conduct extensive experiments to compare our approach with classical methods as well as state-of-the-art Deep Learning-based approaches. Our models significantly outperform others in terms of perception-based metrics such as LPIPS and FID, while remaining competitive in traditional pixel-based metrics like PSNR and SSIM.

title: From Uncertainty to Clarity: Projecting Points onto Classification Boundaries Using Distance Metrics
authors: Michael R. Smith, Eva Domschot
publish: 2025 International Conference on Machine Learning and Applications (ICMLA)
abstract: A significant challenge for machine learning (ML) is determining the reliability of model predictions, particularly in high-stakes applications where erroneous outputs can have dire consequences. In this paper, we propose a simple modification to distance-based out-of-distribution (OOD) detection techniques that projects data points onto the classification boundary of a neural network. Our approach improves OOD detection accuracy for most OOD datasets using a ResNet architecture and a vision transformer. We evaluate our method against state-of-the-art techniques using benchmark datasets within the OpenOOD framework, demonstrating superior performance in identifying OOD data. This work underscores the importance of understanding uncertainty in ML models and presents a promising avenue for improving model reliability in practical applications.

title: BEACON: Behavioral Malware Classification with Large Language Model Embeddings and Deep Learning
authors: Wadduwage Shanika Perera, Haodi Jiang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Machine Learning and Applications (ICMLA)
abstract: Malware is becoming increasingly complex and widespread, making it essential to develop more effective and timely detection methods. Traditional static analysis often fails to defend against modern threats that employ code obfuscation, polymorphism, and other evasion techniques. In contrast, behavioral malware detection, which monitors runtime activities, provides a more reliable and context-aware solution. In this work, we propose BEACON, a novel deep learning framework that leverages large language models (LLMs) to generate dense, contextual embeddings from raw sandbox-generated behavior reports. These embeddings capture semantic and structural patterns of each sample and are processed by a one-dimensional convolutional neural network (1D CNN) for multi-class malware classification.
